title: Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct
text: members, contributors, leaders pledge make participation community harassment-free experience everyone, regardless age, body size, visible invisible disability, ethnicity, sex characteristics, gender identity expression, level experience, education, socio-economic status, nationality, personal appearance, race, caste, color, religion, sexual identity orientation. pledge act interact ways contribute open, welcoming, diverse, inclusive, healthy community.

title: Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct
text: Examples behavior contributes positive environment community include: Demonstrating empathy kindness toward people respectful differing opinions, viewpoints, experiences Giving gracefully accepting constructive feedback Accepting responsibility apologizing affected mistakes, learning experience Focusing best just us individuals, overall community Examples unacceptable behavior include: use sexualized language imagery, sexual attention advances kind Trolling, insulting derogatory comments, personal political attacks Public private harassment Publishing others’ private information, physical email address, without explicit permission conduct reasonably considered inappropriate professional setting

title: Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct
text: Community leaders responsible clarifying enforcing standards acceptable behavior take appropriate fair corrective action response behavior deem inappropriate, threatening, offensive, harmful. Community leaders right responsibility remove, edit, reject comments, commits, code, wiki edits, issues, contributions aligned Code Conduct, communicate reasons moderation decisions appropriate.

title: Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct
text: Code Conduct applies within community spaces, also applies individual officially representing community public spaces. Examples representing community include using official e-mail address, posting via official social media account, acting appointed representative online offline event.

title: Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct
text: Instances abusive, harassing, otherwise unacceptable behavior may reported community leaders responsible enforcement : Paul H. McClelland PaulHMcClelland@protonmail.com complaints reviewed investigated promptly fairly. community leaders obligated respect privacy security reporter incident.

title: Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct
text: Community leaders follow Community Impact Guidelines determining consequences action deem violation Code Conduct:

title: Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct
text: Community Impact: Use inappropriate language behavior deemed unprofessional unwelcome community. Consequence: private, written warning community leaders, providing clarity around nature violation explanation behavior inappropriate. public apology may requested.

title: Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct
text: Community Impact: violation single incident series actions. Consequence: warning consequences continued behavior. interaction people involved, including unsolicited interaction enforcing Code Conduct, specified period time. includes avoiding interactions community spaces well external channels like social media. Violating terms may lead temporary permanent ban.

title: Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct
text: Community Impact: serious violation community standards, including sustained inappropriate behavior. Consequence: temporary ban sort interaction public communication community specified period time. public private interaction people involved, including unsolicited interaction enforcing Code Conduct, allowed period. Violating terms may lead permanent ban.

title: Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct
text: Community Impact: Demonstrating pattern violation community standards, including sustained inappropriate behavior, harassment individual, aggression toward disparagement classes individuals. Consequence: permanent ban sort public interaction within community.

title: Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct
text: Code Conduct adapted Contributor Covenant, version 2.1, available https://www.contributor-covenant.org/version/2/1/code_of_conduct.html. Community Impact Guidelines inspired Mozilla’s code conduct enforcement ladder. answers common questions code conduct, see FAQ https://www.contributor-covenant.org/faq. Translations available https://www.contributor-covenant.org/translations.

title: Advanced Workflows
text: examples vignette use clintrial dataset included summata: clintrial dataset includes 10 study sites, providing natural clustering variable hierarchical analysis.
code: library(summata) library(survival) library(ggplot2)  data(clintrial) data(clintrial_labels)  # Examine the clustering structure table(clintrial$site) ##>  ##>   Site Alpha    Site Beta   Site Gamma   Site Delta Site Epsilon    Site Zeta     Site Eta   Site Theta    Site Iota   Site Kappa  ##>           76           80           72           89           86           93           89           75          102           88

title: Advanced Workflows
text: Interaction analysis tests whether association predictor outcome varies across levels another variable. Interactions specified using colon notation interactions parameter; main effects automatically included alongside interaction term.

title: Advanced Workflows
text: Specify interactions using colon notation interactions parameter:
code: example1 <- fit(   data = clintrial,   outcome = "surgery",   predictors = c("age", "sex", "treatment", "stage"),   interactions = c("sex:treatment"),   model_type = "glm",   labels = clintrial_labels )  example1 ##>  ##> Multivariable Logistic Model ##> Formula: surgery ~ age + sex + treatment + stage + sex:treatment ##> n = 847, Events = 368 ##>  ##>                                  Variable   Group      n Events     aOR (95% CI) p-value ##>                                    <char>  <char> <char> <char>           <char>  <char> ##>  1:                           Age (years)       -    847    368 0.96 (0.94-0.97) < 0.001 ##>  2:                                   Sex  Female    449    203        reference       - ##>  3:                                          Male    398    165 1.01 (0.55-1.87)   0.968 ##>  4:                       Treatment Group Control    194     93        reference       - ##>  5:                                        Drug A    292    173 2.09 (1.20-3.62)   0.009 ##>  6:                                        Drug B    361    102 0.50 (0.29-0.87)   0.013 ##>  7:                         Disease Stage       I    211    125        reference       - ##>  8:                                            II    263    133 0.69 (0.47-1.03)   0.069 ##>  9:                                           III    241     91 0.41 (0.27-0.62) < 0.001 ##> 10:                                            IV    132     19 0.09 (0.05-0.16) < 0.001 ##> 11: Sex (Male) × Treatment Group (Drug A)       -    128     73 0.77 (0.34-1.71)   0.515 ##> 12: Sex (Male) × Treatment Group (Drug B)       -    175     49 0.79 (0.36-1.73)   0.554

title: Advanced Workflows
text: Test multiple effect modifiers simultaneously:

   847    606 1.04 (1.03-1.04) < 0.001 ##>  2:                                            Sex  Female    450    298        reference       - ##>  3:                                                   Male    400    311 1.32 (0.95-1.83)   0.094 ##>  4:                                Treatment Group Control    196    151        reference       - ##>  5:                                                 Drug A    292    184 0.70 (0.43-1.14)   0.153 ##>  6:                                                 Drug B    362    274 0.67 (0.41-1.09)   0.106 ##>  7:                                  Disease Stage       I    211    127        reference       - ##>  8:                                                     II    263    172 1.11 (0.73-1.70)   0.624 ##>  9:                                                    III    241    186 1.94 (1.21-3.09)   0.006 ##> 10:                                                     IV    132    121 3.69 (2.28-5.97) < 0.001 ##> 11:          Sex (Male) × Treatment Group (Drug A)       -    128     89 1.11 (0.72-1.72)   0.637 ##> 12:          Sex (Male) × Treatment Group (Drug B)       -    176    143 0.98 (0.65-1.46)   0.903 ##> 13:  Treatment Group (Drug A) × Disease Stage (II)       -     93     52 0.75 (0.42-1.35)   0.339 ##> 14: Treatment Group (Drug A) × Disease Stage (III)       -     75     50 0.65 (0.35-1.20)   0.171 ##> 15:  Treatment Group (Drug A) × Disease Stage (IV)       - title: Advanced Workflows
text: Use compfit() formally evaluate whether interactions improve model fit (see Model Comparison):
code: example5 <- compfit(   data = clintrial,   outcome = "surgery",   model_list = list(     "Main Effects" = c("age", "sex", "treatment", "stage"),     "Sex × Treatment" = c("age", "sex", "treatment", "stage"),     "Stage × Treatment" = c("age", "sex", "treatment", "stage"),     "Both Interactions" = c("age", "sex", "treatment", "stage")   ),   interactions_list = list(     NULL,     c("sex:treatment"),     c("stage:treatment"),     c("sex:treatment", "stage:treatment")   ),   model_type = "glm",   labels = clintrial_labels )  example5 ##>  ##> Model Comparison Results ##> Outcome: surgery ##> Model Type: glm ##>  ##> CMS Weights: ##>   Convergence: 15% ##>   AIC: 25% ##>   Concordance: 40% ##>   Pseudo-R²: 15% ##>   Brier score: 5% ##>  ##> Recommended Model: Main Effects (CMS: 75.4) ##>  ##> Models ranked by selection score: ##>                Model   CMS     N Events Predictors Converged   AIC    BIC Pseudo-R² Concordance Brier Score Global p ##>               <char> <num> <int>  <num>      <int>    <char> <num>  <num>     <num>       <num>       <num>   <char> ##> 1:      Main Effects  75.4   847    368          4       Yes 984.4 1022.4     0.165       0.765       0.195  < 0.001 ##> 2:   Sex × Treatment  68.6   847    368          4       Yes 987.9 1035.4     0.165       0.765       0.195  < 0.001 ##> 3: Stage × Treatment  57.2   847    368          4       Yes 993.8 1060.1     0.167       0.766       0.194  < 0.001 ##> 4: Both Interactions  50.6   847    368          4       Yes 997.2 1073.1     0.168       0.766       0.194  < 0.001 ##>  ##> CMS interpretation: 85+ Excellent, 75-84 Very Good, 65-74 Good, 55-64 Fair, < 55 Poor

title: Advanced Workflows
text: Visualize models including interaction terms:
code: interaction_model <- fit(   data = clintrial,   outcome = "surgery",   predictors = c("age", "sex", "treatment", "stage"),   interactions = c("sex:treatment"),   model_type = "glm",   labels = clintrial_labels )  example6 <- glmforest(   x = attr(interaction_model, "model"),   title = "Logistic Regression with Interaction",   labels = clintrial_labels,   indent_groups = TRUE,   zebra_stripes = TRUE )

title: Advanced Workflows
text: Mixed-effects models (multilevel hierarchical models) account correlation within clusters incorporating random effects. summata package supports linear mixed models (lmer), generalized linear mixed models (glmer), Cox mixed models (coxme). Random effects specified using pipe notation within either predictors parameter separate random parameter:

title: Advanced Workflows
text: (1|site) syntax specifies random intercept site:
code: example7 <- fit(   data = clintrial,   outcome = "los_days",   predictors = c("age", "sex", "treatment", "stage", "(1|site)"),   model_type = "lmer",   labels = clintrial_labels )  example7 ##>  ##> Multivariable Linear Mixed Model ##> Formula: los_days ~ age + sex + treatment + stage + (1|site) ##> n = 827 ##>  ##>            Variable   Group      n Adj. Coefficient (95% CI) p-value ##>              <char>  <char> <char>                    <char>  <char> ##>  1:     Age (years)       -    827       0.14 (0.11 to 0.16) < 0.001 ##>  2:             Sex  Female    441                 reference       - ##>  3:                    Male    386       0.82 (0.28 to 1.37)   0.003 ##>  4: Treatment Group Control    190                 reference       - ##>  5:                  Drug A    288    -0.94 (-1.68 to -0.20)   0.013 ##>  6:                  Drug B    349       2.41 (1.70 to 3.13) < 0.001 ##>  7:   Disease Stage       I    207                 reference       - ##>  8:                      II    259       1.25 (0.51 to 1.98) < 0.001 ##>  9:                     III    235       2.43 (1.67 to 3.19) < 0.001 ##> 10:                      IV    126       2.96 (2.07 to 3.85) < 0.001

title: Advanced Workflows
text: binary outcomes clustering (note use independent random parameter):
code: example8 <- fit(     data = clintrial,     outcome = "surgery",     predictors = c("age", "sex", "treatment", "stage"),     random = "(1|site)",     model_type = "glmer",     labels = clintrial_labels )  example8 ##>  ##> Multivariable glmerMod Model ##> Formula: surgery ~ age + sex + treatment + stage + (1|site) ##> n = 847, Events = 368 ##>  ##>            Variable   Group      n Events     aOR (95% CI) p-value ##>              <char>  <char> <char> <char>           <char>  <char> ##>  1:     Age (years)       -    847    368 0.96 (0.94-0.97) < 0.001 ##>  2:             Sex  Female    449    203        reference       - ##>  3:                    Male    398    165 0.84 (0.62-1.14)   0.263 ##>  4: Treatment Group Control    194     93        reference       - ##>  5:                  Drug A    292    173 1.84 (1.23-2.75)   0.003 ##>  6:                  Drug B    361    102 0.45 (0.30-0.66) < 0.001 ##>  7:   Disease Stage       I    211    125        reference       - ##>  8:                      II    263    133 0.69 (0.47-1.03)   0.070 ##>  9:                     III    241     91 0.41 (0.27-0.62) < 0.001 ##> 10:                      IV    132     19 0.09 (0.05-0.17) < 0.001

title: Advanced Workflows
text: Allow effects vary cluster:
code: example9 <- fit(   data = clintrial,   outcome = "los_days",   predictors = c("age", "sex", "treatment", "stage", "(1 + treatment|site)"),   model_type = "lmer",   labels = clintrial_labels )  example9 ##>  ##> Multivariable Linear Mixed Model ##> Formula: los_days ~ age + sex + treatment + stage + (1 + treatment|site) ##> n = 827 ##>  ##>            Variable   Group      n Adj. Coefficient (95% CI) p-value ##>              <char>  <char> <char>                    <char>  <char> ##>  1:     Age (years)       -    827       0.14 (0.11 to 0.16) < 0.001 ##>  2:             Sex  Female    441                 reference       - ##>  3:                    Male    386       0.83 (0.28 to 1.38)   0.003 ##>  4: Treatment Group Control    190                 reference       - ##>  5:                  Drug A    288    -0.95 (-1.81 to -0.09)   0.031 ##>  6:                  Drug B    349       2.40 (1.65 to 3.16) < 0.001 ##>  7:   Disease Stage       I    207                 reference       - ##>  8:                      II    259       1.27 (0.53 to 2.00) < 0.001 ##>  9:                     III    235       2.44 (1.68 to 3.20) < 0.001 ##> 10:                      IV    126       3.01 (2.12 to 3.90) < 0.001

title: Advanced Workflows
text: survival outcomes clustering:
code: example10 <- fit(   data = clintrial,   outcome = "Surv(os_months, os_status)",   predictors = c("age", "sex", "treatment", "stage", "(1|site)"),   model_type = "coxme",   labels = clintrial_labels )  example10 ##>  ##> Multivariable Mixed Effects Cox Model ##> Formula: Surv(os_months, os_status) ~ age + sex + treatment + stage + (1|site) ##> n = 847, Events = 606 ##>  ##>            Variable   Group      n Events     aHR (95% CI) p-value ##>              <char>  <char> <char> <char>           <char>  <char> ##>  1:     Age (years)       -    847    606 1.04 (1.03-1.05) < 0.001 ##>  2:             Sex  Female    450    298        reference       - ##>  3:                    Male    400    311 1.36 (1.16-1.60) < 0.001 ##>  4: Treatment Group Control    196    151        reference       - ##>  5:                  Drug A    292    184 0.57 (0.46-0.72) < 0.001 ##>  6:                  Drug B    362    274 0.93 (0.76-1.14)   0.492 ##>  7:   Disease Stage       I    211    127        reference       - ##>  8:                      II    263    172 1.17 (0.93-1.48)   0.178 ##>  9:                     III    241    186 2.04 (1.62-2.58) < 0.001 ##> 10:                      IV    132    121 4.09 (3.16-5.30) < 0.001

what: Example 11: Forest Plot from Mixed Model
title: Advanced Workflows
text: Visualize fixed effects mixed-effects models:
code: example11 <- glmforest(   x = attr(example8, "model"),   title = "Logistic Mixed Model (Fixed Effects)",   labels = clintrial_labels,   indent_groups = TRUE,   zebra_stripes = TRUE )

title: Advanced Workflows
text: Use compfit() compare different random-effects structures (see Model Comparison):
code: example12 <- compfit(   data = clintrial,   outcome = "los_days",   model_list = list(     "Random Intercepts" = c("age", "sex", "treatment", "stage", "(1|site)"),     "Random Slopes" = c("age", "sex", "treatment", "stage", "(1 + treatment|site)")   ),   model_type = "lmer",   labels = clintrial_labels )  example12 ##>  ##> Model Comparison Results ##> Outcome: los_days ##> Model Type: lmer ##>  ##> CMS Weights: ##>   Convergence: 20% ##>   AIC: 25% ##>   Marginal R²: 25% ##>   Conditional R²: 15% ##>   ICC: 15% ##>  ##> Recommended Model: Random Intercepts (CMS: 74) ##>  ##> Models ranked by selection score: ##>                Model   CMS     N Events Predictors Groups Converged    AIC    BIC Pseudo-R² Marginal R² Conditional R²   ICC Concordance Global p ##>               <char> <num> <int>  <num>      <int>  <int>    <char>  <num>  <num>     <num>       <num>          <num> <num>       <num>   <char> ##> 1: Random Intercepts  74.0   827     NA          5     10       Yes 4679.8 4727.0     0.275       0.275          0.330 0.076          NA  < 0.001 ##> 2:     Random Slopes  49.1   827     NA          5     10       Yes 4688.5 4759.3     0.276       0.276          0.334 0.098          NA  < 0.001 ##>  ##> CMS interpretation: 85+ Excellent, 75-84 Very Good, 65-74 Good, 55-64 Fair, < 55 Poor

title: Advanced Workflows
text: addition mixed-effects models, Cox models support stratification (separate baseline hazards) clustered standard errors (robust variance estimation).

title: Advanced Workflows
text: Stratification allows nonproportional hazards across strata without estimating stratum effects. Use strata parameter:
code: example13 <- fit(   data = clintrial,   outcome = "Surv(os_months, os_status)",   predictors = c("age", "sex", "treatment"),   strata = "site",   model_type = "coxph",   labels = clintrial_labels )  example13 ##>  ##> Multivariable Cox PH Model ##> Formula: Surv(os_months, os_status) ~ age + sex + treatment + strata( site ) ##> n = 850, Events = 609 ##>  ##>           Variable   Group      n Events     aHR (95% CI) p-value ##>             <char>  <char> <char> <char>           <char>  <char> ##> 1:     Age (years)       -    850    609 1.04 (1.03-1.04) < 0.001 ##> 2:             Sex  Female    450    298        reference       - ##> 3:                    Male    400    311 1.28 (1.09-1.50)   0.003 ##> 4: Treatment Group Control    196    151        reference       - ##> 5:                  Drug A    292    184 0.62 (0.50-0.77) < 0.001 ##> 6:                  Drug B    362    274 1.00 (0.81-1.22)   0.976

title: Advanced Workflows
text: robust inference correlated observations, cluster-robust standard errors account within-cluster correlation. Use cluster parameter:
code: example14 <- fit(   data = clintrial,   outcome = "Surv(os_months, os_status)",   predictors = c("age", "sex", "treatment", "stage"),   cluster = "site",   model_type = "coxph",   labels = clintrial_labels )  example14 ##>  ##> Multivariable Cox PH Model ##> Formula: Surv(os_months, os_status) ~ age + sex + treatment + stage ##> n = 847, Events = 606 ##>  ##>            Variable   Group      n Events     aHR (95% CI) p-value ##>              <char>  <char> <char> <char>           <char>  <char> ##>  1:     Age (years)       -    847    606 1.04 (1.03-1.04) < 0.001 ##>  2:             Sex  Female    450    298        reference       - ##>  3:                    Male    400    311 1.33 (1.19-1.48) < 0.001 ##>  4: Treatment Group Control    196    151        reference       - ##>  5:                  Drug A    292    184 0.56 (0.40-0.78) < 0.001 ##>  6:                  Drug B    362    274 0.83 (0.63-1.08)   0.167 ##>  7:   Disease Stage       I    211    127        reference       - ##>  8:                      II    263    172 1.16 (0.93-1.44)   0.189 ##>  9:                     III    241    186 1.90 (1.61-2.23) < 0.001 ##> 10:                      IV    132    121 3.57 (2.86-4.46) < 0.001

title: Advanced Workflows
text: survey data inverse probability weighting, use weights parameter. Weights can account sampling design, non-response, confounding adjustment.

title: Advanced Workflows
code: # Create example weights clintrial$analysis_weight <- runif(nrow(clintrial), 0.5, 2.0)  example15 <- fit(   data = clintrial,   outcome = "surgery",   predictors = c("age", "sex", "treatment", "stage"),   weights = "analysis_weight",   model_type = "glm",   labels = clintrial_labels )  example15 ##>  ##> Multivariable Logistic Model ##> Formula: surgery ~ age + sex + treatment + stage ##> n = 847, Events = 368 ##>  ##>            Variable   Group      n Events     aOR (95% CI) p-value ##>              <char>  <char> <char> <char>           <char>  <char> ##>  1:     Age (years)       -    847    368 0.96 (0.95-0.98) < 0.001 ##>  2:             Sex  Female    449    203        reference       - ##>  3:                    Male    398    165 0.80 (0.61-1.05)   0.109 ##>  4: Treatment Group Control    194     93        reference       - ##>  5:                  Drug A    292    173 1.71 (1.19-2.45)   0.004 ##>  6:                  Drug B    361    102 0.42 (0.29-0.59) < 0.001 ##>  7:   Disease Stage       I    211    125        reference       - ##>  8:                      II    263    133 0.68 (0.48-0.96)   0.028 ##>  9:                     III    241     91 0.40 (0.28-0.58) < 0.001 ##> 10:                      IV    132     19 0.08 (0.04-0.13) < 0.001

title: Advanced Workflows
text: uniscreen() function supports advanced model specifications including random effects stratification.

what: Example 16: Univariable Screening with Random Effects
title: Advanced Workflows
text: Screen multiple predictors accounting clustering:
code: example16 <- uniscreen(   data = clintrial,   outcome = "surgery",   predictors = c("age", "sex", "treatment", "stage"),   model_type = "glmer",   random = "(1|site)",   labels = clintrial_labels )  example16 ##>  ##> Univariable Screening Results ##> Outcome: surgery ##> Model Type: glmerMod ##> Predictors Screened: 4 ##> Significant (p < 0.05): 3 ##>  ##>            Variable   Group      n Events      OR (95% CI) p-value ##>              <char>  <char> <char> <char>           <char>  <char> ##>  1:     Age (years)       -    850    370 0.96 (0.95-0.98) < 0.001 ##>  2:             Sex  Female    450    204        reference       - ##>  3:                    Male    400    166 0.85 (0.65-1.12)   0.256 ##>  4: Treatment Group Control    196     94        reference       - ##>  5:                  Drug A    292    173 1.57 (1.09-2.27)   0.016 ##>  6:                  Drug B    362    103 0.43 (0.30-0.62) < 0.001 ##>  7:   Disease Stage       I    211    125        reference       - ##>  8:                      II    263    133 0.72 (0.50-1.04)   0.080 ##>  9:                     III    241     91 0.42 (0.29-0.61) < 0.001 ##> 10:                      IV    132     19 0.12 (0.07-0.20) < 0.001

what: Example 17: Cox Screening with Mixed Effects
title: Advanced Workflows
text: survival outcomes clustering:
code: example17 <- uniscreen(   data = clintrial,   outcome = "Surv(os_months, os_status)",   predictors = c("age", "sex", "treatment", "stage", "ecog"),   model_type = "coxme",   random = "(1|site)",   labels = clintrial_labels )  example17 ##>  ##> Univariable Screening Results ##> Outcome: Surv(os_months, os_status) ##> Model Type: Mixed Effects Cox ##> Predictors Screened: 5 ##> Significant (p < 0.05): 5 ##>  ##>                    Variable   Group      n Events      HR (95% CI) p-value ##>                      <char>  <char> <char> <char>           <char>  <char> ##>  1:             Age (years)       -    850    609 1.04 (1.03-1.04) < 0.001 ##>  2:                     Sex  Female    450    298        reference       - ##>  3:                            Male    400    311 1.30 (1.11-1.53)   0.001 ##>  4:         Treatment Group Control    196    151        reference       - ##>  5:                          Drug A    292    184 0.65 (0.53-0.81) < 0.001 ##>  6:                          Drug B    362    274 1.04 (0.85-1.27)   0.735 ##>  7:           Disease Stage       I    211    127        reference       - ##>  8:                              II    263    172 1.13 (0.90-1.42)   0.307 ##>  9:                             III    241    186 1.78 (1.41-2.23) < 0.001 ##> 10:                              IV    132    121 3.63 (2.81-4.69) < 0.001 ##> 11: ECOG Performance Status       0    265    159        reference       - ##> 12:                               1    302    212 1.31 (1.07-1.62)   0.010 ##> 13:                               2    238    194 1.92 (1.56-2.37) < 0.001 ##> 14:                               3     37     37 2.95 (2.05-4.24) < 0.001

what: Example 18: Forest Plot from Univariable Screening
title: Advanced Workflows
text: uniforest() function visualizes univariable screening results:
code: uni_results <- uniscreen(   data = clintrial,   outcome = "Surv(os_months, os_status)",   predictors = c("age", "sex", "treatment", "stage", "ecog", "grade"),   model_type = "coxph",   labels = clintrial_labels )  example18 <- uniforest(   uni_results,   title = "Univariable Screening Results",   indent_groups = TRUE,   zebra_stripes = TRUE )

title: Advanced Workflows
text: multifit() function supports interactions mixed-effects models testing predictor across multiple outcomes.

title: Advanced Workflows
text: Test effect modification across multiple outcomes:
code: example19 <- multifit(   data = clintrial,   outcomes = c("surgery", "pfs_status", "os_status"),   predictor = "treatment",   covariates = c("age", "sex", "stage"),   interactions = c("treatment:sex"),   labels = clintrial_labels,   parallel = FALSE )  example19 ##>  ##> Multivariate Analysis Results ##> Predictor: treatment ##> Outcomes: 3 ##> Model Type: glm ##> Covariates: age, sex, stage ##> Interactions: treatment:sex ##> Display: adjusted ##>  ##>                        Outcome                             Predictor      n Events     aOR (95% CI) p-value ##>                         <char>                                <char> <char> <char>           <char>  <char> ##>  1:         Surgical Resection              Treatment Group (Drug A)    292    173 2.09 (1.20-3.62)   0.009 ##>  2:         Surgical Resection              Treatment Group (Drug B)    361    102 0.50 (0.29-0.87)   0.013 ##>  3:         Surgical Resection Treatment Group (Drug A) × Sex (Male)      -      - 0.77 (0.34-1.71)   0.515 ##>  4:         Surgical Resection Treatment Group (Drug B) × Sex (Male)      -      - 0.79 (0.36-1.73)   0.554 ##>  5: Progression or Death Event              Treatment Group (Drug A)    292    227 0.41 (0.20-0.84)   0.015 ##>  6: Progression or Death Event              Treatment Group (Drug B)    361    321 0.95 (0.43-2.09)   0.893 ##>  7: Progression or Death Event Treatment Group (Drug A) × Sex (Male)      -      - 0.75 (0.24-2.32)   0.618 ##>  8: Progression or Death Event Treatment Group (Drug B) × Sex (Male)      -      - 0.63 (0.20-2.06)   0.448 ##>  9:                Death Event              Treatment Group (Drug A)    292    184 0.39 (0.22-0.70)   0.002 ##> 10:                Death Event              Treatment Group (Drug B)    361    273 0.61 (0.34-1.10)   0.099 ##> 11:                Death Event Treatment Group (Drug A) × Sex (Male)      -      - 1.29 (0.53-3.18)   0.575 ##> 12:                Death Event Treatment Group (Drug B) × Sex (Male)      -      - 1.59 (0.65-3.91)   0.308

title: Advanced Workflows
text: Account clustering testing effects across outcomes:
code: example20 <- multifit(   data = clintrial,   outcomes = c("surgery", "pfs_status"),   predictor = "treatment",   covariates = c("age", "sex"),   random = "(1|site)",   model_type = "glmer",   labels = clintrial_labels,   parallel = FALSE )  example20 ##>  ##> Multivariate Analysis Results ##> Predictor: treatment ##> Outcomes: 2 ##> Model Type: glmer ##> Covariates: age, sex ##> Random Effects: (1|site) ##> Display: adjusted ##>  ##>                       Outcome                Predictor      n Events     aOR (95% CI) p-value ##>                        <char>                   <char> <char> <char>           <char>  <char> ##> 1:         Surgical Resection Treatment Group (Drug A)    292    173 1.64 (1.13-2.40)   0.010 ##> 2:         Surgical Resection Treatment Group (Drug B)    362    103 0.44 (0.30-0.63) < 0.001 ##> 3: Progression or Death Event Treatment Group (Drug A)    292    227 0.41 (0.24-0.71)   0.001 ##> 4: Progression or Death Event Treatment Group (Drug B)    362    322 1.01 (0.58-1.78)   0.971

title: Performance Benchmarks
text: benchmarks conducted using microbenchmark package following conditions: Iterations: 5–20 per benchmark, adjusted computational intensity Dataset sizes: 500 10,000 observations Data structure: Simulated clinical trial data continuous, categorical, time--event variables Predictors: 14 variables screening benchmarks Datasets generated using fixed random seed ensure reproducibility. Timing measurements exclude package loading data generation. packages tested using default parameters unless otherwise noted.
code: library(summata) library(microbenchmark) library(ggplot2)

title: Performance Benchmarks
text: Descriptive summary tables represent common first step data analysis. following packages provide comparable functionality differing implementation strategies.  observed timing differences reflect underlying implementation choices. Packages built data.table base R matrix operations (summata, tableone, arsenal) exhibit lower overhead employing extensive formatting pipelines (gtsummary). gtsummary package prioritizes output flexibility gt integration, introduces additional computational cost.

title: Performance Benchmarks
text: Survival probability tables summarize Kaplan-Meier estimates specified time points.  Direct survfit() computation provides baseline minimum time required. difference raw computation formatted output reflects cost table construction presentation logic.

title: Performance Benchmarks
text: following benchmarks compare functions extract format regression coefficients. package produces tables suitable publication, though varying levels default formatting. Compared functions follows:

title: Performance Benchmarks
text: Among tested packages, summata performs fastest summarization logistic regression models. summata_minimal configuration, omits sample size counts reference rows, provides additional speed cost less-complete output.

title: Performance Benchmarks
text: linear models, broom::tidy() finalfit achieve faster coefficient extraction due simpler structure lm objects. summata package applies additional formatting default, accounting difference.

title: Performance Benchmarks
text: Poisson regression exhibits similar patterns logistic regression, GLM-based extraction showing comparable relative performance across packages low-n scenarios.

title: Performance Benchmarks
text: Similar linear regression, Cox model coefficient extraction fastest finalfit broom, minimal formatting required; summata takes slightly longer due formatting overhead.

title: Performance Benchmarks
text: Mixed-effects models present useful comparison case underlying model fitting (via lme4) dominates execution time regardless wrapper package.  relatively narrow spread among summata, finalfit, broom reflects dominance model fitting time. Differences wrapper overhead become proportionally less significant underlying computation grows.

title: Performance Benchmarks
text: Univariable screening—fitting separate models predictor—provides test case operations involving many repeated model fits.  Screening 14 predictors: performance differences univariable screening pronounced single-model extraction, overhead compounds across multiple model fits. gtsummary timings reflect extensive table formatting applied predictor.

title: Performance Benchmarks
text: combined univariable screening multivariable modeling workflow represents common analytical pattern statistical research.  summata finalfit packages show comparable performance complete workflow, reflecting similar design philosophies. packages optimize combined operation rather simply chaining separate functions.

title: Performance Benchmarks
text: Forest plot generation combines data extraction graphical rendering.  manual approach produces graphical element, summata survminer generate integrated displays coefficient tables. relatively constant timing across dataset sizes indicates plot rendering, rather data processing, dominates execution time. Also, significant cosmetic differences three graphical outputs, predominates factors selecting plotting function.

title: Performance Benchmarks
text: relationship dataset size execution time provides insight algorithmic complexity. title: Performance Benchmarks
text: performance characteristics documented reflect specific implementation choices: summata: Built data.table data manipulation, coefficient extraction optimized common model classes. Formatting applied extraction rather separate step. gtsummary: Prioritizes output flexibility gt table framework. additional abstraction layers enable extensive customization increase computational overhead. finalfit: Balances functionality performance tidyverse-compatible interface. finalfit() function particularly optimized combined workflow. arsenal: Uses formula-based syntax familiar SAS users. Performance varies operation type. broom: Provides minimal coefficient extraction limited formatting. Suitable building block custom pipelines.

title: Performance Benchmarks
text: default, summata functions compute sample sizes, event counts, reference rows categorical variables. features add computational overhead produce complete output publication. performance-sensitive applications, options can disabled. summata_minimal configuration shown benchmarks generally represents: configuration reduces execution time approximately 25–40% compared default settings, producing output comparable broom finalfit. choice configurations depends use-case: Publication tables: Default settings provide complete output ready manuscripts Simulation studies: Minimal settings reduce per-iteration overhead Exploratory analysis: Either setting appropriate depending information needs

title: Descriptive Tables
text: examples vignette use clintrial dataset included summata: clintrial dataset contains 850 observations continuous, categorical, time--event variables suitable demonstrating descriptive statistics. clintrial_labels vector provides human-readable labels display.

title: Descriptive Tables
text: desctable() function automatically selects appropriate summary statistics hypothesis tests based variable type: categorical variables, Fisher exact test used expected cell count falls 5. continuous variables, test selection follows displayed statistic: parametric tests used mean-based statistics, nonparametric tests median-based statistics.

title: Descriptive Tables
text: common use-case descriptive tables comparing characteristics across groups: output includes “Total” column default, showing overall statistics alongside group-specific values.

title: Descriptive Tables
text: Omitting argument produces overall summary statistics without group comparisons:
code: example2 <- desctable(   data = clintrial,   variables = c("age", "bmi", "sex", "stage"),   labels = clintrial_labels )  example2 #>                   Variable        Group            Total #>                     <char>       <char>           <char> #> 1:                       N                           850 #> 2:             Age (years) Median [IQR] 60.0 [52.0-67.0] #> 3: Body Mass Index (kg/m²) Median [IQR] 28.0 [24.7-31.5] #> 4:                     Sex       Female      450 (52.9%) #> 5:                                 Male      400 (47.1%) #> 6:           Disease Stage            I      211 (24.9%) #> 7:                                   II      263 (31.1%) #> 8:                                  III      241 (28.5%) #> 9:                                   IV      132 (15.6%)

text: comparing groups, hypothesis tests assess whether observed differences statistically significant.

title: Descriptive Tables
text: default, automatic hypothesis testing based summary statistic displayed (test = TRUE). Setting test = FALSE disables functionality:

title: Descriptive Tables
text: Override automatic selection test_continuous test_categorical. Available test specifications include: Continuous (test_continuous): "auto": Automatic selection (default) "t": Student t-test "wrs": Wilcoxon rank-sum test "aov": One-way ANOVA "kwt": Kruskal–Wallis test Categorical (test_categorical): "auto": Automatic selection (default) "chisq": Pearson χ² test "fisher": Fisher exact test following example forces parametric tests continuous variables: example forces Fisher exact test categorical variables:
code: example7a <- desctable(   data = clintrial,   by = "treatment",   variables = c("age", "bmi", "los_days"),   test = TRUE,   test_continuous = "aov",  # ANOVA   labels = clintrial_labels )  example7a #>                          Variable        Group            Total          Control           Drug A           Drug B p-value #>                            <char>       <char>           <char>           <char>           <char>           <char>  <char> #> 1:                              N                           850              196              292              362         #> 2:                    Age (years) Median [IQR] 60.0 [52.0-67.0] 59.0 [52.0-66.5] 60.0 [51.0-68.0] 61.0 [53.0-68.0]   0.472 #> 3:        Body Mass Index (kg/m²) Median [IQR] 28.0 [24.7-31.5] 28.0 [24.4-31.9] 28.2 [24.9-31.3] 27.8 [24.7-31.5]   0.880 #> 4: Length of Hospital Stay (days) Median [IQR] 19.9 [16.5-23.3] 19.0 [15.9-22.2] 18.1 [15.2-21.4] 21.7 [18.8-25.4] < 0.001 example7b <- desctable(   data = clintrial,   by = "treatment",   variables = c("sex", "stage"),   test = TRUE,   test_categorical = "fisher",   labels = clintrial_labels )  example7b #>         Variable  Group       Total     Control      Drug A      Drug B p-value #>           <char> <char>      <char>      <char>      <char>      <char>  <char> #> 1:             N                850         196         292         362         #> 2:           Sex Female 450 (52.9%) 100 (51.0%) 164 (56.2%) 186 (51.4%)   0.394 #> 3:                 Male 400 (47.1%)  96 (49.0%) 128 (43.8%) 176 (48.6%)         #> 4: Disease Stage      I 211 (24.9%)  59 (30.4%)  78 (26.7%)  74 (20.5%)         #> 5:                   II 263 (31.1%)  65 (33.5%)  93 (31.8%) 105 (29.1%)         #> 6:                  III 241 (28.5%)  39 (20.1%)  75 (25.7%) 127 (35.2%)         #> 7:                   IV 132 (15.6%)  31 (16.0%)  46 (15.8%)  55 (15.2%)

title: Descriptive Tables
text: Missing values require special consideration descriptive tables. Options control whether missing values displayed percentages calculated.

title: Descriptive Tables
text: default, missing values excluded calculations. Set na_include = TRUE display separate category:
code: example8 <- desctable(   data = clintrial,   by = "treatment",   variables = c("smoking", "diabetes"),   na_include = TRUE,   labels = clintrial_labels )  example8 #>          Variable   Group       Total     Control      Drug A      Drug B p-value #>            <char>  <char>      <char>      <char>      <char>      <char>  <char> #> 1:              N                 850         196         292         362         #> 2: Smoking Status   Never 337 (40.5%)  83 (43.5%) 123 (42.7%) 131 (37.0%)   0.450 #> 3:                 Former 311 (37.3%)  66 (34.6%) 101 (35.1%) 144 (40.7%)         #> 4:                Current 185 (22.2%)  42 (22.0%)  64 (22.2%)  79 (22.3%)         #> 5:                Unknown          17           5           4           8         #> 6:       Diabetes      No 637 (76.4%) 152 (79.6%) 218 (75.7%) 267 (75.2%)   0.490 #> 7:                    Yes 197 (23.6%)  39 (20.4%)  70 (24.3%)  88 (24.8%)         #> 8:                Unknown          16           5           4           7

title: Descriptive Tables
text: na_percent parameter controls whether missing values included percentage calculations:
code: # Percentages exclude missing (denominator = non-missing) example9a <- desctable(   data = clintrial,   by = "treatment",   variables = c("smoking"),   na_include = TRUE,   na_percent = FALSE,   labels = clintrial_labels )  example9a #>          Variable   Group       Total    Control      Drug A      Drug B p-value #>            <char>  <char>      <char>     <char>      <char>      <char>  <char> #> 1:              N                 850        196         292         362         #> 2: Smoking Status   Never 337 (40.5%) 83 (43.5%) 123 (42.7%) 131 (37.0%)   0.450 #> 3:                 Former 311 (37.3%) 66 (34.6%) 101 (35.1%) 144 (40.7%)         #> 4:                Current 185 (22.2%) 42 (22.0%)  64 (22.2%)  79 (22.3%)         #> 5:                Unknown          17          5           4           8  # Percentages include missing (denominator = total) example9b <- desctable(   data = clintrial,   by = "treatment",   variables = c("smoking"),   na_include = TRUE,   na_percent = TRUE,   labels = clintrial_labels )  example9b #>          Variable   Group       Total    Control      Drug A      Drug B p-value #>            <char>  <char>      <char>     <char>      <char>      <char>  <char> #> 1:              N                 850        196         292         362         #> 2: Smoking Status   Never 337 (39.6%) 83 (42.3%) 123 (42.1%) 131 (36.2%)   0.450 #> 3:                 Former 311 (36.6%) 66 (33.7%) 101 (34.6%) 144 (39.8%)         #> 4:                Current 185 (21.8%) 42 (21.4%)  64 (21.9%)  79 (21.8%)         #> 5:                Unknown   17 (2.0%)   5 (2.6%)    4 (1.4%)    8 (2.2%)

text: Descriptive tables can exported various formats. See Table Export vignette comprehensive documentation.
code: # Microsoft Word table2docx(   table = table1,   file = "Table1.docx",   caption = "Table 1. Baseline Characteristics by Group" )  # PDF (requires LaTeX) table2pdf(   table = table1,   file = "Table1.pdf",   caption = "Table 1. Baseline Characteristics by Group" )  # HTML table2html(   table = table1,   file = "Table1.html",   caption = "Table 1. Baseline Characteristics by Group" )

title: Descriptive Tables
text: Include relevant baseline characteristics Order variables logically (typically chronologically domain) Exclude grouping variable variables list

title: Descriptive Tables
text: Use automatic test selection unless specific justification otherwise Report exact p-values possible; small values display “< 0.001” (preferred degree precision) Consider multiple comparison adjustments testing many variables skewed non-normally distributed continuous variables, report using nonparametric statistical procedures (e.g., median IQR) rather parametric ones (e.g., mean SD)

title: Descriptive Tables
text: Use consistent decimal precision within variable types Include units variable labels (e.g., “Age (years)”) Include total column context Use landscape orientation tables many columns

title: Descriptive Tables
text: factor level zero observations group, ensure levels explicitly defined:
code: data$stage <- factor(data$stage, levels = c("I", "II", "III", "IV"))

title: Descriptive Tables
text: highly skewed distributions, use median IQR:
code: desctable(data, by, variables, stats_continuous = "median_iqr")

title: Descriptive Tables
text: tables many variables, consider splitting category using landscape orientation export:
code: table2pdf(table, "table1.pdf", orientation = "landscape", font_size = 8)

title: Feature Comparison
text: R ecosystem includes several well-established packages creating publication-ready regression tables. following comparison identifies areas overlap distinction summata alternatives.

title: Feature Comparison
text: Legend: ✓ Full support | ◐ Partial support | — available

title: Feature Comparison
text: following features distinguish summata comparable packages:

title: Feature Comparison
text: multifit() multiforest() functions implement inverted screening paradigm: testing single predictor across multiple outcomes simultaneously. workflow common epidemiological clinical research directly supported packages.
code: # Test treatment effect across multiple outcomes result <- multifit(   data = clintrial,   outcomes = c("surgery", "pfs_status", "os_status"),   predictor = "treatment",   covariates = c("age", "sex", "stage") )

title: Feature Comparison
text: Customizable forest plots generated directly analysis results without intermediate steps:
code: # From univariable screening screen_result <- uniscreen(data, outcome, predictors) uniforest(screen_result)  # From multivariate regression multi_result <- multifit(data, outcomes, predictor) multiforest(multi_result)

title: Feature Comparison
text: Built data.table computational efficiency, summata demonstrates 35–54% faster execution comparable workflows packages smaller dependency footprint. See Benchmarks article detailed comparisons.

title: Feature Comparison
text: modeling functions share consistent syntax across model types:
code: # Same interface for different model types fit(data, outcome, predictors, model_type = "glm") fit(data, outcome, predictors, model_type = "coxph") fit(data, outcome, predictors, model_type = "lmer", random = "(1|site)")

title: Feature Comparison
text: Full support coxme (mixed-effects Cox models) alongside lmer glmer, limited absent packages.

title: Forest Plots
text: examples vignette use clintrial dataset included summata: Moreover, note plots vignette generated using ggsave() function recommended dimensions—.e., ensures figure size always large enough accommodate constituent plot text graphics, generally preferred method saving forest plot outputs summata.
code: library(summata) library(survival) library(ggplot2)  data(clintrial) data(clintrial_labels) ggsave("output.png", plot,         width = attr(plot, "rec_dims")$width,         height = attr(plot, "rec_dims")$height,         units = "in")

what: Creating Forest Plots from Model Objects
title: Forest Plots
text: Forest plots can created standard R model objects summata function output.

previous_headings: Creating Forest Plots from Model Objects
title: Forest Plots
text: Fit model using base R, create forest plot:
code: logistic_model <- glm(   surgery ~ age + sex + stage + treatment + ecog,   data = clintrial,   family = binomial )  example1 <- glmforest(   x = logistic_model,   data = clintrial,   title = "Logistic Regression: Predictors of Outcome",   labels = clintrial_labels )

previous_headings: Creating Forest Plots from Model Objects
title: Forest Plots
text: continuous outcomes, use lmforest():
code: linear_model <- lm(   los_days ~ age + sex + stage + surgery + ecog,   data = clintrial )  example2 <- lmforest(   x = linear_model,   data = clintrial,   title = "Linear Regression: Length of Stay",   labels = clintrial_labels )

previous_headings: Creating Forest Plots from Model Objects
title: Forest Plots
text: survival models, use coxforest():
code: cox_model <- coxph(   Surv(os_months, os_status) ~ age + sex + stage + treatment + ecog,   data = clintrial )  example3 <- coxforest(   x = cox_model,   data = clintrial,   title = "Cox Regression: Survival Analysis",   labels = clintrial_labels )

previous_headings: Creating Forest Plots from Model Objects
title: Forest Plots
text: autoforest() function detects model type automatically:
code: example4 <- autoforest(   x = cox_model,   data = clintrial,   labels = clintrial_labels )

what: Creating Forest Plots from summata Output
title: Forest Plots
text: Forest plots integrate seamlessly fit() fullfit() output extracting attached model object.

previous_headings: Creating Forest Plots from summata Output
what: Example 5: Direct Extraction from Fit Output
title: Forest Plots
text: approach works logistic models:
code: table_logistic <- fit(   data = clintrial,   outcome = "surgery",   predictors = c("age", "sex", "stage", "treatment", "ecog"),   model_type = "glm",   labels = clintrial_labels )  example5 <- glmforest(   x = attr(table_logistic, "model"),   title = "Predictors of Surgical Intervention",   labels = clintrial_labels,   zebra_stripes = TRUE )

previous_headings: Creating Forest Plots from summata Output
what: Example 6: Model Attribute from Fit Output
title: Forest Plots
text: Cox models:
code: table_cox <- fit(   data = clintrial,   outcome = "Surv(os_months, os_status)",   predictors = c("age", "sex", "stage", "treatment", "ecog"),   model_type = "coxph",   labels = clintrial_labels )  example6 <- coxforest(   x = attr(table_cox, "model"),   title = "Predictors of Overall Survival",   labels = clintrial_labels,   zebra_stripes = TRUE )

title: Forest Plots
text: indent_groups parameter creates hierarchical display compact aesthetic:
code: example7 <- glmforest(   x = attr(table_logistic, "model"),   title = "Indented Factor Levels",   labels = clintrial_labels,   indent_groups = TRUE )

title: Forest Plots
text: condense_table parameter displays binary variables single rows. Group indenting applied automatically:
code: example8 <- glmforest(   x = attr(table_logistic, "model"),   title = "Condensed Display",   labels = clintrial_labels,   condense_table = TRUE )

title: Forest Plots
text: Zebra striping enabled default. can disabled via zebra_stripes = FALSE:
code: example9 <- glmforest(   x = attr(table_logistic, "model"),   title = "Without Zebra Striping",   labels = clintrial_labels,   indent_groups = TRUE,   zebra_stripes = FALSE )

what: Example 10: Sample Size and Event Columns
title: Forest Plots
text: Control display sample size (n) event counts:
code: # Show both n and events example10a <- coxforest(   x = attr(table_cox, "model"),   title = "With Sample Size and Events",   labels = clintrial_labels,   show_n = TRUE,   show_events = TRUE,   indent_groups = TRUE,   zebra_stripes = TRUE ) # Minimal display example10b <- coxforest(   x = attr(table_cox, "model"),   title = "Minimal Display",   labels = clintrial_labels,   show_n = FALSE,   show_events = FALSE,   indent_groups = TRUE )

title: Forest Plots
text: digits parameter controls decimal places effect estimates confidence intervals:
code: example11 <- glmforest(   x = attr(table_logistic, "model"),   title = "Custom Precision (3 decimal places)",   labels = clintrial_labels,   digits = 3,   indent_groups = TRUE )

title: Forest Plots
text: Customize label shown reference categories:
code: example12 <- glmforest(   x = attr(table_logistic, "model"),   title = "Custom Reference Label",   labels = clintrial_labels,   ref_label = "ref",   indent_groups = TRUE )

title: Forest Plots
text: Change column header effect measure:
code: example13 <- coxforest(   x = attr(table_cox, "model"),   title = "Custom Effect Label",   labels = clintrial_labels,   effect_label = "Effect (95% CI)",   indent_groups = TRUE )

title: Forest Plots
text: color parameter changes point line color:
code: example14 <- glmforest(   x = attr(table_logistic, "model"),   title = "Custom Color",   labels = clintrial_labels,   color = "#E41A1C",   indent_groups = TRUE )

title: Forest Plots
text: Adjust text size font_size multiplier:
code: example15 <- glmforest(   x = attr(table_logistic, "model"),   title = "Larger Font (1.5×)",   labels = clintrial_labels,   font_size = 1.5,   indent_groups = TRUE )

title: Forest Plots
text: table_width parameter adjusts proportion space allocated table vs. forest plot:
code: # Wide table (for long variable names) example16a <- glmforest(   x = attr(table_logistic, "model"),   title = "Wide Table (75%)",   labels = clintrial_labels,   table_width = 0.75 ) # Narrow table (emphasizes forest plot) example16b <- glmforest(   x = attr(table_logistic, "model"),   title = "Narrow Table (50%)",   labels = clintrial_labels,   table_width = 0.50 )

title: Forest Plots
text: Forest plots include rec_dims attribute optimal sizing saving files.

title: Forest Plots
text: Use recommended dimensions optimal output:
code: p <- glmforest(   x = attr(table_logistic, "model"),   title = "Publication-Ready Plot",   labels = clintrial_labels,   indent_groups = TRUE,   zebra_stripes = TRUE )  # Get recommended dimensions dims <- attr(p, "rec_dims") cat("Width:", dims$width, "inches\n") cat("Height:", dims$height, "inches\n")  # Save with recommended dimensions ggsave(   filename = "forest_plot.pdf",   plot = p,   width = dims$width,   height = dims$height,   units = "in" )

title: Forest Plots
text: Export different formats needed:
code: p <- glmforest(   x = attr(table_logistic, "model"),   title = "Forest Plot",   labels = clintrial_labels )  dims <- attr(p, "rec_dims")  # PDF (vector, best for publications) # ggsave("forest.pdf", p, width = dims$width, height = dims$height)  # PNG (raster, good for presentations) # ggsave("forest.png", p, width = dims$width, height = dims$height, dpi = 300)  # TIFF (high-quality raster, often required by journals) # ggsave("forest.tiff", p, width = dims$width, height = dims$height, dpi = 300)  # SVG (vector, good for web) # ggsave("forest.svg", p, width = dims$width, height = dims$height)

title: Forest Plots
text: Forest plots support extensive customization publication requirements.

title: Forest Plots
text: Combine multiple options publication-ready output:
code: example19 <- coxforest(   x = attr(table_cox, "model"),   title = "Comprehensive Survival Analysis",   labels = clintrial_labels,   effect_label = "Hazard Ratio",   digits = 2,   show_n = TRUE,   show_events = TRUE,   indent_groups = TRUE,   condense_table = FALSE,   zebra_stripes = TRUE,   ref_label = "reference",   font_size = 1.0,   table_width = 0.62,   color = "#8A61D8" )

title: Forest Plots
text: Forest plots ggplot2 objects can modified :
code: example20 <- glmforest(   x = attr(table_logistic, "model"),   title = "Extended with ggplot2",   labels = clintrial_labels,   indent_groups = TRUE )  example20_modified <- example20 +   theme(     plot.title = element_text(face = "italic", color = "#A72727"),     plot.background = element_rect(fill = "white", color = NA)   )

title: Forest Plots
text: glmforest() function supports GLM families. can plotted similar fashion standard logistic regression forest plots. See Regression Modeling full list supported model types.

title: Forest Plots
text: equidispersed count outcomes (variance ≈ mean), use Poisson regression:
code: poisson_model <- glm(   fu_count ~ age + stage + treatment + surgery,   data = clintrial,   family = poisson )  example21 <- glmforest(   x = poisson_model,   data = clintrial,   title = "Poisson Regression: Follow-Up Visits",   labels = clintrial_labels )

title: Forest Plots
text: overdispersed count outcomes (variance > mean), negative binomial regression preferred. Using fit() ensures proper handling:
code: nb_result <- fit(   data = clintrial,   outcome = "ae_count",   predictors = c("age", "treatment", "diabetes", "surgery"),   model_type = "negbin",   labels = clintrial_labels )  example22 <- glmforest(   x = nb_result,   title = "Negative Binomial: Adverse Events" )

title: Forest Plots
text: Ensure factor levels properly defined fitting Use meaningful reference categories Consider centering continuous variables interpretability Check model convergence plotting

title: Forest Plots
text: Use indent_groups = TRUE cleaner presentation categorical variables Match table_width variable name lengths Consider condense_table = TRUE binary predictors Use consistent colors across related figures

title: Forest Plots
text: Use rec_dims attribute optimal sizing Save PDF vector graphics print Use 300+ DPI raster formats Check reference lines confidence intervals clearly visible

title: Forest Plots
text: variable names truncated, increase table_width:
code: p <- glmforest(model, table_width = 0.75)

title: Forest Plots
text: Reduce font size increase figure dimensions:
code: p <- glmforest(model, font_size = 0.9) ggsave("plot.pdf", p, width = 14, height = 8)

title: Forest Plots
text: Ensure labels vector includes variable names:
code: labels <- c(   age = "Age (years)",   sex = "Sex",   stage = "Disease Stage" ) p <- glmforest(model, labels = labels)

title: Forest Plots
text: Descriptive Tables: desctable() baseline characteristics Survival Tables: survtable() time--event summaries Regression Modeling: fit(), uniscreen(), fullfit() Model Comparison: compfit() comparing models Table Export: Export PDF, Word, formats Multivariate Regression: multifit() multi-outcome analysis Advanced Workflows: Interactions mixed-effects models

title: Installation and Setup
text: package yet available CRAN. stable release may installed GitHub: development version available Codeberg:
code: install.packages("remotes") remotes::install_github("phmcc/summata") remotes::install_git("https://codeberg.org/phmcc/summata.git")

title: Installation and Setup
text: Confirm successful installation loading package:
code: library(summata) packageVersion("summata")

title: Installation and Setup
text: following packages installed automatically dependencies:

title: Installation and Setup
text: Additional packages extend functionality specific use-cases: Install optional dependencies needed: install suggested packages :
code: # For mixed-effects models install.packages(c("lme4", "coxme"))  # For table export (all formats) install.packages(c("xtable", "flextable", "officer", "knitr", "tinytex"))  # For enhanced graphics install.packages(c("ragg", "systemfonts"))  # For model diagnostics and selection install.packages(c("MASS", "MuMIn", "pROC", "ResourceSelection")) install.packages("summata", dependencies = TRUE)

title: Installation and Setup
text: PDF LaTeX export table2pdf() table2tex() require LaTeX distribution.

title: Installation and Setup
text: TinyTeX provides lightweight, R-integrated LaTeX distribution: Verify installation: TinyTeX installs required LaTeX packages automatically first use.
code: install.packages("tinytex") tinytex::install_tinytex() tinytex::is_tinytex() tinytex::tlmgr_version()

title: Installation and Setup
text: Full LaTeX distributions may installed system-wide: Debian/Ubuntu systems:
code: sudo apt install texlive-latex-base texlive-latex-extra texlive-fonts-recommended

title: Installation and Setup
text: export functions utilize following LaTeX packages: TinyTeX, packages install automatically. standard distributions:
code: tlmgr install fontenc inputenc array booktabs longtable graphicx geometry \   pdflscape lscape helvet standalone varwidth float caption xcolor colortbl

title: Installation and Setup
text: Export Word, PowerPoint, RTF formats requires: packages enable creation .docx, .pptx, .rtf files can opened edited Microsoft Office compatible applications.
code: install.packages(c("flextable", "officer"))

title: Installation and Setup
text: Execute following verify complete functionality: Successful execution confirms proper configuration export-related dependencies.
code: library(summata)  # Create test data test_data <- data.frame(   Variable = c("Sample Size", "Mean", "Standard Deviation"),   Value = c("150", "45.3", "12.1") )  # Test Word export (requires flextable, officer) table2docx(test_data, file = "verification_test.docx")  # Test PDF export (requires xtable, LaTeX) table2pdf(test_data, file = "verification_test.pdf")  # Clean up file.remove("verification_test.docx", "verification_test.pdf")

title: Installation and Setup
text: PDF generation fails, verify LaTeX accessibility: empty return value indicates LaTeX system PATH. Solutions include: Restart R session LaTeX installation Add LaTeX binary directory PATH manually Reinstall TinyTeX: tinytex::reinstall_tinytex()
code: Sys.which("pdflatex")

title: Installation and Setup
text: TinyTeX, install missing packages directly: compilation errors, examine log file:
code: tinytex::tlmgr_install("package_name") table2pdf(test_data, file = "debug.pdf", show_logs = TRUE)

title: Installation and Setup
text: Ensure repository access configured: packages requiring system libraries (Linux):
code: options(repos = c(CRAN = "https://cloud.r-project.org")) sudo apt install r-base-dev libcurl4-openssl-dev libssl-dev libxml2-dev

title: Installation and Setup
text: Verify write access target directory:
code: getwd() file.access(getwd(), mode = 2)  # Returns 0 if writable

title: Model Comparison
text: examples vignette use clintrial dataset included summata:
code: library(summata)  data(clintrial) data(clintrial_labels)

title: Model Comparison
text: compfit() function uses several metrics comparing models. mathematical details, see Statistical Foundations article.

title: Model Comparison
text: Compare models increasing complexity:
code: example1 <- compfit(   data = clintrial,   outcome = "surgery",   model_list = list(     "Demographics" = c("age", "sex"),     "Plus Stage" = c("age", "sex", "stage", "ecog"),     "Full Model" = c("age", "sex", "stage", "ecog", "treatment", "smoking")   ),   model_type = "glm" )  example1 #>  #> Model Comparison Results #> Outcome: surgery #> Model Type: glm #>  #> CMS Weights: #>   Convergence: 15% #>   AIC: 25% #>   Concordance: 40% #>   Pseudo-R²: 15% #>   Brier score: 5% #>  #> Recommended Model: Full Model (CMS: 81.2) #>  #> Models ranked by selection score: #>           Model   CMS     N Events Predictors Converged    AIC    BIC Pseudo-R² Concordance Brier Score Global p #>          <char> <num> <int>  <num>      <int>    <char>  <num>  <num>     <num>       <num>       <num>   <char> #> 1:   Full Model  81.2   833    362          6       Yes  900.0  961.4     0.234       0.811       0.175  < 0.001 #> 2:   Plus Stage  68.9   842    368          4       Yes  969.9 1012.5     0.175       0.773       0.192  < 0.001 #> 3: Demographics  33.6   850    370          2       Yes 1132.9 1147.1     0.032       0.617       0.235  < 0.001 #>  #> CMS interpretation: 85+ Excellent, 75-84 Very Good, 65-74 Good, 55-64 Fair, < 55 Poor

title: Model Comparison
text: continuous outcomes, use model_type = "lm":
code: example2 <- compfit(   data = clintrial,   outcome = "los_days",   model_list = list(     "Simple" = c("age", "sex"),     "Disease" = c("age", "sex", "stage", "ecog"),     "Treatment" = c("age", "sex", "stage", "ecog", "surgery", "treatment")   ),   model_type = "lm" )  example2 #>  #> Model Comparison Results #> Outcome: los_days #> Model Type: lm #>  #> CMS Weights: #>   Convergence: 15% #>   AIC: 25% #>   Pseudo-R²: 45% #>   RMSE: 15% #>  #> Recommended Model: Treatment (CMS: 72.2) #>  #> Models ranked by selection score: #>        Model   CMS     N Events Predictors Converged    AIC    BIC Pseudo-R² Concordance Global p #>       <char> <num> <int>  <num>      <int>    <char>  <num>  <num>     <num>       <num>   <char> #> 1: Treatment  72.2   822     NA          6       Yes 4297.3 4358.6     0.550          NA  < 0.001 #> 2:   Disease  44.2   822     NA          4       Yes 4670.3 4717.5     0.287          NA  < 0.001 #> 3:    Simple  28.0   830     NA          2       Yes 4873.9 4892.8     0.122          NA  < 0.001 #>  #> CMS interpretation: 85+ Excellent, 75-84 Very Good, 65-74 Good, 55-64 Fair, < 55 Poor

title: Model Comparison
text: time--event outcomes, use model_type = "coxph":
code: example3 <- compfit(   data = clintrial,   outcome = "Surv(os_months, os_status)",   model_list = list(     "Unadjusted" = c("treatment"),     "Demographics" = c("treatment", "age", "sex"),     "Full" = c("treatment", "age", "sex", "stage", "ecog")   ),   model_type = "coxph" )  example3 #>  #> Model Comparison Results #> Outcome: Surv(os_months, os_status) #> Model Type: coxph #>  #> CMS Weights: #>   Convergence: 15% #>   AIC: 30% #>   Concordance: 40% #>   Global p-value: 15% #>  #> Recommended Model: Full (CMS: 75.8) #>  #> Models ranked by selection score: #>           Model   CMS     N Events Predictors Converged    AIC    BIC Pseudo-R² Concordance Global p #>          <char> <num> <int>  <num>      <int>    <char>  <num>  <num>     <num>       <num>   <char> #> 1:         Full  75.8   196    151          5       Yes 7188.4 7232.4     0.283       0.699  < 0.001 #> 2: Demographics  48.0   196    151          3       Yes 7438.4 7456.0     0.126       0.629  < 0.001 #> 3:   Unadjusted  33.8   196    151          1       Yes 7527.1 7535.9     0.026       0.549  < 0.001 #>  #> CMS interpretation: 85+ Excellent, 75-84 Very Good, 65-74 Good, 55-64 Fair, < 55 Poor

title: Model Comparison
text: count outcomes, use model_type = "glm" count model family (e.g., family = "poisson"):
code: example4 <- compfit(   data = clintrial,   outcome = "fu_count",   model_list = list(     "Minimal" = c("age", "treatment"),     "Clinical" = c("age", "treatment", "stage", "ecog"),     "Full" = c("age", "treatment", "stage", "ecog", "surgery", "diabetes")   ),   model_type = "glm",   family = "poisson",   labels = clintrial_labels )  example4 #>  #> Model Comparison Results #> Outcome: fu_count #> Model Type: glm #>  #> CMS Weights: #>   Convergence: 15% #>   AIC: 25% #>   Concordance: 40% #>   Pseudo-R²: 15% #>   Brier score: 5% #>  #> Recommended Model: Full (CMS: 67.5) #>  #> Models ranked by selection score: #>       Model   CMS     N Events Predictors Converged    AIC    BIC Pseudo-R² Concordance Brier Score Global p #>      <char> <num> <int>  <num>      <int>    <char>  <num>  <num>     <num>       <num>       <num>   <char> #> 1:     Full  67.5   826   5410          6       Yes 3956.9 4013.5        NA          NA          NA     <NA> #> 2: Clinical  57.0   834   5482          4       Yes 4009.2 4056.4        NA          NA          NA     <NA> #> 3:  Minimal  42.5   842   5533          2       Yes 4081.4 4100.4        NA          NA          NA     <NA> #>  #> CMS interpretation: 85+ Excellent, 75-84 Very Good, 65-74 Good, 55-64 Fair, < 55 Poor

title: Model Comparison
text: key application compfit() testing whether interaction terms improve model fit.

title: Model Comparison
text: Compare main-effects model one interaction term:
code: example5 <- compfit(   data = clintrial,   outcome = "surgery",   model_list = list(     "Main Effects" = c("age", "treatment", "sex"),     "With Interaction" = c("age", "treatment", "sex")   ),   interactions_list = list(     NULL,     c("sex:treatment")   ),   model_type = "glm" )  example5 #>  #> Model Comparison Results #> Outcome: surgery #> Model Type: glm #>  #> CMS Weights: #>   Convergence: 15% #>   AIC: 25% #>   Concordance: 40% #>   Pseudo-R²: 15% #>   Brier score: 5% #>  #> Recommended Model: Main Effects (CMS: 67.1) #>  #> Models ranked by selection score: #>               Model   CMS     N Events Predictors Converged    AIC    BIC Pseudo-R² Concordance Brier Score Global p #>              <char> <num> <int>  <num>      <int>    <char>  <num>  <num>     <num>       <num>       <num>   <char> #> 1:     Main Effects  67.1   850    370          3       Yes 1071.7 1095.5     0.088       0.697       0.218  < 0.001 #> 2: With Interaction  42.1   850    370          3       Yes 1075.6 1108.8     0.088       0.697       0.218  < 0.001 #>  #> CMS interpretation: 85+ Excellent, 75-84 Very Good, 65-74 Good, 55-64 Fair, < 55 Poor

title: Model Comparison
text: Compare different interaction hypotheses:
code: example6 <- compfit(   data = clintrial,   outcome = "Surv(os_months, os_status)",   model_list = list(     "Main Effects" = c("age", "treatment", "sex", "stage"),     "Age × Treatment" = c("age", "treatment", "sex", "stage"),     "Sex × Treatment" = c("age", "treatment", "sex", "stage"),     "Both" = c("age", "treatment", "sex", "stage")   ),   interactions_list = list(     NULL,     c("age:treatment"),     c("sex:treatment"),     c("age:treatment", "sex:treatment")   ),   model_type = "coxph" )  example6 #>  #> Model Comparison Results #> Outcome: Surv(os_months, os_status) #> Model Type: coxph #>  #> CMS Weights: #>   Convergence: 15% #>   AIC: 30% #>   Concordance: 40% #>   Global p-value: 15% #>  #> Recommended Model: Main Effects (CMS: 74.2) #>  #> Models ranked by selection score: #>              Model   CMS     N Events Predictors Converged    AIC    BIC Pseudo-R² Concordance Global p #>             <char> <num> <int>  <num>      <int>    <char>  <num>  <num>     <num>       <num>   <char> #> 1:    Main Effects  74.2   847    606          4       Yes 7295.8 7326.7     0.232        0.68  < 0.001 #> 2: Age × Treatment  60.2   847    606          4       Yes 7298.8 7338.4     0.232        0.68  < 0.001 #> 3: Sex × Treatment  57.8   847    606          4       Yes 7299.3 7339.0     0.232        0.68  < 0.001 #> 4:            Both  44.3   847    606          4       Yes 7302.2 7350.7     0.233        0.68  < 0.001 #>  #> CMS interpretation: 85+ Excellent, 75-84 Very Good, 65-74 Good, 55-64 Fair, < 55 Poor

title: Model Comparison
text: Setting include_coefficients = TRUE generates table comparing coefficients across models:
code: example7 <- compfit(   data = clintrial,   outcome = "surgery",   model_list = list(     "Model A" = c("age", "sex"),     "Model B" = c("age", "sex", "stage"),     "Model C" = c("age", "sex", "stage", "treatment")   ),   model_type = "glm",   include_coefficients = TRUE,   labels = clintrial_labels )  # Main comparison example7 #>  #> Model Comparison Results #> Outcome: surgery #> Model Type: glm #>  #> CMS Weights: #>   Convergence: 15% #>   AIC: 25% #>   Concordance: 40% #>   Pseudo-R²: 15% #>   Brier score: 5% #>  #> Recommended Model: Model C (CMS: 75.4) #>  #> Models ranked by selection score: #>      Model   CMS     N Events Predictors Converged    AIC    BIC Pseudo-R² Concordance Brier Score Global p #>     <char> <num> <int>  <num>      <int>    <char>  <num>  <num>     <num>       <num>       <num>   <char> #> 1: Model C  75.4   847    368          4       Yes  984.4 1022.4     0.165       0.765       0.195  < 0.001 #> 2: Model B  59.4   847    368          3       Yes 1045.0 1073.5     0.109       0.719       0.211  < 0.001 #> 3: Model A  33.6   850    370          2       Yes 1132.9 1147.1     0.032       0.617       0.235  < 0.001 #>  #> CMS interpretation: 85+ Excellent, 75-84 Very Good, 65-74 Good, 55-64 Fair, < 55 Poor #>  #> Note: Coefficient comparison available via attr(result, 'coefficients')  # Coefficient table coef_table <- attr(example7, "coefficients") coef_table #>       Model        Variable   Group      n Events     aOR (95% CI) p-value #>      <char>          <char>  <char> <char> <char>           <char>  <char> #>  1: Model A     Age (years)       -    850    370 0.96 (0.95-0.98) < 0.001 #>  2: Model A             Sex  Female    450    204        reference       - #>  3: Model A                    Male    400    166 0.85 (0.64-1.12)   0.252 #>  4: Model B     Age (years)       -    847    368 0.96 (0.95-0.97) < 0.001 #>  5: Model B             Sex  Female    449    203        reference       - #>  6: Model B                    Male    398    165 0.79 (0.59-1.06)   0.123 #>  7: Model B   Disease Stage       I    211    125        reference       - #>  8: Model B                      II    263    133 0.68 (0.47-0.99)   0.046 #>  9: Model B                     III    241     91 0.37 (0.25-0.55) < 0.001 #> 10: Model B                      IV    132     19 0.10 (0.06-0.18) < 0.001 #> 11: Model C     Age (years)       -    847    368 0.96 (0.94-0.97) < 0.001 #> 12: Model C             Sex  Female    449    203        reference       - #> 13: Model C                    Male    398    165 0.84 (0.62-1.14)   0.263 #> 14: Model C   Disease Stage       I    211    125        reference       - title: Model Comparison
text: best-performing model identified automatically based Composite Model Score (CMS):
code: example9 <- compfit(   data = clintrial,   outcome = "Surv(os_months, os_status)",   model_list = list(     "Minimal" = c("treatment"),     "Standard" = c("treatment", "age", "sex", "stage"),     "Extended" = c("treatment", "age", "sex", "stage", "ecog", "grade")   ),   model_type = "coxph" )  recommended <- attr(example9, "best_model") cat("Recommended model:", recommended, "\n") #> Recommended model: Extended

title: Model Comparison
text: Composite Model Score (CMS) metric designed facilitate rapid model comparison. combines multiple quality indicators single value ranging 0 100.

title: Model Comparison
text: score components default weights vary model type. Linear regression: Logistic regression: Cox regression:

title: Model Comparison
text: Modify CMS scoring weights emphasize specific metrics:
code: example10 <- compfit(   data = clintrial,   outcome = "surgery",   model_list = list(     "Simple" = c("age", "sex"),     "Standard" = c("age", "sex", "stage"),     "Full" = c("age", "sex", "stage", "treatment", "ecog")   ),   model_type = "glm",   scoring_weights = list(     convergence = 0.05,     aic = 0.20,     concordance = 0.60,     pseudo_r2 = 0.10,     brier = 0.05   ) )  example10 #>  #> Model Comparison Results #> Outcome: surgery #> Model Type: glm #>  #> CMS Weights: #>   Convergence: 5% #>   AIC: 20% #>   Concordance: 60% #>   Pseudo-R²: 10% #>   Brier score: 5% #>  #> Recommended Model: Full (CMS: 79.5) #>  #> Models ranked by selection score: #>       Model   CMS     N Events Predictors Converged    AIC    BIC Pseudo-R² Concordance Brier Score Global p #>      <char> <num> <int>  <num>      <int>    <char>  <num>  <num>     <num>       <num>       <num>   <char> #> 1:     Full  79.5   842    368          5       Yes  904.8  956.9     0.235       0.811       0.175  < 0.001 #> 2: Standard  53.7   847    368          3       Yes 1045.0 1073.5     0.109       0.719       0.211  < 0.001 #> 3:   Simple  31.8   850    370          2       Yes 1132.9 1147.1     0.032       0.617       0.235  < 0.001 #>  #> CMS interpretation: 85+ Excellent, 75-84 Very Good, 65-74 Good, 55-64 Fair, < 55 Poor
