title: Model Comparison
text: Compare automated versus theory-driven selection:
code: # Identify candidates via screening screening <- uniscreen(   data = clintrial,   outcome = "surgery",   predictors = c("age", "sex", "bmi", "smoking", "diabetes",                  "hypertension", "stage", "ecog", "treatment"),   model_type = "glm",   p_threshold = 0.10 )  # Extract significant predictors sig_vars <- attr(screening, "significant")  scenario2 <- compfit(   data = clintrial,   outcome = "surgery",   model_list = list(     "Theory-Driven" = c("age", "sex", "stage", "treatment"),     "Data-Driven" = sig_vars,     "Combined" = unique(c("age", "sex", "stage", "treatment", sig_vars))   ),   model_type = "glm" )  scenario2 #>  #> Model Comparison Results #> Outcome: surgery #> Model Type: glm #>  #> CMS Weights: #>   Convergence: 15% #>   AIC: 25% #>   Concordance: 40% #>   Pseudo-R²: 15% #>   Brier score: 5% #>  #> Recommended Model: Data-Driven (CMS: 81.3) #>  #> Models ranked by selection score: #>            Model   CMS     N Events Predictors Converged   AIC    BIC Pseudo-R² Concordance Brier Score Global p #>           <char> <num> <int>  <num>      <int>    <char> <num>  <num>     <num>       <num>       <num>   <char> #> 1:   Data-Driven  81.3   842    368          4       Yes 903.2  950.5     0.235       0.811       0.176  < 0.001 #> 2:      Combined  80.8   842    368          5       Yes 904.8  956.9     0.235       0.811       0.175  < 0.001 #> 3: Theory-Driven  50.4   847    368          4       Yes 984.4 1022.4     0.165       0.765       0.195  < 0.001 #>  #> CMS interpretation: 85+ Excellent, 75-84 Very Good, 65-74 Good, 55-64 Fair, < 55 Poor

title: Model Comparison
text: Test whether additional predictors meaningfully improve fit:
code: scenario3 <- compfit(   data = clintrial,   outcome = "los_days",   model_list = list(     "3 Predictors" = c("age", "surgery", "ecog"),     "5 Predictors" = c("age", "surgery", "ecog", "stage", "treatment"),     "8 Predictors" = c("age", "surgery", "ecog", "stage", "treatment",                        "sex", "smoking", "diabetes")   ),   model_type = "lm",   labels = clintrial_labels )  scenario3 #>  #> Model Comparison Results #> Outcome: los_days #> Model Type: lm #>  #> CMS Weights: #>   Convergence: 15% #>   AIC: 25% #>   Pseudo-R²: 45% #>   RMSE: 15% #>  #> Recommended Model: 8 Predictors (CMS: 75.1) #>  #> Models ranked by selection score: #>           Model   CMS     N Events Predictors Converged    AIC    BIC Pseudo-R² Concordance Global p #>          <char> <num> <int>  <num>      <int>    <char>  <num>  <num>     <num>       <num>   <char> #> 1: 8 Predictors  75.1   813     NA          8       Yes 4135.8 4211.0     0.613          NA  < 0.001 #> 2: 5 Predictors  64.1   822     NA          5       Yes 4310.6 4367.1     0.542          NA  < 0.001 #> 3: 3 Predictors  34.1   822     NA          3       Yes 4696.4 4729.4     0.258          NA  < 0.001 #>  #> CMS interpretation: 85+ Excellent, 75-84 Very Good, 65-74 Good, 55-64 Fair, < 55 Poor

title: Model Comparison
text: Comparison tables can exported various formats:
code: # Main comparison table table2docx(   table = example1,   file = "Model_Comparison.docx",   caption = "Table 3. Model Comparison Results" )  # Coefficient table table2docx(   table = attr(example6, "coefficients"),   file = "Coefficient_Comparison.docx",   caption = "Table S1. Coefficient Estimates Across Models" )  # PDF with landscape orientation for wide tables table2pdf(   table = example1,   file = "Model_Comparison.pdf",   caption = "Model Comparison",   orientation = "landscape" )

title: Model Comparison
text: Covariate selection: Determine appropriate adjustment level Interaction testing: Evaluate effect modification Parsimony: Balance complexity fit Sensitivity analysis: Compare different specifications

title: Model Comparison
text: Multiple metrics: Composite Model Score (CMS) provides summary, examine individual metrics Practical significance: Statistical improvement may translate meaningful differences Sample size: Complex models require larger samples Convergence: Non-converged models interpreted cautiously

title: Model Comparison
text: Composite Model Score (CMS) heuristic, formal statistical test Comparisons assume models fit identical observations Information criteria meaningful nested models Small score differences may practically significant

title: Model Comparison
text: Check convergence status consider simplifying non-converging models: encountering non-converging models, consider reducing complexity, examining predictor correlations, checking perfect separation (primarily logistic models).
code: # Check convergence status comparison[, .(Model, Converged)]  # For non-converging models: # 1. Reduce complexity # 2. Check for separation (logistic) # 3. Examine predictor correlations

title: Model Comparison
text: Models missing data may different sample sizes, complicates comparison:
code: # Check sample sizes comparison[, .(Model, N, Events)]  # Use complete cases for fair comparison complete_data <- na.omit(data[, relevant_vars, with = FALSE])

title: Model Comparison
text: scores similar, prefer parsimony consider domain knowledge:
code: # Examine individual metrics comparison[, .(Model, `Composite Model Score (CMS)`, AIC, Concordance)]  # Prefer parsimony when scores are close # Consider interpretability

title: Model Comparison
text: Statistical Foundations: Mathematical details model quality metrics Descriptive Tables: desctable() baseline characteristics Regression Modeling: fit(), uniscreen(), fullfit() Table Export: Export PDF, Word, formats Forest Plots: Visualization regression results Multivariate Regression: multifit() multi-outcome analysis Advanced Workflows: Interactions mixed-effects models

title: Multivariate Regression
text: examples vignette use clintrial dataset included summata: clintrial dataset includes multiple outcome types suitable multivariate analysis:
code: library(summata) library(survival) library(ggplot2)  data(clintrial) data(clintrial_labels)

title: Multivariate Regression
text: Test single predictor multiple binary outcomes: row shows effect predictor specific outcome. categorical predictors, non-reference level appears separately.
code: example1 <- multifit(   data = clintrial,   outcomes = c("any_complication", "wound_infection",                "readmission_30d", "icu_admission"),   predictor = "surgery",   labels = clintrial_labels,   parallel = FALSE )  example1 ##>  ##> Multivariate Analysis Results ##> Predictor: surgery ##> Outcomes: 4 ##> Model Type: glm ##> Display: unadjusted ##>  ##>                   Outcome          Predictor      n Events      OR (95% CI) p-value ##>                    <char>             <char> <char> <char>           <char>  <char> ##> 1:       Any Complication Surgical Resection    370    236 1.70 (1.29-2.25) < 0.001 ##> 2:   Wound/Site Infection Surgical Resection    370    121 4.58 (3.16-6.66) < 0.001 ##> 3:     30-Day Readmission Surgical Resection    370    181 0.91 (0.69-1.19)   0.500 ##> 4: ICU Admission Required Surgical Resection    370    179 2.25 (1.70-2.99) < 0.001

title: Multivariate Regression
text: Include covariates confounding control:
code: example2 <- multifit(   data = clintrial,   outcomes = c("any_complication", "wound_infection",                "readmission_30d", "icu_admission"),   predictor = "surgery",   covariates = c("age", "sex", "smoking", "diabetes"),   labels = clintrial_labels,   parallel = FALSE )  example2 ##>  ##> Multivariate Analysis Results ##> Predictor: surgery ##> Outcomes: 4 ##> Model Type: glm ##> Covariates: age, sex, smoking, diabetes ##> Display: adjusted ##>  ##>                   Outcome          Predictor      n Events     aOR (95% CI) p-value ##>                    <char>             <char> <char> <char>           <char>  <char> ##> 1:       Any Complication Surgical Resection    362    229 1.84 (1.38-2.47) < 0.001 ##> 2:   Wound/Site Infection Surgical Resection    362    117 5.21 (3.48-7.80) < 0.001 ##> 3:     30-Day Readmission Surgical Resection    362    176 1.07 (0.80-1.43)   0.641 ##> 4: ICU Admission Required Surgical Resection    362    173 2.56 (1.90-3.46) < 0.001

title: Multivariate Regression
text: Use columns = "" display unadjusted adjusted results side--side: Comparing columns reveals confounding (large differences) robust associations (similar estimates).
code: example3 <- multifit(   data = clintrial,   outcomes = c("any_complication", "wound_infection",                "readmission_30d", "icu_admission"),   predictor = "surgery",   covariates = c("age", "sex", "diabetes", "surgery"),   columns = "both",   labels = clintrial_labels,   parallel = FALSE )  example3 ##>  ##> Multivariate Analysis Results ##> Predictor: surgery ##> Outcomes: 4 ##> Model Type: glm ##> Covariates: age, sex, diabetes, surgery ##> Display: both ##>  ##>                   Outcome          Predictor      n Events      OR (95% CI)   Uni p     aOR (95% CI) Multi p ##>                    <char>             <char> <char> <char>           <char>  <char>           <char>  <char> ##> 1:       Any Complication Surgical Resection    370    236 1.70 (1.29-2.25) < 0.001 1.82 (1.36-2.43) < 0.001 ##> 2: ICU Admission Required Surgical Resection    370    179 2.25 (1.70-2.99) < 0.001 2.57 (1.90-3.47) < 0.001 ##> 3:     30-Day Readmission Surgical Resection    370    181 0.91 (0.69-1.19)   0.500 1.05 (0.79-1.40)   0.720 ##> 4:   Wound/Site Infection Surgical Resection    370    121 4.58 (3.16-6.66) < 0.001 4.98 (3.35-7.40) < 0.001

title: Multivariate Regression
text: continuous predictors, one row appears per outcome: effect estimate represents change log-odds per one-unit increase predictor.
code: example4 <- multifit(   data = clintrial,   outcomes = c("any_complication", "wound_infection", "icu_admission"),   predictor = "age",   covariates = c("sex", "treatment", "surgery"),   labels = clintrial_labels,   parallel = FALSE )  example4 ##>  ##> Multivariate Analysis Results ##> Predictor: age ##> Outcomes: 3 ##> Model Type: glm ##> Covariates: sex, treatment, surgery ##> Display: adjusted ##>  ##>                   Outcome      n Events     aOR (95% CI) p-value ##>                    <char> <char> <char>           <char>  <char> ##> 1:       Any Complication    850    480 1.01 (1.00-1.03)   0.024 ##> 2:   Wound/Site Infection    850    167 1.01 (0.99-1.02)   0.338 ##> 3: ICU Admission Required    850    320 1.03 (1.02-1.04) < 0.001

text: categorical predictors multiple levels, display expanded:

title: Multivariate Regression
text: time--event outcomes, use model_type = "coxph":
code: example6 <- multifit(   data = clintrial,   outcomes = c("Surv(pfs_months, pfs_status)",                "Surv(os_months, os_status)"),   predictor = "treatment",   covariates = c("age", "sex", "stage"),   model_type = "coxph",   labels = clintrial_labels,   parallel = FALSE )  example6 ##>  ##> Multivariate Analysis Results ##> Predictor: treatment ##> Outcomes: 2 ##> Model Type: coxph ##> Covariates: age, sex, stage ##> Display: adjusted ##>  ##>                               Outcome                Predictor      n Events     aHR (95% CI) p-value ##>                                <char>                   <char> <char> <char>           <char>  <char> ##> 1: Progression-Free Survival (months) Treatment Group (Drug A)    721    721 0.54 (0.44-0.66) < 0.001 ##> 2: Progression-Free Survival (months) Treatment Group (Drug B)    721    721 0.82 (0.68-0.98)   0.033 ##> 3:          Overall Survival (months) Treatment Group (Drug A)    606    606 0.56 (0.45-0.70) < 0.001 ##> 4:          Overall Survival (months) Treatment Group (Drug B)    606    606 0.83 (0.67-1.01)   0.062

Coefficient (95% CI) p-value ##>                             <char>                   <char> <char>                    <char>  <char> ##> 1:  Length of Hospital Stay (days) Treatment Group (Drug A)    288    -1.07 (-1.83 to -0.32)   0.006 ##> 2:  Length of Hospital Stay (days) Treatment Group (Drug B)    350       3.10 (2.36 to 3.83) < 0.001 ##> 3: Postoperative Pain Score (0-10) Treatment Group (Drug A)    289    -0.56 (-0.84 to -0.27) < 0.001 ##> 4: Postoperative Pain Score (0-10) Treatment Group (Drug B)    359       0.86 (0.59 to 1.13) < 0.001 ##> 5:     Days to Functional Recovery Treatment Group (Drug A)    288    -1.73 (-2.96 to -0.50)   0.006 ##> 6:     Days to Functional Recovery Treatment Group (Drug B)    352       2.79 (1.60 to 3.98) < 0.001

title: Multivariate Regression
text: Account clustering using random effects:
code: example8 <- multifit(   data = clintrial,   outcomes = c("any_complication", "wound_infection"),   predictor = "treatment",   covariates = c("age", "sex"),   random = "(1|site)",   model_type = "glmer",   labels = clintrial_labels,   parallel = FALSE )  example8 ##>  ##> Multivariate Analysis Results ##> Predictor: treatment ##> Outcomes: 2 ##> Model Type: glmer ##> Covariates: age, sex ##> Random Effects: (1|site) ##> Display: adjusted ##>  ##>                 Outcome                Predictor      n Events     aOR (95% CI) p-value ##>                  <char>                   <char> <char> <char>           <char>  <char> ##> 1:     Any Complication Treatment Group (Drug A)    292    143 0.74 (0.51-1.06)   0.104 ##> 2:     Any Complication Treatment Group (Drug B)    362    226 1.29 (0.90-1.84)   0.170 ##> 3: Wound/Site Infection Treatment Group (Drug A)    292     72 2.17 (1.32-3.57)   0.002 ##> 4: Wound/Site Infection Treatment Group (Drug B)    362     69 1.62 (0.99-2.66)   0.055

title: Multivariate Regression
text: Interaction terms can added test effect modification:
code: example9 <- multifit(   data = clintrial,   outcomes = c("any_complication", "wound_infection"),   predictor = "treatment",   covariates = c("age", "sex"),   interactions = c("treatment:sex"),   labels = clintrial_labels,   parallel = FALSE )  example9 ##>  ##> Multivariate Analysis Results ##> Predictor: treatment ##> Outcomes: 2 ##> Model Type: glm ##> Covariates: age, sex ##> Interactions: treatment:sex ##> Display: adjusted ##>  ##>                 Outcome                             Predictor      n Events     aOR (95% CI) p-value ##>                  <char>                                <char> <char> <char>           <char>  <char> ##> 1:     Any Complication              Treatment Group (Drug A)    292    143 0.63 (0.38-1.04)   0.074 ##> 2:     Any Complication              Treatment Group (Drug B)    362    226 1.27 (0.78-2.08)   0.340 ##> 3:     Any Complication Treatment Group (Drug A) × Sex (Male)      -      - 1.41 (0.68-2.93)   0.360 ##> 4:     Any Complication Treatment Group (Drug B) × Sex (Male)      -      - 0.99 (0.48-2.00)   0.968 ##> 5: Wound/Site Infection              Treatment Group (Drug A)    292     72 2.20 (1.13-4.28)   0.020 ##> 6: Wound/Site Infection              Treatment Group (Drug B)    362     69 1.30 (0.66-2.57)   0.455 ##> 7: Wound/Site Infection Treatment Group (Drug A) × Sex (Male)      -      - 0.93 (0.35-2.50)   0.884 ##> 8: Wound/Site Infection Treatment Group (Drug B) × Sex (Male)      -      - 1.43 (0.54-3.83)   0.472

title: Multivariate Regression
text: Outputs can filtered retain significant associations:
code: example10 <- multifit(   data = clintrial,   outcomes = c("any_complication", "wound_infection",                "readmission_30d", "icu_admission"),   predictor = "treatment",   covariates = c("age", "sex", "surgery"),   p_threshold = 0.01,   labels = clintrial_labels,   parallel = FALSE )  example10 ##>  ##> Multivariate Analysis Results ##> Predictor: treatment ##> Outcomes: 4 ##> Model Type: glm ##> Covariates: age, sex, surgery ##> Display: adjusted ##>  ##>                   Outcome                Predictor      n Events     aOR (95% CI) p-value ##>                    <char>                   <char> <char> <char>           <char>  <char> ##> 1:   Wound/Site Infection Treatment Group (Drug B)    362     69 2.26 (1.34-3.79)   0.002 ##> 2:     30-Day Readmission Treatment Group (Drug B)    362    209 1.61 (1.12-2.31)   0.010 ##> 3: ICU Admission Required Treatment Group (Drug B)    362    145 1.70 (1.16-2.50)   0.007

title: Multivariate Regression
text: Underlying model objects stored attributes additional analysis:
code: result <- multifit(   data = clintrial,   outcomes = c("any_complication", "wound_infection"),   predictor = "treatment",   covariates = c("age", "sex"),   labels = clintrial_labels,   keep_models = TRUE,   parallel = FALSE )  # Access individual models models <- attr(result, "models") names(models) ##> [1] "any_complication" "wound_infection"  # Examine a specific model summary(models[["any_complication"]]) ##>          Length Class Mode ##> adjusted 30     glm   list

title: Multivariate Regression
text: multiforest() function creates forest plots multifit() results.

what: Example 12: Forest Plot for Logistic (Binary) Outcomes
title: Multivariate Regression
text: Results multifit() can directly inserted multiforest() function generate forest plot:
code: result <- multifit(   data = clintrial,   outcomes = c("any_complication", "wound_infection",                "readmission_30d", "icu_admission"),   predictor = "treatment",   covariates = c("age", "sex", "diabetes", "surgery"),   labels = clintrial_labels,   parallel = FALSE )  example12 <- multiforest(   result,   title = "Treatment Effects Across Outcomes",   indent_predictor = TRUE,   zebra_stripes = TRUE )

title: Multivariate Regression
text: Customize appearance forest plot adjusting function parameters:
code: example13 <- multiforest(   result,   title = "Effect Estimates",   column = "adjusted",   show_predictor = FALSE,   covariates_footer = TRUE,   table_width = 0.65,   color = "#4BA6B6" )

what: Example 14: Forest Plot for Continuous Outcomes
title: Multivariate Regression
text: Create forest plots continuous outcomes:
code: lm_result <- multifit(   data = clintrial,   outcomes = c("pain_score", "recovery_days", "los_days"),   predictor = "treatment",   covariates = c("age", "sex", "surgery"),   model_type = "lm",   parallel = FALSE )  example14 <- multiforest(   lm_result,   title = "Treatment Effects on Recovery Metrics",   show_predictor = FALSE,   covariates_footer = TRUE,   labels = clintrial_labels )

what: Example 15: Forest Plot for Survival Outcomes
title: Multivariate Regression
text: multiforest() function supports multiple model_type outputs multifit(). addition, variable labels can applied multiforest() outputs similarly forest plot functions:
code: cox_result <- multifit(   data = clintrial,   outcomes = c("Surv(pfs_months, pfs_status)",                "Surv(os_months, os_status)"),   predictor = "treatment",   covariates = c("age", "sex", "stage"),   model_type = "coxph",   parallel = FALSE )  example15 <- multiforest(   cox_result,   title = "Treatment Effects on Survival Outcomes",   indent_predictor = TRUE,   zebra_stripes = TRUE,   labels = clintrial_labels )

title: Multivariate Regression
text: Export tables using standard export functions:
code: table2docx(   table = result,   file = "multioutcome_analysis.docx",   caption = "Treatment Effects Across Outcomes" )  table2pdf(   table = result,   file = "multioutcome_analysis.pdf",   caption = "Treatment Effects Across Outcomes" )

title: Multivariate Regression
text: Save forest plots using ggsave():
code: p <- multiforest(result, title = "Effect Estimates") dims <- attr(p, "rec_dims")  ggsave("multioutcome_forest.pdf", p,        width = attr(result, "rec_dims")$width,        height = attr(result, "rec_dims")$height,         units = "in")

title: Multivariate Regression
text: following demonstrates complete multi-outcome analysis workflow:
code: ## Define outcomes by type binary_outcomes <- c("any_complication", "wound_infection",                      "readmission_30d", "icu_admission") survival_outcomes <- c("Surv(pfs_months, pfs_status)",                        "Surv(os_months, os_status)")  ## Unadjusted screening unadjusted <- multifit(   data = clintrial,   outcomes = binary_outcomes,   predictor = "treatment",   labels = clintrial_labels,   parallel = FALSE )  unadjusted ##>  ##> Multivariate Analysis Results ##> Predictor: treatment ##> Outcomes: 4 ##> Model Type: glm ##> Display: unadjusted ##>  ##>                   Outcome                Predictor      n Events      OR (95% CI) p-value ##>                    <char>                   <char> <char> <char>           <char>  <char> ##> 1:       Any Complication Treatment Group (Drug A)    292    143 0.73 (0.51-1.06)   0.097 ##> 2:       Any Complication Treatment Group (Drug B)    362    226 1.27 (0.89-1.81)   0.182 ##> 3:   Wound/Site Infection Treatment Group (Drug A)    292     72 2.14 (1.31-3.50)   0.002 ##> 4:   Wound/Site Infection Treatment Group (Drug B)    362     69 1.54 (0.94-2.51)   0.084 ##> 5:     30-Day Readmission Treatment Group (Drug A)    292    127 0.89 (0.62-1.28)   0.523 ##> 6:     30-Day Readmission Treatment Group (Drug B)    362    209 1.58 (1.11-2.24)   0.011 ##> 7: ICU Admission Required Treatment Group (Drug A)    292    111 1.26 (0.86-1.85)   0.226 ##> 8: ICU Admission Required Treatment Group (Drug B)    362    145 1.38 (0.96-1.99)   0.085  ## Adjusted analysis with comparison adjusted <- multifit(   data = clintrial,   outcomes = binary_outcomes,   predictor = "treatment",   covariates = c("age", "sex", "diabetes", "surgery"),   columns = "both",   labels = clintrial_labels,   parallel = FALSE )  adjusted ##>  ##> Multivariate Analysis Results ##> Predictor: treatment ##> Outcomes: 4 ##> Model Type: glm ##> Covariates: age, sex, diabetes, surgery ##> Display: both ##>  ##>                   Outcome                Predictor      n Events      OR (95% CI)  Uni p     aOR (95% CI) Multi p ##>                    <char>                   <char> <char> <char>           <char> <char>           <char>  <char> ##> 1:       Any Complication Treatment Group (Drug A)    292    143 0.73 (0.51-1.06)  0.097 0.62 (0.43-0.92)   0.016 ##> 2:       Any Complication Treatment Group (Drug B)    362    226 1.27 (0.89-1.81)  0.182 1.44 (0.99-2.09)   0.057 ##> 3: ICU Admission Required Treatment Group (Drug A)    292    111 1.26 (0.86-1.85)  0.226 1.11 (0.74-1.67)   0.599 ##> 4: ICU Admission Required Treatment Group (Drug B)    362    145 1.38 (0.96-1.99)  0.085 1.75 (1.18-2.60)   0.005 ##> 5:     30-Day Readmission Treatment Group (Drug A)    292    127 0.89 (0.62-1.28)  0.523 0.86 (0.59-1.25)   0.427 ##> 6:     30-Day Readmission Treatment Group (Drug B)    362    209 1.58 (1.11-2.24)  0.011 1.56 (1.08-2.25)   0.018 ##> 7:   Wound/Site Infection Treatment Group (Drug A)    292     72 2.14 (1.31-3.50)  0.002 1.86 (1.10-3.15)   0.021 ##> 8:   Wound/Site Infection Treatment Group (Drug B)    362     69 1.54 (0.94-2.51)  0.084 2.22 (1.30-3.81)   0.004  ## Forest plot visualization forest_plot <- multiforest(   adjusted,   title = "Treatment Effect Estimates",   column = "adjusted",   indent_predictor = TRUE,   zebra_stripes = TRUE,   table_width = 0.65,   labels = clintrial_labels )

title: Multivariate Regression
text: Use compatible outcome types within single analysis Group conceptually related outcomes Consider multiple testing adjustments testing many outcomes

title: Multivariate Regression
text: Pre-specify covariates based domain knowledge Use columns = "" assess confounding Apply consistent covariates across outcomes comparability

title: Multivariate Regression
text: Focus effect magnitude precision, p-values Look consistent patterns across related outcomes Consider practical significance alongside statistical significance

title: Multivariate Regression
text: results empty, verify: Predictor variable exists variation Outcomes correctly specified (Surv() syntax survival) Model type matches outcome type

title: Multivariate Regression
text: mixed-effects models, simplify random effects structure:
code: ## Start with random intercepts only multifit(data, outcomes, predictor,          random = "(1|site)",          model_type = "glmer")

title: Multivariate Regression
text: predictors many levels, consider collapsing categories:
code: clintrial$treatment_binary <- ifelse(clintrial$treatment == "Control",                                        "Control", "Active")

title: Multivariate Regression
text: distinction multifit() uniscreen() important: outcomes single multifit() call type (binary, continuous, survival). Mixing outcome types produces tables incompatible effect measures. function validates outcome compatibility issues warning mixed types detected.

what: Categorical Outcomes with More Than Two Levels
title: Multivariate Regression
text: multifit() supports binary outcomes (via logistic regression) multinomial ordinal outcomes. categorical outcome three levels included (e.g., treatment group “Control”, “Drug ”, “Drug B”), function issue warning binomial GLM coerces variables binary (first level vs. others). multilevel categorical outcomes, use dedicated packages outside summata:
code: ## Multinomial regression (unordered categories) library(nnet) model <- multinom(treatment ~ age + sex + stage, data = clintrial)  ## Ordinal regression (ordered categories) library(MASS) model <- polr(grade ~ age + sex + stage, data = clintrial, Hess = TRUE)

title: Multivariate Regression
text: Statistical Foundations: Mathematical details model types Descriptive Tables: desctable() baseline characteristics Regression Modeling: fit(), uniscreen(), fullfit() Model Comparison: compfit() comparing models Table Export: Export PDF, Word, formats Forest Plots: Visualization regression results Advanced Workflows: Interactions mixed-effects models

title: Regression Modeling
text: examples vignette use clintrial dataset included summata: clintrial dataset simulates multisite oncology clinical trial realistic outcomes site-level clustering. Variables suitable different regression approaches include:
code: library(summata) library(survival)  data(clintrial) data(clintrial_labels)

title: Regression Modeling
text: following table summarizes supported model types (model_type) families/links (family). estimates, effect measures displayed default; set exponentiate = FALSE display raw coefficients.

title: Regression Modeling
text: uniscreen() function fits separate regression models predictor, combining results single table. approach identifies candidate predictors multivariable modeling.

title: Regression Modeling
text: binary outcomes, use model_type = "glm" (logistic regression default family). examine predictors 30-day hospital readmission: entry represents separate univariable model. categorical predictors, non-reference level appears separate row.

Error z value Pr(>|z|)     #> (Intercept) -1.780821   0.369774  -4.816 1.46e-06 *** #> age          0.029855   0.006056   4.930 8.22e-07 *** #> --- #> Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1 #>  #> (Dispersion parameter for binomial family taken to be 1) #>  #>     Null deviance: 1178.3  on 849  degrees of freedom #> Residual deviance: 1152.9  on 848  degrees of freedom #> AIC: 1156.9 #>  #> Number of Fisher Scoring iterations: 4

title: Regression Modeling
text: fit() function estimates single regression model multiple predictors, producing adjusted effect estimates. effectively wrapper existing model functions R supported packages enforces summata syntax consistent function calling.

title: Regression Modeling
text: time--event outcomes:
code: example7 <- fit(   data = clintrial,   outcome = "Surv(os_months, os_status)",   predictors = c("age", "sex", "treatment", "stage"),   model_type = "coxph",   labels = clintrial_labels )  example7 #>  #> Multivariable Cox PH Model #> Formula: Surv(os_months, os_status) ~ age + sex + treatment + stage #> n = 847, Events = 606 #>  #>            Variable   Group      n Events     aHR (95% CI) p-value #>              <char>  <char> <char> <char>           <char>  <char> #>  1:     Age (years)       -    847    606 1.04 (1.03-1.04) < 0.001 #>  2:             Sex  Female    450    298        reference       - #>  3:                    Male    400    311 1.33 (1.13-1.56) < 0.001 #>  4: Treatment Group Control    196    151        reference       - #>  5:                  Drug A    292    184 0.56 (0.45-0.70) < 0.001 #>  6:                  Drug B    362    274 0.83 (0.67-1.01)   0.062 #>  7:   Disease Stage       I    211    127        reference       - #>  8:                      II    263    172 1.16 (0.92-1.46)   0.214 #>  9:                     III    241    186 1.90 (1.51-2.38) < 0.001 #> 10:                      IV    132    121 3.57 (2.77-4.60) < 0.001

title: Regression Modeling
text: count outcomes variance approximately equals mean, use model_type = "glm" family = "poisson". fu_count variable represents number follow-clinic visits, equidispersed (suitable standard Poisson): Output displays rate ratios (RR). rate ratio 1.10 indicates 10% higher event rate compared reference group. overdispersed counts (variance > mean), consider negative binomial quasipoisson regression instead (see Example 18).
code: example9 <- fit(   data = clintrial,   outcome = "fu_count",   predictors = c("age", "stage", "treatment", "surgery"),   model_type = "glm",   family = "poisson",   labels = clintrial_labels )  example9 #>  #> Multivariable Poisson Model #> Formula: fu_count ~ age + stage + treatment + surgery #> n = 839, Events = 5517 #>  #>               Variable   Group      n Events     aRR (95% CI) p-value #>                 <char>  <char> <char> <char>           <char>  <char> #>  1:        Age (years)       -    839  5,517 1.00 (1.00-1.00)   0.038 #>  2:      Disease Stage       I    207  1,238        reference       - #>  3:                         II    261  1,638 1.05 (0.98-1.13)   0.169 #>  4:                        III    240  1,638 1.14 (1.06-1.23) < 0.001 #>  5:                         IV    131  1,003 1.33 (1.21-1.45) < 0.001 #>  6:    Treatment Group Control    191  1,169        reference       - #>  7:                     Drug A    290  1,910 1.06 (0.99-1.14)   0.101 #>  8:                     Drug B    358  2,438 1.12 (1.04-1.20)   0.002 #>  9: Surgical Resection      No    476  3,091        reference       - #> 10:                        Yes    363  2,426 1.09 (1.03-1.16)   0.003

what: Example 10: Toggling Reference Rows for Factors
title: Regression Modeling
text: default, reference rows shown output tables (reference_rows = TRUE). Setting parameter FALSE removes reference rows:
code: example10 <- fit(   data = clintrial,   outcome = "readmission_30d",   predictors = c("sex", "stage", "treatment"),   model_type = "glm",   reference_rows = FALSE,   labels = clintrial_labels )  example10 #>  #> Multivariable Logistic Model #> Formula: readmission_30d ~ sex + stage + treatment #> n = 398, Events = 216 #>  #>           Variable  Group      n Events     aOR (95% CI) p-value #>             <char> <char> <char> <char>           <char>  <char> #> 1:             Sex   Male    398    216 1.38 (1.05-1.82)   0.022 #> 2:   Disease Stage     II    263    126 1.23 (0.85-1.78)   0.270 #> 3:                    III    241    133 1.59 (1.09-2.33)   0.016 #> 4:                     IV    132     77 1.91 (1.22-2.99)   0.004 #> 5: Treatment Group Drug A    292    127 0.88 (0.61-1.28)   0.510 #> 6:                 Drug B    361    208 1.50 (1.05-2.14)   0.027

title: Regression Modeling

code: example11 <- fit(   data = clintrial,   outcome = "readmission_30d",   predictors = c("age", "sex", "stage"),   model_type = "glm",   conf_level = 0.90 )  example11 #>  #> Multivariable Logistic Model #> Formula: readmission_30d ~ age + sex + stage #> n = 847, Events = 425 #>  #>    Variable  Group      n Events     aOR (90% CI) p-value #>      <char> <char> <char> <char>           <char>  <char> #> 1:      age      -    847    425 1.03 (1.02-1.04) < 0.001 #> 2:      sex Female    449    209        reference       - #> 3:            Male    398    216 1.41 (1.12-1.79)   0.014 #> 4:    stage      I    211     89        reference       - #> 5:              II    263    126 1.28 (0.94-1.75)   0.196 #> 6:             III    241    133 1.83 (1.32-2.51)   0.002 #> 7:              IV    132     77 2.03 (1.39-2.96)   0.002

title: Regression Modeling

code: example12 <- fit(   data = clintrial,   outcome = "readmission_30d",   predictors = c("age", "sex", "stage"),   model_type = "glm",   exponentiate = FALSE )  example12 #>  #> Multivariable Logistic Model #> Formula: readmission_30d ~ age + sex + stage #> n = 847, Events = 425 #>  #>    Variable  Group      n Events Adj. Coefficient (95% CI) p-value #>      <char> <char> <char> <char>                    <char>  <char> #> 1:      age      -    847    425       0.03 (0.02 to 0.04) < 0.001 #> 2:      sex Female    449    209                 reference       - #> 3:            Male    398    216       0.35 (0.07 to 0.62)   0.014 #> 4:    stage      I    211     89                 reference       - #> 5:              II    263    126      0.25 (-0.13 to 0.62)   0.196 #> 6:             III    241    133       0.60 (0.22 to 0.98)   0.002 #> 7:              IV    132     77       0.71 (0.26 to 1.16)   0.002

title: Regression Modeling
text: fullfit() function integrates univariable screening multivariable modeling single workflow, producing combined table unadjusted adjusted estimates. function extends fit() additional parameter (method) variable selection: method parameter, following options available. Setting method one following options controls predictors entering multivariable model:
code: fullfit(data, outcome, predictors, model_type, method, ...)

title: Regression Modeling
text: default method = "screen" approach specifies predictors univariable p-value p_threshold subsequently used multivariable analysis:
code: example13 <- fullfit(   data = clintrial,   outcome = "readmission_30d",   predictors = c("age", "sex", "bmi", "smoking", "diabetes",                  "stage", "treatment"),   model_type = "glm",   method = "screen",   p_threshold = 0.05,   labels = clintrial_labels )  example13 #>  #> Fullfit Analysis Results #> Outcome: readmission_30d #> Model Type: glm #> Method: screen (p < 0.05) #> Predictors Screened: 7 #> Multivariable Predictors: 6 #>  #>                    Variable   Group      n Events      OR (95% CI)   Uni p     aOR (95% CI) Multi p #>                      <char>  <char> <char> <char>           <char>  <char>           <char>  <char> #>  1:             Age (years)       - title: Regression Modeling
text: summata package supports several additional model types specialized analyses. require external packages suggested dependencies.

title: Regression Modeling
text: Negative binomial models appropriate overdispersed count data variance exceeds mean—common violation Poisson assumption. model type requires MASS package. ae_count variable clintrial generated overdispersion, making ideal demonstration.
code: example18 <- fit(   data = clintrial,   outcome = "ae_count",   predictors = c("age", "sex", "diabetes", "treatment"),   model_type = "negbin",   labels = clintrial_labels )  example18 #>  #> Multivariable Negative Binomial Model #> Formula: ae_count ~ age + sex + diabetes + treatment #> n = 824, Events = 4506 #>  #>           Variable   Group      n Events     aRR (95% CI) p-value #>             <char>  <char> <char> <char>           <char>  <char> #> 1:     Age (years)       -    824  4,506 1.01 (1.01-1.01) < 0.001 #> 2:             Sex  Female    440  2,248        reference       - #> 3:                    Male    384  2,258 1.10 (0.99-1.23)   0.072 #> 4:        Diabetes      No    630  2,998        reference       - #> 5:                     Yes    194  1,508 1.56 (1.38-1.76) < 0.001 #> 6: Treatment Group Control    189    826        reference       - #> 7:                  Drug A    287  1,221 0.96 (0.83-1.12)   0.635 #> 8:                  Drug B    348  2,459 1.52 (1.32-1.75) < 0.001

title: Regression Modeling
text: Gamma regression appropriate positive, continuous, right-skewed outcomes. clintrial dataset, length hospital stay (los_days) fits description. Note default link Gamma inverse link, produces coefficients difficult--interpret scale. log link generally preferred interpretability—exponentiated coefficients represent multiplicative effects mean.
code: example19 <- fit(   data = clintrial,   outcome = "los_days",   predictors = c("age", "ecog", "stage", "treatment"),   model_type = "glm",   family = Gamma(link = "log"),   labels = clintrial_labels )  example19 #>  #> Multivariable Gamma Model #> Formula: los_days ~ age + ecog + stage + treatment #> n = 822 #>  #>                    Variable   Group      n Adj. Coefficient (95% CI) p-value #>                      <char>  <char> <char>                    <char>  <char> #>  1:             Age (years)       -    822          1.01 (1.01-1.01) < 0.001 #>  2: ECOG Performance Status       0    261                 reference       - #>  3:                               1    295          1.14 (1.10-1.18) < 0.001 #>  4:                               2    229          1.21 (1.17-1.25) < 0.001 #>  5:                               3     37          1.29 (1.21-1.38) < 0.001 #>  6:           Disease Stage       I    206                 reference       - #>  7:                              II    257          1.06 (1.02-1.10)   0.002 #>  8:                             III    235          1.13 (1.08-1.17) < 0.001 #>  9:                              IV    124          1.15 (1.10-1.20) < 0.001 #> 10:         Treatment Group Control    190                 reference       - #> 11:                          Drug A    287          0.95 (0.92-0.99)   0.007 #> 12:                          Drug B    345          1.13 (1.09-1.17) < 0.001

title: Regression Modeling
text: Linear mixed-effects models (LMMs) account hierarchical clustered data structures. clintrial dataset includes site variable representing study site, making suitable demonstrating random effects. model type requires lme4 package. Include random intercepts study site using (1|site) notation predictors:
code: example20 <- fit(   data = clintrial,   outcome = "los_days",   predictors = c("age", "sex", "treatment", "stage", "(1|site)"),   model_type = "lmer",   labels = clintrial_labels )  example20 #>  #> Multivariable Linear Mixed Model #> Formula: los_days ~ age + sex + treatment + stage + (1|site) #> n = 827 #>  #>            Variable   Group      n Adj. Coefficient (95% CI) p-value #>              <char>  <char> <char>                    <char>  <char> #>  1:     Age (years)       -    827       0.14 (0.11 to 0.16) < 0.001 #>  2:             Sex  Female    441                 reference       - #>  3:                    Male    386       0.82 (0.28 to 1.37)   0.003 #>  4: Treatment Group Control    190                 reference       - #>  5:                  Drug A    288    -0.94 (-1.68 to -0.20)   0.013 #>  6:                  Drug B    349       2.41 (1.70 to 3.13) < 0.001 #>  7:   Disease Stage       I    207                 reference       - #>  8:                      II    259       1.25 (0.51 to 1.98) < 0.001 #>  9:                     III    235       2.43 (1.67 to 3.19) < 0.001 #> 10:                      IV    126       2.96 (2.07 to 3.85) < 0.001

title: Regression Modeling
text: Generalized linear mixed-effects models (GLMMs) extend mixed-effects models non-normal outcomes (binary, count). model type also requires lme4 package. Model 30-day readmission site-level random effects:
code: example21 <- fit(   data = clintrial,   outcome = "readmission_30d",   predictors = c("age", "sex", "diabetes", "treatment", "(1|site)"),   model_type = "glmer",   family = "binomial",   labels = clintrial_labels )  example21 #>  #> Multivariable glmerMod Model #> Formula: readmission_30d ~ age + sex + diabetes + treatment + (1|site) #> n = 834, Events = 417 #>  #>           Variable   Group      n Events     aOR (95% CI) p-value #>             <char>  <char> <char> <char>           <char>  <char> #> 1:     Age (years)       -    834    417 1.03 (1.02-1.05) < 0.001 #> 2:             Sex  Female    444    207        reference       - #> 3:                    Male    390    210 1.36 (1.02-1.81)   0.036 #> 4:        Diabetes      No    637    301        reference       - #> 5:                     Yes    197    116 1.65 (1.17-2.33)   0.004 #> 6: Treatment Group Control    191     88        reference       - #> 7:                  Drug A    288    126 0.87 (0.59-1.28)   0.471 #> 8:                  Drug B    355    203 1.58 (1.09-2.28)   0.016

title: Regression Modeling
text: count outcomes clustering, use fu_count (equidispersed) site-level random effects. Standard Poisson GLMMs assume equidispersion:
code: example22 <- fit(   data = clintrial,   outcome = "fu_count",   predictors = c("age", "stage", "treatment", "(1|site)"),   model_type = "glmer",   family = "poisson",   labels = clintrial_labels )  example22 #>  #> Multivariable glmerMod Model #> Formula: fu_count ~ age + stage + treatment + (1|site) #> n = 839 #>  #>           Variable   Group      n Events     aRR (95% CI) p-value #>             <char>  <char> <char> <char>           <char>  <char> #> 1:     Age (years)       -    839   <NA> 1.00 (0.99-1.00)   0.005 #> 2:   Disease Stage       I    207  1,238        reference       - #> 3:                      II    261  1,638 1.05 (0.97-1.13)   0.233 #> 4:                     III    240  1,638 1.12 (1.04-1.21)   0.002 #> 5:                      IV    131  1,003 1.27 (1.17-1.38) < 0.001 #> 6: Treatment Group Control    191  1,169        reference       - #> 7:                  Drug A    290  1,910 1.07 (1.00-1.16)   0.053 #> 8:                  Drug B    358  2,438 1.11 (1.03-1.19)   0.005

title: Regression Modeling
text: Cox mixed-effects models account within-cluster correlation survival outcomes. useful patients nested within sites physicians outcomes may correlated within clusters. model type requires coxme package. Model overall survival site-level random effects:
code: example23 <- fit(   data = clintrial,   outcome = "Surv(os_months, os_status)",   predictors = c("age", "sex", "treatment", "stage", "(1|site)"),   model_type = "coxme",   labels = clintrial_labels )  example23 #>  #> Multivariable Mixed Effects Cox Model #> Formula: Surv(os_months, os_status) ~ age + sex + treatment + stage + (1|site) #> n = 847, Events = 606 #>  #>            Variable   Group      n Events     aHR (95% CI) p-value #>              <char>  <char> <char> <char>           <char>  <char> #>  1:     Age (years)       -    847    606 1.04 (1.03-1.05) < 0.001 #>  2:             Sex  Female    450    298        reference       - #>  3:                    Male    400    311 1.36 (1.16-1.60) < 0.001 #>  4: Treatment Group Control    196    151        reference       - #>  5:                  Drug A    292    184 0.57 (0.46-0.72) < 0.001 #>  6:                  Drug B    362    274 0.93 (0.76-1.14)   0.492 #>  7:   Disease Stage       I    211    127        reference       - #>  8:                      II    263    172 1.17 (0.93-1.48)   0.178 #>  9:                     III    241    186 2.04 (1.62-2.58) < 0.001 #> 10:                      IV    132    121 4.09 (3.16-5.30) < 0.001

what: Example 24: Quasibinomial for Overdispersed Binary Data
title: Regression Modeling
text: binary count data exhibit overdispersion (residual deviance >> residual degrees freedom), quasi-likelihood models provide appropriate standard errors.
code: example24 <- fit(   data = clintrial,   outcome = "any_complication",   predictors = c("age", "sex", "diabetes", "stage"),   model_type = "glm",   family = "quasibinomial",   labels = clintrial_labels )  example24 #>  #> Multivariable Quasi-Binomial Model #> Formula: any_complication ~ age + sex + diabetes + stage #> n = 834, Events = 468 #>  #>         Variable  Group      n Events     aOR (95% CI) p-value #>           <char> <char> <char> <char>           <char>  <char> #> 1:   Age (years)      -    834    468 1.01 (1.00-1.02)   0.155 #> 2:           Sex Female    444    236        reference       - #> 3:                 Male    390    232 1.33 (1.00-1.76)   0.048 #> 4:      Diabetes     No    637    335        reference       - #> 5:                  Yes    197    133 1.93 (1.37-2.71) < 0.001 #> 6: Disease Stage      I    207    117        reference       - #> 7:                   II    261    131 0.75 (0.52-1.09)   0.132 #> 8:                  III    237    144 1.22 (0.83-1.79)   0.317 #> 9:                   IV    129     76 1.07 (0.68-1.69)   0.764

what: Example 25: Conditional Logistic Model for Matched Data
title: Regression Modeling
text: Conditional logistic regression used matched case-control studies cases controls matched within strata. stratification variable specified using strata parameter. model type requires survival package. output shows odds ratios conditional matching strata.
code: # Matched case-control dataset (100 pairs) set.seed(456) n_pairs <- 100 matched_data <- do.call(rbind, lapply(1:n_pairs, function(i) {   base_bmi <- rnorm(1, 27, 4)   data.frame(     match_id = i,     case = c(1, 0),     smoking = factor(c(rbinom(1, 1, 0.45), rbinom(1, 1, 0.30)),                       levels = c(0, 1), labels = c("No", "Yes")),     diabetes = factor(c(rbinom(1, 1, 0.30), rbinom(1, 1, 0.20)),                        levels = c(0, 1), labels = c("No", "Yes")),     bmi = c(base_bmi + rnorm(1, 1.5, 2), base_bmi + rnorm(1, -0.5, 2))   ) }))  example25 <- fit(   data = matched_data,   outcome = "case",   predictors = c("smoking", "diabetes", "bmi"),   model_type = "clogit",   strata = "match_id" )  example25 #>  #> Multivariable Conditional Logistic Model #> Formula: case ~ smoking + diabetes + bmi + strata( match_id ) #> n = 124, Events = 100 #>  #>    Variable  Group      n Events     aHR (95% CI) p-value #>      <char> <char> <char> <char>           <char>  <char> #> 1:  smoking     No    124    100        reference       - #> 2:             Yes     76    100 3.46 (1.48-8.12)   0.004 #> 3: diabetes     No    140    100        reference       - #> 4:             Yes     60    100 0.90 (0.40-2.03)   0.795 #> 5:      bmi      -    200    100 1.61 (1.30-2.00) < 0.001

title: Regression Modeling
text: Exploratory phase: Use uniscreen() liberal p-threshold (e.g., 0.20) Model building: Use fullfit() method = "screen" method = "custom" Confirmatory analysis: Use fit() pre-specified predictors Sensitivity analysis: Compare specifications compfit() (see Model Comparison)

title: Regression Modeling
text: comparison univariable multivariable estimates reveals confounding: Large differences: Substantial confounding present Similar estimates: Association robust adjustment Sign reversal: Simpson’s paradox; investigate carefully

title: Regression Modeling
text: Adequate events per predictor required stable estimation: Logistic/Cox models: ≥10 events per predictor (rule thumb) Linear models: ≥10–20 observations per predictor Mixed-effects models: ≥5–10 clusters recommended Use method = "screen" reduce predictors sample size limited

title: Regression Modeling
text: Regression functions use complete-case analysis default:
code: # Check missingness sapply(clintrial[, c("age", "sex", "stage")], function(x) sum(is.na(x)))  # Create complete-case dataset explicitly complete_data <- na.omit(clintrial[, c("readmission_30d", "age", "sex", "stage")])

title: Regression Modeling
text: Ensure reference categories set appropriately:
code: # Set specific reference level clintrial$stage <- relevel(factor(clintrial$stage), ref = "I")

title: Regression Modeling
text: models fail converge:
code: # Access model for diagnostics result <- fit(data, outcome, predictors, model_type = "glm") model <- attr(result, "model")  # Check convergence model$converged  # Large coefficients may indicate separation coef(model)

title: Regression Modeling
text: check overdispersion Poisson binomial models: dispersion substantially greater 1, consider using quasipoisson, quasibinomial, negbin instead.
code: # Fit Poisson model result <- fit(data, outcome, predictors, model_type = "glm", family = "poisson") model <- attr(result, "model")  # Dispersion estimate (should be ~1 for no overdispersion) sum(residuals(model, type = "pearson")^2) / model$df.residual

title: Regression Modeling
text: Mixed-effects models can sensitive starting values optimization:
code: # Increase iterations result <- fit(   data = clintrial,   outcome = "readmission_30d",   predictors = c("age", "treatment", "(1|site)"),   model_type = "glmer",   family = "binomial",   control = lme4::glmerControl(optimizer = "bobyqa", optCtrl = list(maxfun = 100000)) )

title: Regression Modeling
text: summata package currently supports binary outcomes (via logistic regression) multinomial ordinal outcomes two categories. categorical outcomes two levels, use dedicated packages nnet::multinom() multinomial regression MASS::polr() ordinal regression.

title: Regression Modeling
text: Statistical Foundations: Mathematical details supported models Descriptive Tables: desctable() baseline characteristics Model Comparison: compfit() comparing models Table Export: Export PDF, Word, formats Forest Plots: Visualization regression results Multivariate Regression: multifit() multi-outcome analysis Advanced Workflows: Interactions mixed-effects models

title: Statistical Foundations
text: Throughout article, adopt following notation:

title: Statistical Foundations
text: Linear regression models expected value continuous response linear combination predictors: E[Yi|Xi]=β0+β1Xi1+β2Xi2+⋯+βpXipE[Y_i | X_i] = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_{i1} + \beta_2 X_{i2} + \cdots + \beta_p X_{ip} Equivalently, matrix notation: 𝐘=𝐗𝛃+𝛆\mathbf{Y} = \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta} + \boldsymbol{\varepsilon} 𝛆∼N(𝟎,σ2𝐈)\boldsymbol{\varepsilon} \sim N(\mathbf{0}, \sigma^2\mathbf{}).

title: Statistical Foundations
text: Linearity: relationship predictors response linear Independence: Observations independent Homoscedasticity: Constant error variance across levels predictors Normality: Errors normally distributed (inference) multicollinearity: Predictors perfectly correlated

title: Statistical Foundations
text: Coefficients estimated ordinary least squares (OLS): 𝛃̂=(𝐗′𝐗)−1𝐗′𝐘\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = (\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y}

title: Statistical Foundations
text: coefficient βⱼ represents expected change Y one-unit increase Xⱼ, holding predictors constant.

title: Statistical Foundations
text: Coefficient determination (R²): R2=1−∑(Yi−Ŷ)2∑(Yi−Y‾)2R^2 = 1 - \frac{\sum_i (Y_i - \hat{Y}_i)^2}{\sum_i (Y_i - \bar{Y})^2} Adjusted R²: Radj2=1−(1−R2)(n−1)n−p−1R^2_{\text{adj}} = 1 - \frac{(1 - R^2)(n - 1)}{n - p - 1} Root mean squared error (RMSE): RMSE=1n∑(Yi−Ŷ)2\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{n}\sum_i (Y_i - \hat{Y}_i)^2}

title: Statistical Foundations
text: summata, linear regression specified model_type = "lm":
code: fit(data, outcome = "continuous_var", predictors, model_type = "lm")

title: Statistical Foundations
text: Generalized linear models (GLMs) extend linear regression accommodate non-normal response distributions three components: Random component: Distribution Y exponential family Systematic component: Linear predictor η=𝐗𝛃\eta = \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta} Link function: g(μ)=ηg(\mu) = \eta, μ=E[Y]\mu = E[Y]

title: Statistical Foundations
text: binary outcomes Y ∈ {0, 1}, logistic regression models log-odds event: log⁡(P(Yi=1|Xi)1−P(Yi=1|Xi))=β0+β1Xi1+⋯+βpXip\log\left(\frac{P(Y_i = 1 | X_i)}{1 - P(Y_i = 1 | X_i)}\right) = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_{i1} + \cdots + \beta_p X_{ip} Equivalently: P(Yi=1|Xi)=exp⁡(β0+β1Xi1+⋯+βpXip)1+exp⁡(β0+β1Xi1+⋯+βpXip)P(Y_i = 1 | X_i) = \frac{\exp(\beta_0 + \beta_1 X_{i1} + \cdots + \beta_p X_{ip})}{1 + \exp(\beta_0 + \beta_1 X_{i1} + \cdots + \beta_p X_{ip})}

title: Statistical Foundations
text: Binary outcome: Y takes values 0 1 Independence: Observations independent Linearity log-odds: Log-odds linear function predictors multicollinearity: Predictors perfectly correlated Large sample: Adequate events per predictor (≥10 rule thumb)

title: Statistical Foundations
text: exponentiated coefficient exp(βⱼ) yields odds ratio (): =exp⁡(βj)=Odds(Y=1|Xj+1)Odds(Y=1|Xj)\text{} = \exp(\beta_j) = \frac{\text{Odds}(Y = 1 | X_j + 1)}{\text{Odds}(Y = 1 | X_j)} continuous predictor, represents multiplicative change odds per one-unit increase. categorical predictor, compares level reference category.

title: Statistical Foundations
code: fit(data, outcome = "binary_var", predictors, model_type = "glm", family = "binomial")

title: Statistical Foundations
text: count outcomes Y ∈ {0, 1, 2, …}, Poisson regression models log expected count: log⁡(E[Yi|Xi])=β0+β1Xi1+⋯+βpXip\log(E[Y_i | X_i]) = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_{i1} + \cdots + \beta_p X_{ip}

title: Statistical Foundations
text: Count outcome: Y non-negative integer Independence: Observations independent Equidispersion: Mean equals variance (E[Y] = Var(Y)) Log-linear relationship: Log expected count linear predictors

title: Statistical Foundations
text: exponentiated coefficient exp(βⱼ) yields rate ratio (RR): RR=exp⁡(βj)=E[Y|Xj+1]E[Y|Xj]\text{RR} = \exp(\beta_j) = \frac{E[Y | X_j + 1]}{E[Y | X_j]}

title: Statistical Foundations
text: Var(Y) > E[Y] (overdispersion), standard Poisson SEs underestimated. Solutions include: Quasipoisson: Estimates dispersion parameter φ Negative binomial: Models additional variance component

title: Statistical Foundations
code: # Standard Poisson fit(data, outcome = "count_var", predictors, model_type = "glm", family = "poisson")  # Overdispersed counts fit(data, outcome = "count_var", predictors, model_type = "glm", family = "quasipoisson")

previous_headings: Generalized Linear Models > Negative Binomial Regression
title: Statistical Foundations
text: Negative binomial regression extends Poisson regression additional dispersion parameter θ: Yi∼NegBin(μi,θ)Y_i \sim \text{NegBin}(\mu_i, \theta)log⁡(μi)=β0+β1Xi1+⋯+βpXip\log(\mu_i) = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_{i1} + \cdots + \beta_p X_{ip} variance : Var(Yi)=μi+μi2θ\text{Var}(Y_i) = \mu_i + \frac{\mu_i^2}{\theta} θ → ∞, negative binomial converges Poisson.

title: Statistical Foundations
text: Positive outcome: Y > 0 Gamma distribution: Right-skewed, variance proportional mean squared Independence: Observations independent

title: Statistical Foundations
text: log link, exp(βⱼ) represents multiplicative effect (ratio): E[Y|Xj+1]E[Y|Xj]=exp⁡(βj)\frac{E[Y | X_j + 1]}{E[Y | X_j]} = \exp(\beta_j)

text: overdispersed binary data, quasibinomial regression estimates dispersion parameter: Var(Yi)=ϕ⋅μi(1−μi)\text{Var}(Y_i) = \phi \cdot \mu_i(1 - \mu_i) φ > 1 indicates overdispersion. Coefficients identical binomial GLM, standard errors adjusted.

title: Statistical Foundations
text: Cox model relates hazard function predictors: h(t|Xi)=h0(t)exp⁡(β1Xi1+β2Xi2+⋯+βpXip)h(t | X_i) = h_0(t) \exp(\beta_1 X_{i1} + \beta_2 X_{i2} + \cdots + \beta_p X_{ip}) : h(t | X) hazard (instantaneous risk) time t h₀(t) baseline hazard (unspecified) exp(βⱼ) hazard ratio predictor j

text: Proportional hazards: Hazard ratios constant time Independence: Observations independent (appropriately modeled) Censoring: Non-informative censoring multicollinearity: Predictors perfectly correlated

title: Statistical Foundations
text: hazard ratio (HR) = exp(βⱼ) represents relative hazard: HR=h(t|Xj+1)h(t|Xj)=exp⁡(βj)\text{HR} = \frac{h(t | X_j + 1)}{h(t | X_j)} = \exp(\beta_j)

previous_headings: Survival Analysis > Cox Proportional Hazards Model
title: Statistical Foundations
text: Concordance (Harrell’s C-index): C=P(ĥ>ĥj|Ti<Tj)C = P(\hat{h}_i > \hat{h}_j | T_i < T_j) Proportion concordant pairs; C = 0.5 indicates discrimination, C = 1 indicates perfect discrimination.

title: Statistical Foundations
text: Mixed-effects models (hierarchical multilevel models) account correlation within clusters incorporating random effects.

title: Statistical Foundations
text: observation within cluster j: g(E[Yij|Xij,uj])=𝐗ij𝛃+𝐙ij𝐮jg(E[Y_{ij} | X_{ij}, u_j]) = \mathbf{X}_{ij}\boldsymbol{\beta} + \mathbf{Z}_{ij}\mathbf{u}_j : β fixed effects (population-averaged) uⱼ ~ N(0, Σ) random effects (cluster-specific deviations) Zᵢⱼ random effects design matrix

title: Statistical Foundations
text: continuous outcomes: Yij=β0+β1X1ij+⋯+βpXpij+u0j+u1jX1ij+εijY_{ij} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_{1ij} + \cdots + \beta_p X_{pij} + u_{0j} + u_{1j}X_{1ij} + \varepsilon_{ij} : u₀ⱼ ~ N(0, σ²ᵤ₀) random intercept cluster j u₁ⱼ ~ N(0, σ²ᵤ₁) random slope (included) εᵢⱼ ~ N(0, σ²) residual error

text: clustered survival data: hij(t)=h0(t)exp⁡(β1X1ij+⋯+βpXpij+uj)h_{ij}(t) = h_0(t) \exp(\beta_1 X_{1ij} + \cdots + \beta_p X_{pij} + u_j) uⱼ ~ N(0, σ²ᵤ) cluster-specific frailty term.

title: Statistical Foundations
text: Mixed-effects models require adequate sample sizes levels:

title: Statistical Foundations
text: interaction occurs effect one predictor depends level another. predictors X Z: g(E[Y])=β0+β1X+β2Z+β3(X×Z)g(E[Y]) = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X + \beta_2 Z + \beta_3 (X \times Z) : β₁ effect X Z = 0 β₂ effect Z X = 0 β₃ interaction effect (modification X effect Z)

title: Statistical Foundations
text: continuous X Z, effect X Y : ∂E[Y]∂X=β1+β3Z\frac{\partial E[Y]}{\partial X} = \beta_1 + \beta_3 Z effect X varies linearly Z.

title: Statistical Foundations
text: continuous X binary Z (0/1): Z = 0: Effect X β₁ Z = 1: Effect X β₁ + β₃

title: Statistical Foundations
text: two categorical variables, combination levels distinct effect. interaction coefficient represents additional effect beyond sum main effects.

title: Statistical Foundations
text: Wald test: Test H₀: β₃ = 0 Likelihood ratio test: Compare models without interaction Model comparison: Use compfit() compare AIC/BIC

title: Statistical Foundations
code: fit(data, outcome, predictors, interactions = c("X:Z"), model_type = "glm")

title: Statistical Foundations
text: AIC=−2log⁡L+2k\text{AIC} = -2\log L + 2k L maximized likelihood k number parameters. AIC balances fit complexity; lower values indicate better models.

title: Statistical Foundations
text: BIC=−2log⁡L+klog⁡n\text{BIC} = -2\log L + k\log n BIC penalizes complexity heavily AIC, especially large samples.

title: Statistical Foundations
text: binary outcomes survival models: C=P(p̂>p̂j|Yi=1,Yj=0)C = P(\hat{p}_i > \hat{p}_j | Y_i = 1, Y_j = 0)

title: Statistical Foundations
text: binary outcomes: Brier=1n∑(Yi−p̂)2\text{Brier} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_i (Y_i - \hat{p}_i)^2 Lower values indicate better calibration; Brier = 0 perfect.

title: Statistical Foundations
text: R2=1−SSresSStotR^2 = 1 - \frac{SS_{\text{res}}}{SS_{\text{tot}}} Proportion variance explained.

title: Statistical Foundations
text: McFadden’s pseudo-R²: RMcF2=1−log⁡Lfulllog⁡LnullR^2_{\text{McF}} = 1 - \frac{\log L_{\text{full}}}{\log L_{\text{null}}} Nagelkerke’s pseudo-R²: RN2=1−(Lnull/Lfull)2/n1−Lnull2/nR^2_N = \frac{1 - (L_{\text{null}}/L_{\text{full}})^{2/n}}{1 - L_{\text{null}}^{2/n}} Values directly comparable linear R²; 0.2–0.4 typically indicates good fit logistic models.

title: Statistical Foundations
text: comparing multiple candidate models, researchers typically examine several quality metrics: information criteria (AIC, BIC), discrimination (concordance), calibration (Brier score), explained variation (R² pseudo-R²). metric captures different aspect model performance, single measure universally optimal model selection. Composite Model Score (CMS) addresses challenge combining multiple metrics single summary value, facilitating rapid comparison across candidate models. approach: Synthesizes complementary information diverse metrics Provides model-type-specific weighting based statistical conventions Normalizes metrics common 0–100 scale interpretability Accounts convergence issues may invalidate model results CMS intended screening tool identify promising models, replacement substantive evaluation. Final model selection always consider domain knowledge, parsimony, specific research objectives.

title: Statistical Foundations
text: component metric transformed 0–100 scale higher values indicate better performance: Information criteria (AIC, BIC) — Lower better: SAIC=100×(1−AICi−AICminAICmax−AICmin)S_{\text{AIC}} = 100 \times \left(1 - \frac{\text{AIC}_i - \text{AIC}_{\min}}{\text{AIC}_{\max} - \text{AIC}_{\min}}\right) Concordance (C-statistic) — Higher better, 0.5 floor: SC={0C≤0.5200×(C−0.5)C>0.5S_C = \begin{cases}  0 & C \leq 0.5 \\ 200 \times (C - 0.5) & C > 0.5 \end{cases} Pseudo-R² (McFadden) — Scaled recognizing typical range: SR2=min⁡(100,250×RMcF2)S_{R^2} = \min(100, \; 250 \times R^2_{\text{McF}}) scaling reflects McFadden’s R² rarely exceeds 0.4 even well-fitting models. Brier score — Lower better, 0.25 -skill baseline: SBrier=100×(1−Brier0.25)S_{\text{Brier}} = 100 \times \left(1 - \frac{\text{Brier}}{0.25}\right) Convergence — Categorical assessment: Sconv={100converged normally70suspect (warnings)30failed convergeS_{\text{conv}} = \begin{cases} 100 & \text{converged normally} \\ 70 & \text{suspect (warnings)} \\ 30 & \text{failed converge} \end{cases}

title: Statistical Foundations
text: Component scores combined using model-type-specific weights reflect relative importance metric model class: mixed-effects models, additional components include marginal R², conditional R², ICC.

title: Statistical Foundations
text: CMS computed weighted sum: CMS=∑jwj×Sj\text{CMS} = \sum_j w_j \times S_j wⱼ weights Sⱼ normalized component scores.

title: Statistical Foundations
text: thresholds provide general guidance. model CMS = 80 necessarily “better” one CMS = 78—small differences prompt examination individual metrics substantive considerations.

title: Statistical Foundations
text: Relative ranking: CMS values relative set models compared; indicate absolute model quality Weight sensitivity: Results depend chosen weights; users strong priors metric importance can supply custom weights Metric availability: metrics available model types; weights renormalized components missing substitute judgment: CMS facilitates screening replace careful model diagnostics domain expertise

title: Statistical Foundations
code: # Compare candidate models comparison <- compfit(   data = mydata,   outcome = "y",   predictors = list(     "Model 1" = c("x1", "x2"),     "Model 2" = c("x1", "x2", "x3"),     "Model 3" = c("x1", "x2", "x3", "x4")   ),   model_type = "logistic" )  # Custom weights emphasizing discrimination comparison_custom <- compfit(   data = mydata,   outcome = "y",   predictors = model_list,   model_type = "logistic",   scoring_weights = list(     convergence = 0.10,     aic = 0.20,     concordance = 0.50,     pseudo_r2 = 0.15,     brier = 0.05   ) )

title: Statistical Foundations
text: coefficient β standard error SE(β): β±z1−α/2⋅SE(β)\beta \pm z_{1-\alpha/2} \cdot \text{SE}(\beta) z₁₋α/₂ standard normal quantile (1.96 95% CI).

title: Statistical Foundations
text: accurate small samples boundary estimates; based likelihood ratio: {β:2[log⁡L(β̂)−log⁡L(β)]≤χ1,1−α2}\{\beta : 2[\log L(\hat{\beta}) - \log L(\beta)] \leq \chi^2_{1,1-\alpha}\}

text: exponentiated coefficients (, HR, RR): exp⁡(β±z1−α/2⋅SE(β))\exp\left(\beta \pm z_{1-\alpha/2} \cdot \text{SE}(\beta)\right)

title: Survival Tables
text: examples vignette use clintrial dataset included summata: clintrial dataset contains 850 observations time--event variables suitable demonstrating survival summaries.
code: library(summata) library(survival)  data(clintrial) data(clintrial_labels)

title: Survival Tables
text: Landmark survival estimates report probability survival specific time points (e.g., 1-year, 2-year survival rates).

title: Survival Tables
text: following example reports survival probabilities 12, 24, 36 months (default median survival reporting included):
code: example1 <- survtable(   data = clintrial,   outcome = "Surv(os_months, os_status)",   by = "treatment",   times = c(12, 24, 36),   time_unit = "months" )  example1 #>  #> Survival Summary Table #> Outcome: Surv(os_months, os_status) #> Stratified by: treatment #> Time points: 12, 24, 36 months #> Quantiles: 50% #> Statistic: Survival probability #> Test: Log-rank (p = < 0.001) #>  #>    Variable    12 months    24 months    36 months  Median (95% CI) p-value #>      <char>       <char>       <char>       <char>           <char>  <char> #> 1:    Total 59% (56-62%) 46% (43-49%) 36% (33-40%) 19.4 (16.2-23.4) < 0.001 #> 2:  Control 55% (49-63%) 37% (31-44%) 26% (20-33%) 14.7 (10.5-19.2)         #> 3:   Drug A 68% (63-74%) 56% (51-62%) 48% (42-54%) 33.6 (24.5-42.2)         #> 4:   Drug B 54% (49-59%) 43% (38-48%) 33% (28-38%) 14.7 (11.0-21.8)

title: Survival Tables
text: default, column showing median survival displayed (probs = 0.5). remove , set probs = NULL.
code: example2 <- survtable(   data = clintrial,   outcome = "Surv(os_months, os_status)",   by = "treatment",   times = c(12, 24),   probs = NULL,   time_unit = "months",   total = FALSE )  example2 #>  #> Survival Summary Table #> Outcome: Surv(os_months, os_status) #> Stratified by: treatment #> Time points: 12, 24 months #> Statistic: Survival probability #> Test: Log-rank (p = < 0.001) #>  #>    Variable    12 months    24 months p-value #>      <char>       <char>       <char>  <char> #> 1:  Control 55% (49-63%) 37% (31-44%) < 0.001 #> 2:   Drug A 68% (63-74%) 56% (51-62%)         #> 3:   Drug B 54% (49-59%) 43% (38-48%)

title: Survival Tables
text: Survival quantiles report time specified proportion subjects experienced event. median survival time (50th percentile) common, quantiles provide additional context.

title: Survival Tables
text: Report multiple survival quantiles specifying probs parameter:
code: example3 <- survtable(   data = clintrial,   outcome = "Surv(os_months, os_status)",   by = "stage",   times = NULL,   probs = c(0.25, 0.5, 0.75),   labels = clintrial_labels )  example3 #>  #> Survival Summary Table #> Outcome: Surv(os_months, os_status) #> Stratified by: stage #> Quantiles: 25%, 50%, 75% #> Statistic: Survival probability #> Test: Log-rank (p = < 0.001) #>  #>    Variable 75% Survival Time (95% CI)  Median (95% CI) 25% Survival Time (95% CI) p-value #>      <char>                     <char>           <char>                     <char>  <char> #> 1:    Total              5.1 (4.3-6.1) 19.5 (16.1-23.5)                         NR < 0.001 #> 2:        I             9.0 (7.4-12.5) 33.7 (22.4-45.2)                         NR         #> 3:       II             7.7 (6.1-12.3) 32.2 (24.8-42.0)                         NR         #> 4:      III              3.9 (3.0-5.2) 13.7 (10.4-22.3)             44.7 (35.8-NR)         #> 5:       IV              2.0 (1.4-2.8)    5.9 (4.3-8.2)           15.6 (10.8-26.7)

title: Survival Tables
text: Studies often include multiple time--event outcomes, progression-free survival (PFS) overall survival (OS). survtable() function handles multiple endpoints single call.

title: Survival Tables
text: Pass vector outcomes compare multiple survival endpoints:
code: example4 <- survtable(   data = clintrial,   outcome = c("Surv(pfs_months, pfs_status)", "Surv(os_months, os_status)"),   by = "treatment",   times = c(12, 24),   probs = 0.5,   time_unit = "months",   total = FALSE,   labels = c(     "Surv(pfs_months, pfs_status)" = "Progression-Free Survival",     "Surv(os_months, os_status)" = "Overall Survival"   ) )  example4 #>  #> Survival Summary Table #> Outcomes: 2 #> Stratified by: treatment #> Time points: 12, 24 months #> Quantiles: 50% #> Statistic: Survival probability #> Test: Log-rank #>  #>                     Variable   Group    12 months    24 months  Median (95% CI) p-value #>                       <char>  <char>       <char>       <char>           <char>  <char> #> 1: Progression-Free Survival Control 29% (23-36%)  14% (9-20%)    4.7 (3.6-6.9) < 0.001 #> 2:                            Drug A 44% (39-51%) 30% (25-36%)   9.0 (7.5-12.4)         #> 3:                            Drug B 30% (26-36%) 18% (14-22%)    4.7 (3.7-6.2)         #> 4:          Overall Survival Control 55% (49-63%) 37% (31-44%) 14.7 (10.5-19.2) < 0.001 #> 5:                            Drug A 68% (63-74%) 56% (51-62%) 33.6 (24.5-42.2)         #> 6:                            Drug B 54% (49-59%) 43% (38-48%) 14.7 (11.0-21.8)

title: Survival Tables
text: competing risks analyses reporting event rates rather survival probabilities, use type = "risk" display cumulative incidence (1 − survival):
code: example5 <- survtable(   data = clintrial,   outcome = "Surv(os_months, os_status)",   by = "treatment",   times = c(12, 24, 36),   type = "risk",   time_unit = "months" )  example5 #>  #> Survival Summary Table #> Outcome: Surv(os_months, os_status) #> Stratified by: treatment #> Time points: 12, 24, 36 months #> Quantiles: 50% #> Statistic: Cumulative incidence #> Test: Log-rank (p = < 0.001) #>  #>    Variable    12 months    24 months    36 months  Median (95% CI) p-value #>      <char>       <char>       <char>       <char>           <char>  <char> #> 1:    Total 41% (38-44%) 54% (51-57%) 64% (60-67%) 19.4 (16.2-23.4) < 0.001 #> 2:  Control 45% (37-51%) 63% (56-69%) 74% (67-80%) 14.7 (10.5-19.2)         #> 3:   Drug A 32% (26-37%) 44% (38-49%) 52% (46-58%) 33.6 (24.5-42.2)         #> 4:   Drug B 46% (41-51%) 57% (52-62%) 67% (62-72%) 14.7 (11.0-21.8)

title: Survival Tables
text: number risk time point provides context precision survival estimates:
code: example6 <- survtable(   data = clintrial,   outcome = "Surv(os_months, os_status)",   by = "treatment",   times = c(12, 24),   stats = c("survival", "ci", "n_risk"),   time_unit = "months",   total = FALSE )  example6 #>  #> Survival Summary Table #> Outcome: Surv(os_months, os_status) #> Stratified by: treatment #> Time points: 12, 24 months #> Quantiles: 50% #> Statistic: Survival probability #> Test: Log-rank (p = < 0.001) #>  #>    Variable              12 months              24 months  Median (95% CI) p-value #>      <char>                 <char>                 <char>           <char>  <char> #> 1:  Control 55% (49-63%) [n = 106]  37% (31-44%) [n = 70] 14.7 (10.5-19.2) < 0.001 #> 2:   Drug A 68% (63-74%) [n = 192] 56% (51-62%) [n = 154] 33.6 (24.5-42.2)         #> 3:   Drug B 54% (49-59%) [n = 190] 43% (38-48%) [n = 145] 14.7 (11.0-21.8)

title: Survival Tables
text: Survival tables often presented alongside Kaplan-Meier curves publications. summata focuses tabular output, survminer ggsurvfit packages provide excellent options survival curves.

title: Survival Tables
text: following workflow produces matched figure-table pair suitable publication: workflow ensures Kaplan-Meier curve survival table report consistent time points groupings.
code: library(ggsurvfit) library(survival)  # Fit the survival model km_fit <- survfit(Surv(os_months, os_status) ~ treatment, data = clintrial)  # Create Kaplan-Meier plot with risk table ggsurvfit(km_fit) +   add_confidence_interval() +   add_risktable() +   add_quantile(y_value = 0.5, linetype = "dashed") +   scale_ggsurvfit() +   labs(     title = "Overall Survival by Treatment",     x = "Time (months)",     y = "Survival Probability"   ) +   theme_minimal()  # Generate the companion table surv_table <- survtable(   data = clintrial,   outcome = "Surv(os_months, os_status)",   by = "treatment",   times = c(12, 24, 36),   probs = 0.5,   time_unit = "months" )  # Export both for publication ggsave("km_curve.pdf", width = 8, height = 6) table2pdf(surv_table, "survival_table.pdf",            caption = "Table 2. Survival Estimates by Treatment Group")

title: Survival Tables
text: Survival tables can exported various formats using standard summata export functions. See Table Export vignette comprehensive documentation.
code: # Microsoft Word table2docx(   table = example1,   file = "SurvivalTable.docx",   caption = "Table 2. Survival Estimates by Treatment Group" )  # PDF (requires LaTeX) table2pdf(   table = example1,   file = "SurvivalTable.pdf",   caption = "Table 2. Survival Estimates by Treatment Group" )

title: Survival Tables
text: selecting landmark time points, consider following: Use clinically meaningful intervals (e.g., 1-year, 2-year oncology) Ensure adequate follow-selected time points Match time points across treatment arms comparability Consider median follow-selecting latest time point

title: Survival Tables
text: Always report confidence intervals alongside point estimates Include number risk space permits Specify time unit table headers footnotes Use consistent decimal precision across estimates

title: Survival Tables
text: Descriptive Tables: desctable() baseline characteristics Regression Modeling: fit(), uniscreen(), fullfit() Model Comparison: compfit() comparing models Table Export: Export PDF, Word, formats Forest Plots: Visualization regression results Multivariate Regression: multifit() multi-outcome analysis Advanced Workflows: Interactions mixed-effects models

title: Table Export
text: examples vignette create sample tables using summata functions:
code: library(summata)  data(clintrial) data(clintrial_labels)  # Create sample tables for export table1 <- desctable(   data = clintrial,   by = "treatment",   variables = c("age", "sex", "bmi", "smoking", "stage", "ecog"),   labels = clintrial_labels )  table2 <- fullfit(   data = clintrial,   outcome = "surgery",   predictors = c("age", "sex", "stage", "treatment", "ecog"),   model_type = "glm",   labels = clintrial_labels )  table3 <- fullfit(   data = clintrial,   outcome = "Surv(os_months, os_status)",   predictors = c("age", "sex", "stage", "treatment", "ecog"),   model_type = "coxph",   labels = clintrial_labels )  table4 <- compfit(   data = clintrial,   outcome = "surgery",   model_list = list(     "Demographics" = c("age", "sex"),     "Clinical" = c("age", "sex", "stage", "ecog"),     "Full" = c("age", "sex", "stage", "ecog", "treatment")   ),   model_type = "glm" )

title: Table Export
text: summata output tables data.table objects, can written delimited text files directly data.table::fwrite(). simplest export path requires additional dependencies beyond data.table, summata already imports.

title: Table Export
code: data.table::fwrite(table1, "Table1.csv")

title: Table Export
text: Specify tab delimiter TSV output:
code: data.table::fwrite(table1, "Table1.tsv", sep = "\t")

title: Table Export
text: Use semicolon delimiter compatibility locales comma decimal separator: CSV TSV files useful archiving raw table output, importing spreadsheet software, downstream processing statistical environments. formatted, publication-ready output, use export functions described sections follow.
code: data.table::fwrite(table1, "Table1.csv", sep = ";")

title: Table Export
text: table2pdf() function creates PDF documents using LaTeX typesetting via xtable package. LaTeX distribution (TinyTeX, TeX Live, MiKTeX, MacTeX) required.

title: Table Export
text: simplest usage requires table filename:
code: table2pdf(   table = table1,   file = "Table1.pdf" )

title: Table Export
text: Add caption using caption parameter:
code: table2pdf(   table = table1,   file = "Table1.pdf",   caption = "Table 1. Baseline Characteristics by Group" )

title: Table Export
text: Multiple formatting options can combined:
code: table2pdf(   table = table2,   file = "Table2.pdf",   caption = "Table 2. Regression Analysis",   font_size = 8,   bold_significant = TRUE,   p_threshold = 0.05,   indent_groups = TRUE,   variable_padding = TRUE,   zebra_stripes = TRUE )

title: Table Export
text: Control page size orientation:
code: table2pdf(   table = table2,   file = "Table2_Landscape.pdf",   caption = "Table 2. Regression Results",   paper = "letter",   orientation = "landscape" )

title: Table Export
text: Adjust page margins (inches):
code: table2pdf(   table = table2,   file = "Table2_Margins.pdf",   margins = c(0.5, 0.5, 0.5, 0.5)  # top, right, bottom, left )

title: Table Export
text: Scale tables fit page width:
code: table2pdf(   table = table2,   file = "Table2_Fitted.pdf",   fit_to_page = TRUE )

title: Table Export
text: Standalone settings useful embedding tables larger documents:
code: table2pdf(   table = table2,   file = "Table2_Auto.pdf",   paper = "auto" )

title: Table Export

title: Table Export

title: Table Export
text: Control page size orientation wide tables:

text: autotable() function detects output format file extension, simplifying export workflow: Format-specific parameters pass underlying function:
code: autotable(table1, "Table1.csv")   # CSV output autotable(table1, "Table1.tsv")   # TSV output autotable(table1, "Table1.pdf")   # PDF output autotable(table1, "Table1.docx")  # DOCX output autotable(table1, "Table1.html")  # HTML output autotable(table1, "Table1.pptx")  # PPTX output autotable(table1, "Table1.tex")   # TeX output autotable(table1, "Table1.rtf")   # RTF output autotable(   table = table2,   file = "Table2.pdf",   caption = "Table 2. Regression Analysis",   orientation = "landscape",   font_size = 8,   bold_significant = TRUE,   indent_groups = TRUE,   zebra_stripes = TRUE )

title: Table Export
text: export functions share common parameters consistent results:

title: Table Export
text: condense_table condense_quantitative parameters reduce table height: condense_table = TRUE: Condenses continuous, survival, binary categorical variables condense_quantitative = TRUE: Condenses continuous survival variables (desctable() outputs )
code: # Full display table2docx(table1, "Table1_Full.docx")  # Condense all variable types table2docx(   table = table1,   file = "Table1_Condensed.docx",   condense_table = TRUE,   zebra_stripes = TRUE )  # Condense only continuous/survival (descriptive tables only) table2docx(   table = table1,   file = "Table1_CondenseQuant.docx",   condense_quantitative = TRUE,   zebra_stripes = TRUE )

title: Table Export
text: Portrait: Tables ≤ 6 columns Landscape: Tables > 6 columns Auto-sizing: Use paper = "auto" embedding

title: Table Export
text: Export multiple formats consistent formatting:
code: common_opts <- list(   caption = "Table 2. Regression Analysis",   bold_significant = TRUE,   indent_groups = TRUE,   zebra_stripes = TRUE )  formats <- c("csv", "pdf", "docx", "html", "pptx", "rtf", "tex")  for (fmt in formats) {   autotable(     table = table2,     file = paste0("Table2.", fmt),     caption = common_opts$caption,     bold_significant = common_opts$bold_significant,     indent_groups = common_opts$indent_groups,     zebra_stripes = common_opts$zebra_stripes   ) }

title: Table Export
text: Verify LaTeX installation consider keeping log files debugging: See Installation Setup LaTeX configuration details.
code: # Check LaTeX installation Sys.which("pdflatex")  # Install TinyTeX if needed # tinytex::install_tinytex()  # Keep log files for debugging table2pdf(table, "debug.pdf", show_logs = TRUE)

title: Table Export

title: Table Export
text: Get flextable object customization:
code: ft <- table2docx(table1, "Table1.docx", return_ft = TRUE)  library(flextable) ft <- ft %>%   bold(i = 1, part = "header") %>%   color(i = 1, color = "navy", part = "header")  save_as_docx(ft, path = "Table1_Custom.docx")
