title: summata - Publication-Ready Summary Tables and Forest Plots
text: architecture summata reflects three guiding principles: Consistent syntax. modeling functions share common signature: data first, followed outcome variable interest, constituent/modifying variables. convention facilitates pipe-based workflows pedagogical clarity. Transparent computation. Functions attach underlying model objects raw numerical results attributes, permitting verification computations extension analyses beyond formatted output. Separation concerns. Analysis, formatting, export constitute distinct operations, allowing stage modified independently. principles manifest standard calling convention: data dataset, variable variable interest (dependent variable, endpoint/outcome, stratification/grouping variable, etc.), c("var1", "var2", ..., "varN")vector constituent/modifying variables (independent variables, model covariates, compared/explanatory factors, etc.). result formatted table export, readily accessible attributes analysis.
code: result <- fit(data, variable, c("var1", "var2", ..., "varN"), ...) # The formatted table print(result)  # The underlying model object model <- attr(result, "model")  # Raw numerical results raw <- attr(result, "raw_data")

title: summata - Publication-Ready Summary Tables and Forest Plots
text: clintrial dataset included package provides simulated clinical trial data comprising patient identifiers, baseline characteristics, therapeutic interventions, short-term outcomes, long-term survival statistics. following example demonstrates summata functions can used analyze perioperative factors affecting 30-day hospital readmission.

title: summata - Publication-Ready Summary Tables and Forest Plots
text: Prior analysis, load dataset, apply labels, define predictors:
code: library(summata)  # Load example data data("clintrial") data("clintrial_labels")  # Define candidate predictors predictors <- c("age", "sex", "race", "ethnicity", "bmi", "smoking",                 "hypertension", "diabetes", "ecog", "creatinine",                 "hemoglobin", "biomarker_x", "biomarker_y", "grade",                 "treatment", "surgery", "los_days", "stage")

title: summata - Publication-Ready Summary Tables and Forest Plots
text: Use desctable() function generate summary statistics stratification grouping variable (case, 30-day readmission):
code: table1 <- desctable(     data = clintrial,     by = "readmission_30d",     variables = predictors,     labels = clintrial_labels )  table2pdf(table1, "table1.pdf",           caption = "\\textbf{Table 1} - Baseline characteristics by 30-day readmission status",           paper = "auto",           condense_table = TRUE,           dark_header = TRUE,           zebra_stripes = TRUE )

title: summata - Publication-Ready Summary Tables and Forest Plots
text: Perform integrated univariable--multivariable regression workflow using fullfit() function:
code: table2 <- fullfit(     data = clintrial,     outcome = "readmission_30d",     predictors = predictors,     method = "screen",     p_threshold = 0.05,     model_type = "glm",     labels = clintrial_labels )  table2pdf(table2, "table2.pdf",           caption = "\\textbf{Table 2} - Predictors of 30-day readmission, multivariable Cox regression with univariable screen",           paper = "auto",           condense_table = TRUE,           dark_header = TRUE,           zebra_stripes = TRUE )

title: summata - Publication-Ready Summary Tables and Forest Plots
text: Finally, generate forest plot provide graphical representation effect estimates using glmforest() function:
code: forest_30d <- glmforest(table2,                         title = "Factors Affecting 30-Day Readmission",                         labels = clintrial_labels,                         indent_groups = TRUE                         )  ggsave("forest_30d.pdf", forest_30d,        width = attr(forest_30d, "rec_dims")$width,        height = attr(forest_30d, "rec_dims")$height,         units = "in")

what: Create Forest Plot with Automatic Model Detection — autoforest
title: Create Forest Plot with Automatic Model Detection — autoforest
text: convenience wrapper function automatically detects input type routes appropriate specialized forest plot function. eliminates need remember forest function call different model types analysis objects, making ideal exploratory analysis rapid prototyping.

title: Create Forest Plot with Automatic Model Detection — autoforest
code: autoforest(x, data = NULL, title = NULL, ...)

title: Create Forest Plot with Automatic Model Detection — autoforest
text: x One following: fitted model object: glm, lm, coxph, etc. fit_result object fit() fullfit_result object fullfit() uniscreen_result object uniscreen() multifit_result object multifit() data Data frame data.table containing original data. Required x raw model object. Ignored x result object fit(), fullfit(), uniscreen(), multifit() since contain embedded data. title Character string plot title. NULL (default), appropriate title generated based detected model type: Cox models: "Cox Proportional Hazards Model" Logistic regression: "Logistic Regression Model" Poisson regression: "Poisson Regression Model" Linear regression: "Linear Regression Model" Uniscreen results: "Univariable [Type] Screening" Multifit results: "Multivariate [Type] Analysis" ... Additional arguments passed specific forest plot function. Common arguments include: labels Named character vector variable labels digits Number decimal places estimates (default 2) p_digits Number decimal places p-values (default 3) conf_level Confidence level intervals (default 0.95) show_n Logical, show sample sizes (default TRUE) show_events Logical, show event counts (default TRUE survival/binomial) qc_footnote Logical, show model QC stats footer (default TRUE) zebra_stripes Logical, alternating row shading (default TRUE) indent_groups Logical, indent factor levels (default FALSE) color Color point estimates table_width Proportion width table (default 0.6) plot_width, plot_height Explicit dimensions units Dimension units: "", "cm", "mm" See documentation specific forest function available options.

title: Create Forest Plot with Automatic Model Detection — autoforest
text: ggplot object containing complete forest plot. plot   can : Displayed directly: print(plot) Saved file: ggsave("forest.pdf", plot, width = 12, height = 8) customized ggplot2 functions returned object includes attribute "rec_dims"   accessible via attr(plot, "rec_dims"), list   containing: width Numeric. Recommended plot width specified units height Numeric. Recommended plot height specified units recommendations automatically calculated based number   variables, text sizes, layout parameters, printed console   plot_width plot_height specified.

title: Create Forest Plot with Automatic Model Detection — autoforest
text: function provides convenient wrapper around specialized forest plot functions, automatically routing appropriate function based model class result type. parameters passed underlying function, full range options remains available. model-specific advanced features, individual forest functions may called directly. Automatic Detection Logic: function uses following priority order detection: uniscreen results: Detected class "uniscreen_result"     presence attributes outcome, predictors, model_type,     model_scope = "Univariable". Routes uniforest(). multifit results: Detected presence attributes     predictor, outcomes, model_type, raw_data.     Routes multiforest(). Cox models: Classes coxph clogit. Routes     coxforest(). GLM models: Class glm. Routes glmforest(). Linear models: Class lm (glm). Routes     lmforest().

title: Create Forest Plot with Automatic Model Detection — autoforest
code: if (FALSE) { # Load example data data(clintrial) data(clintrial_labels) library(survival)  # Example 1: Logistic regression model glm_model <- glm(surgery ~ age + sex + bmi + smoking,                  family = binomial, data = clintrial)  plot1 <- autoforest(glm_model, data = clintrial) print(plot1) # Automatically detects GLM and routes to glmforest()  # Example 2: Cox proportional hazards model cox_model <- coxph(Surv(os_months, os_status) ~ age + sex + treatment + stage,                    data = clintrial)  plot2 <- autoforest(cox_model, data = clintrial) print(plot2) # Automatically detects coxph and routes to coxforest()  # Example 3: Linear regression model lm_model <- lm(biomarker_x ~ age + sex + bmi + treatment, data = clintrial)  plot3 <- autoforest(lm_model, data = clintrial) print(plot3) # Automatically detects lm and routes to lmforest()  # Example 4: With custom labels plot4 <- autoforest(     glm_model,     data = clintrial,     labels = clintrial_labels,     title = "Risk Factors for Surgical Intervention" ) print(plot4)  # Example 5: Pass additional formatting options plot5 <- autoforest(     cox_model,     data = clintrial,     labels = clintrial_labels,     zebra_stripes = TRUE,     indent_groups = TRUE,     color = "#E74C3C" ) print(plot5)  # Example 6: From fit() result - data and labels extracted automatically fit_result <- fit(     data = clintrial,     outcome = "surgery",     predictors = c("age", "sex", "bmi", "treatment"),     labels = clintrial_labels )  plot6 <- autoforest(fit_result) print(plot6) # No need to pass data or labels - extracted from fit_result  # Example 7: From fullfit() result ff_result <- fullfit(     data = clintrial,     outcome = "surgery",     predictors = c("age", "sex", "bmi", "smoking", "treatment"),     labels = clintrial_labels )  plot7 <- autoforest(ff_result) print(plot7)  # Example 8: From uniscreen() result uni_result <- uniscreen(     data = clintrial,     outcome = "surgery",     predictors = c("age", "sex", "bmi", "smoking", "treatment", "stage"),     labels = clintrial_labels )  plot8 <- autoforest(uni_result) print(plot8) # Automatically detects uniscreen_result and routes to uniforest()  # Example 9: From multifit() result mf_result <- multifit(     data = clintrial,     outcomes = c("surgery", "any_complication", "readmission_30d"),     predictor = "treatment",     labels = clintrial_labels )  plot9 <- autoforest(mf_result) print(plot9) # Automatically detects multifit result and routes to multiforest()  # Example 10: Survival uniscreen surv_uni <- uniscreen(     data = clintrial,     outcome = "Surv(os_months, os_status)",     predictors = c("age", "sex", "treatment", "stage", "grade"),     model_type = "coxph",     labels = clintrial_labels )  plot10 <- autoforest(surv_uni) print(plot10) # Title automatically set to "Univariable Survival Analysis Screening"  # Example 11: Override auto-generated title plot11 <- autoforest(     surv_uni,     title = "Univariable Predictors of Overall Survival" ) print(plot11)  # Example 12: Save with recommended dimensions plot12 <- autoforest(cox_model, data = clintrial, labels = clintrial_labels) dims <- attr(plot12, "rec_dims")  # Save to file # ggsave("forest_plot.pdf", plot12, width = dims$width, height = dims$height) # ggsave("forest_plot.png", plot12, width = dims$width, height = dims$height, dpi = 300)  # Example 13: Poisson regression clintrial$event_count <- rpois(nrow(clintrial), lambda = 3) pois_model <- glm(event_count ~ age + sex + treatment,                   family = poisson, data = clintrial)  plot13 <- autoforest(pois_model, data = clintrial) print(plot13) # Automatically detects Poisson GLM, title set to "Poisson Regression Model"  # Example 14: Mixed-Effects uniscreen if (requireNamespace("lme4", quietly = TRUE)) {     me_uni <- uniscreen(         data = clintrial,         outcome = "surgery",         predictors = c("age", "sex", "treatment"),         model_type = "glmer",         random = "(1 | site)",         labels = clintrial_labels     )          plot14 <- autoforest(me_uni)     print(plot14)     # Title: "Univariable Mixed-Effects Logistic Screening" }  # Example 15: Quick comparison workflow # Fit multiple model types and visualize each models <- list(     logistic = glm(surgery ~ age + sex + treatment, binomial, clintrial),     survival = coxph(Surv(os_months, os_status) ~ age + sex + treatment, clintrial),     linear = lm(biomarker_x ~ age + sex + treatment, clintrial) )  # autoforest handles each appropriately plots <- lapply(models, function(m) autoforest(m, data = clintrial))  # Each plot uses the correct forest function automatically print(plots$logistic) print(plots$survival) print(plots$linear) }

what: Export Table with Automatic Format Detection — autotable
title: Export Table with Automatic Format Detection — autotable
text: Automatically detects output format based file extension exports table using appropriate specialized function. Provides unified interface table export across supported formats.

title: Export Table with Automatic Format Detection — autotable
code: autotable(table, file, ...)

title: Export Table with Automatic Format Detection — autotable
text: table Data frame, data.table, matrix export. Can output desctable(), survtable(), fit(), uniscreen(), fullfit(), compfit(), multifit(), tabular data structure. file Character string specifying output filename. file extension determines export format: .pdf - PDF via LaTeX (uses table2pdf()) .docx - Microsoft Word (uses table2docx()) .html .htm - HTML (uses table2html()) .pptx - Microsoft PowerPoint (uses table2pptx()) .tex - LaTeX source (uses table2tex()) .rtf - Rich Text Format (uses table2rtf()) ... Additional arguments passed format-specific function. See documentation individual functions available parameters: PDF table2pdf() - orientation, paper, margins,              fit_to_page, etc. DOCX table2docx() - font_size, font_family,              caption, etc. HTML table2html() - format_headers, zebra_stripes,              etc. PPTX table2pptx() - font_size, font_family,              caption, etc. TEX table2tex() - caption, format_headers,              align, etc. RTF table2rtf() - font_size, font_family,              caption, etc. Common parameters across formats include: caption Table caption (supported formats) font_size Base font size points (PDF, DOCX, PPTX, RTF) format_headers Format column headers (formats) bold_significant Bold significant p-values (formats) p_threshold Threshold p-value bolding (formats) indent_groups Indent factor levels (formats) condense_table Condense essential rows (formats) zebra_stripes Alternating background colors (formats)

title: Export Table with Automatic Format Detection — autotable
text: Invisibly returns file path. Called primarily side effect   creating output file.

title: Export Table with Automatic Format Detection — autotable
text: function provides convenient wrapper around format-specific export functions, automatically routing appropriate function based file extension. parameters passed underlying function, full range format-specific options remains available. format-specific advanced features, may prefer use individual export functions directly: PDF exports support orientation, paper size, margins, auto-sizing DOCX/PPTX/RTF support font customization flextable formatting HTML supports CSS styling, responsive design, custom themes TeX generates standalone LaTeX source booktabs styling

title: Export Table with Automatic Format Detection — autotable
code: if (FALSE) { # Load example data data(clintrial) data(clintrial_labels)  # Create a regression table results <- fit(     data = clintrial,     outcome = "os_status",     predictors = c("age", "sex", "treatment"),     labels = clintrial_labels )  # Export automatically detects format from extension autotable(results, "results.pdf")   # Creates PDF autotable(results, "results.docx")  # Creates Word document autotable(results, "results.html")  # Creates HTML file autotable(results, "results.pptx")  # Creates PowerPoint slide autotable(results, "results.tex")   # Creates LaTeX source autotable(results, "results.rtf")   # Creates RTF document  # Pass format-specific parameters autotable(results, "results.pdf",             orientation = "landscape",            paper = "a4",            font_size = 10)  autotable(results, "results.docx",            caption = "Table 1: Logistic Regression Results",            font_family = "Times New Roman",            condense_table = TRUE)  autotable(results, "results.html",            zebra_stripes = TRUE,            dark_header = TRUE,            bold_significant = TRUE)  # Works with any summata table output desc <- desctable(clintrial,                   by = "treatment",                   variables = c("age", "sex", "bmi")) autotable(desc, "demographics.pdf")  comparison <- compfit(     data = clintrial,     outcome = "os_status",     model_list = list(         base = c("age", "sex"),         full = c("age", "sex", "treatment", "stage")     ) ) autotable(comparison, "model_comparison.docx") }

title: Simulated Clinical Trial Dataset — clintrial
text: data frame 850 observations 32 variables: patient_id Unique patient identifier (character) age Age enrollment years (numeric: 18-90) sex Biological sex (factor: Female, Male) race Self-reported race (factor: White, Black, Asian, ) ethnicity Hispanic ethnicity (factor: Non-Hispanic, Hispanic) bmi Body mass index kg/m² (numeric) smoking Smoking history (factor: Never, Former, Current) hypertension Hypertension diagnosis (factor: , Yes) diabetes Diabetes diagnosis (factor: , Yes) ecog ECOG performance status (factor: 0, 1, 2, 3) creatinine Baseline creatinine mg/dL (numeric) hemoglobin Baseline hemoglobin g/dL (numeric) biomarker_x Serum biomarker ng/mL (numeric) biomarker_y Serum biomarker B U/L (numeric) site Enrolling site (factor: Site Alpha Site Kappa) grade Tumor grade (factor: Well/Moderately/Poorly differentiated) stage Disease stage diagnosis (factor: , II, III, IV) treatment Randomized treatment (factor: Control, Drug , Drug B) surgery Surgical resection (factor: , Yes) any_complication post-operative complication (factor: , Yes) wound_infection Post-operative wound infection (factor: , Yes) icu_admission ICU admission required (factor: , Yes) readmission_30d Hospital readmission within 30 days (factor: , Yes) pain_score Pain score discharge (numeric: 0-10) recovery_days Days functional recovery (numeric) los_days Hospital length stay days (numeric) ae_count Adverse event count (integer). Overdispersed count suitable     negative binomial quasipoisson regression. fu_count Follow-visit count (integer). Equidispersed count     suitable standard Poisson regression. pfs_months Progression-Free Survival Time (months) pfs_status Progression Death Event os_months Overall survival time months (numeric) os_status Death indicator (numeric: 0=censored, 1=death)

title: Compare Multiple Regression Models — compfit
text: Fits multiple regression models provides comprehensive comparison table model quality metrics, convergence diagnostics, selection guidance. Computes composite score combining multiple quality metrics facilitate rapid model comparison selection.

title: Compare Multiple Regression Models — compfit
code: compfit(   data,   outcome,   model_list,   model_names = NULL,   interactions_list = NULL,   random = NULL,   model_type = "auto",   family = "binomial",   conf_level = 0.95,   p_digits = 3,   include_coefficients = FALSE,   scoring_weights = NULL,   labels = NULL,   number_format = NULL,   verbose = NULL,   ... )

title: Compare Multiple Regression Models — compfit
text: data Data frame data.table containing dataset. outcome Character string specifying outcome variable. survival analysis, use Surv() syntax (e.g., "Surv(time, status)"). model_list List character vectors, containing predictor names one model. Can also single character vector auto-generate nested models. model_names Character vector names model. NULL, uses "Model 1", "Model 2", etc. Default NULL. interactions_list List character vectors specifying interaction terms model. element corresponds one model model_list. Use NULL models without interactions. Use colon notation interactions (e.g., c("age:treatment")). NULL, interactions added model. Default NULL. random Character string specifying random-effects formula mixed-effects models (glmer, lmer, coxme). Use standard lme4/coxme syntax, e.g., "(1|site)" random intercepts site. random effects formula applied models comparison. Alternatively, random effects can included directly predictor vectors within model_list using syntax, allows different random effects structures across models. Default NULL. model_type Character string specifying model type. "auto", detects based outcome. Options include: "auto" - Automatically detect based outcome type (default) "glm" - Generalized linear model. Supports logistic, Poisson,     Gamma, Gaussian via family parameter. "lm" - Linear regression continuous outcomes "coxph" - Cox proportional hazards survival analysis "negbin" - Negative binomial regression overdispersed counts     (requires MASS package) "lmer" - Mixed-effects linear regression clustered continuous     outcomes "glmer" - Mixed-effects logistic regression clustered     categorical outcomes "coxme" - Mixed-effects Cox regression clustered time--event     outcomes family GLM GLMER models, specifies error distribution link function. Common options include: "binomial" - Logistic regression binary outcomes (default) "poisson" - Poisson regression count data "quasibinomial" - Logistic overdispersion "quasipoisson" - Poisson overdispersion "gaussian" - Normal distribution (linear regression via GLM) "Gamma" - Gamma positive continuous data "inverse.gaussian" - positive, highly skewed data negative binomial, use model_type = "negbin" instead. See family options. conf_level Numeric confidence level intervals. Default 0.95. p_digits Integer specifying number decimal places p-values. Values smaller 10^(-p_digits) displayed "< 0.001" (p_digits = 3), "< 0.0001" (p_digits = 4), etc. Default 3. include_coefficients Logical. TRUE, includes second table coefficient estimates. Default FALSE. scoring_weights Named list scoring weights. weight 0 1, sum 1. Available metrics depend model type. NULL, uses sensible defaults. See Details available metrics. labels Named character vector providing custom display labels variables. Default NULL. number_format Character string two-element character vector   controlling thousand decimal separators formatted output. Named   presets: "us" - Comma thousands, period decimal: 1,234.56 [default] "eu" - Period thousands, comma decimal: 1.234,56 "space" - Thin-space thousands, period decimal: 1 234.56       (SI/ISO 31-0) "none" - thousands separator: 1234.56 provide custom two-element vector c(big.mark, decimal.mark),   e.g., c("'", ".") Swiss-style: 1'234.56. NULL (default), uses   getOption("summata.number_format", "us"). Set global option   per session avoid passing argument repeatedly: verbose Logical. TRUE, displays model fitting warnings (e.g., singular fit, convergence issues). FALSE (default), routine fitting messages suppressed unexpected warnings preserved. NULL, uses getOption("summata.verbose", FALSE). ... Additional arguments passed model fitting functions.
code: options(summata.number_format = "eu")

title: Compare Multiple Regression Models — compfit
text: data.table class "compfit_result" containing: Model Model name/identifier CMS Composite Model Score model selection (higher better) N Sample size Events Number events (survival/logistic) Predictors Number predictors Converged Whether model converged properly AIC Akaike Information Criterion BIC Bayesian Information Criterion Pseudo-R² McFadden's pseudo-R-squared (GLM) Concordance C-statistic (logistic/survival) Brier Score Brier accuracy score (logistic) Global p Overall model p-value Attributes include: models List fitted model objects coefficients Coefficient comparison table (requested) best_model Name recommended model

title: Compare Multiple Regression Models — compfit
text: function fits specified models computes comprehensive quality metrics comparison. generates Composite Model Score (CMS) combines multiple metrics: lower AIC/BIC (information criteria), higher concordance (discrimination), model convergence status. GLMs, McFadden's pseudo-R-squared calculated 1 - (logLik/logLik_null). survival models, global p-value comes log-rank test. Models fail converge flagged penalized composite score. Interaction Terms: interactions_list provided, element specifies interaction terms corresponding model model_list. particularly useful testing whether adding interactions improves model fit: Use NULL models without interactions Specify interactions using colon notation: c("age:treatment", "sex:stage") Main effects variables interactions must predictor list Common pattern: Compare main effects model vs model interactions Scoring weights can customized based model type: GLM: "convergence", "aic", "concordance", "pseudo_r2", "brier" Cox: "convergence", "aic", "concordance", "global_p" Linear: "convergence", "aic", "pseudo_r2", "rmse" Default weights emphasize discrimination (concordance) model fit (AIC). composite score designed tool quickly rank models quality metrics. used alongside traditional model selection criteria rather definitive model selection method.

title: Compare Multiple Regression Models — compfit
code: # Load example data data(clintrial) data(clintrial_labels)  # Example 1: Compare nested logistic regression models models <- list(     base = c("age", "sex"),     clinical = c("age", "sex", "smoking", "diabetes"),     full = c("age", "sex", "smoking", "diabetes", "stage", "ecog") )  comparison <- compfit(     data = clintrial,     outcome = "os_status",     model_list = models,     model_names = c("Base", "Clinical", "Full") ) #> Auto-detected binary outcome, using logistic regression #> Fitting Base with 2 predictors... #> Fitting Clinical with 4 predictors... #> Fitting Full with 6 predictors... comparison #>  #> Model Comparison Results #> Outcome: os_status #> Model Type: glm #>  #> CMS Weights: #>   Convergence: 15% #>   AIC: 25% #>   Concordance: 40% #>   Pseudo-R²: 15% #>   Brier score: 5% #>  #> Recommended Model: Full (CMS: 73.2) #>  #> Models ranked by selection score: #>       Model   CMS     N Events Predictors Converged   AIC   BIC Pseudo-R² Concordance Brier Score Global p #>      <char> <num> <int>  <num>      <int>    <char> <num> <num>     <num>       <num>       <num>   <char> #> 1:     Full  73.2   833    594          6   Suspect 836.0 892.7 what: Create Forest Plot for Cox Proportional Hazards Models — coxforest
title: Create Forest Plot for Cox Proportional Hazards Models — coxforest
text: Generates publication-ready forest plot combines formatted data table graphical representation hazard ratios Cox proportional hazards survival model. plot integrates variable names, group levels, sample sizes, event counts, hazard ratios confidence intervals, p-values, model diagnostics single comprehensive visualization designed manuscripts presentations.

title: Create Forest Plot for Cox Proportional Hazards Models — coxforest
code: coxforest(   x,   data = NULL,   title = "Cox Proportional Hazards Model",   effect_label = "Hazard Ratio",   digits = 2,   p_digits = 3,   conf_level = 0.95,   font_size = 1,   annot_size = 3.88,   header_size = 5.82,   title_size = 23.28,   plot_width = NULL,   plot_height = NULL,   table_width = 0.6,   show_n = TRUE,   show_events = TRUE,   indent_groups = FALSE,   condense_table = FALSE,   bold_variables = FALSE,   center_padding = 4,   zebra_stripes = TRUE,   ref_label = "reference",   labels = NULL,   color = "#8A61D8",   qc_footer = TRUE,   units = "in",   number_format = NULL )

title: Create Forest Plot for Cox Proportional Hazards Models — coxforest
text: x Either fitted Cox model object (class coxph coxme), fit_result object fit(), fullfit_result object fullfit(). fit_result fullfit_result provided, model, data, labels automatically extracted. data Data frame data.table containing original data used fit model. NULL (default) x model, function attempts extract data model object. x fit_result, data extracted automatically. Providing data explicitly recommended passing model directly. title Character string specifying plot title displayed top. Default "Cox Proportional Hazards Model". Use descriptive titles publication. effect_label Character string effect measure label forest plot axis. Default "Hazard Ratio". Alternatives might include "HR" space-constrained plots. digits Integer specifying number decimal places hazard ratios confidence intervals. Default 2. p_digits Integer specifying number decimal places p-values. Values smaller 10^(-p_digits) displayed "< 0.001" (p_digits = 3), "< 0.0001" (p_digits = 4), etc. Default 3. conf_level Numeric confidence level confidence intervals. Must 0 1. Default 0.95 (95% confidence intervals). CI percentage automatically displayed column headers (e.g., "90% CI" conf_level = 0.90). font_size Numeric multiplier controlling base font size text elements. Default 1.0. annot_size Numeric value controlling relative font size data annotations. Default 3.88. header_size Numeric value controlling relative font size column headers. Default 5.82. title_size Numeric value controlling relative font size main plot title. Default 23.28. plot_width Numeric value specifying intended output width specified units. Used optimizing layout. Default NULL (automatic). Recommended: 10-16 inches. plot_height Numeric value specifying intended output height specified units. Default NULL (automatic based number rows). table_width Numeric value 0 1 specifying proportion total plot width allocated data table. Default 0.6 (60% table, 40% forest plot). show_n Logical. TRUE, includes column showing group-specific sample sizes. Default TRUE. show_events Logical. TRUE, includes column showing number events (deaths, failures) group. Critical survival analysis interpretation. Default TRUE. indent_groups Logical. TRUE, indents factor levels parent variable, creating hierarchical structure. "Group" column hidden TRUE. Default FALSE. condense_table Logical. TRUE, condenses binary variables show non-reference level (e.g., "Yes" Yes/variables), variable name indicating displayed level. creates compact table models many binary predictors. Default FALSE. bold_variables Logical. TRUE, variable names displayed bold. FALSE (default), variable names displayed plain text. center_padding Numeric value specifying horizontal spacing table forest plot. Default 4. zebra_stripes Logical. TRUE, applies alternating gray background shading different variables improved readability. Default TRUE. ref_label Character string display reference categories. Default "reference". labels Named character vector providing custom display labels variables. Example: c(age = "Age (years)", stage = "Disease Stage"). Default NULL. color Character string specifying color hazard ratio point estimates forest plot. Default "#8A61D8" (purple). Use hex codes R color names. qc_footer Logical. TRUE, displays model quality control statistics footer (events analyzed, global log-rank p-value, concordance index, AIC). Default TRUE. units Character string specifying units plot dimensions: "" (inches), "cm", "mm". Default "". number_format Character string two-element character vector   controlling thousand decimal separators formatted output. Named   presets: "us" - Comma thousands, period decimal: 1,234.56 [default] "eu" - Period thousands, comma decimal: 1.234,56 "space" - Thin-space thousands, period decimal: 1 234.56       (SI/ISO 31-0) "none" - thousands separator: 1234.56 provide custom two-element vector c(big.mark, decimal.mark),   e.g., c("'", ".") Swiss-style: 1'234.56. NULL (default), uses   getOption("summata.number_format", "us"). Set global option   per session avoid passing argument repeatedly:
code: options(summata.number_format = "eu")

title: Create Forest Plot for Cox Proportional Hazards Models — coxforest
text: ggplot object containing complete forest plot. plot   can : Displayed directly: print(plot) Saved file: ggsave("forest.pdf", plot, width = 12, height = 8) customized ggplot2 functions returned object includes attribute "rec_dims"   accessible via attr(plot, "rec_dims"), list   containing: width Numeric. Recommended plot width specified units height Numeric. Recommended plot height specified units recommendations automatically calculated based number   variables, text sizes, layout parameters, printed console   plot_width plot_height specified.

title: Create Forest Plot for Cox Proportional Hazards Models — coxforest
text: Survival-Specific Features: Cox forest plot includes several survival analysis-specific components: Event counts: Number events (deaths, failures) shown     predictor category, critical assessing statistical power Hazard ratios: Always exponentiated coefficients (never raw),     interpreted multiplicative change hazard Log scale: Forest plot uses log scale HR (reference line 1) Model diagnostics: Includes concordance (C-index), global     log-rank test p-value, AIC Plot Components: Title: Centered top Data Table (left): Contains: Variable Group columns n: Sample sizes group Events: Event counts group (critical survival) aHR (95% CI); p-value: Adjusted hazard ratios CIs p-values Forest Plot (right): Point estimates (squares sized sample size) 95% confidence intervals Reference line HR = 1 Log scale hazard ratios Model Statistics (footer): Events analyzed (percentage total) Global log-rank test p-value Concordance (C-index) standard error AIC Interpreting Hazard Ratios: HR = 1: effect hazard (reference) HR > 1: Increased hazard (worse survival) HR < 1: Decreased hazard (better survival) Example: HR = 2.0 means twice hazard event time Event Counts: "Events" column particularly important survival analysis: Indicates number actual events (censored observations)     group Essential assessing statistical power Categories events may unreliable HR estimates footer shows total events analyzed percentage events     original data Concordance (C-index): concordance statistic displayed footer indicates discrimination: Range: 0.5 1.0 0.5 = random prediction (coin flip) 0.7-0.8 = acceptable discrimination > 0.8 = excellent discrimination Standard error provided confidence interval calculation Global Log-Rank Test: global p-value tests null hypothesis coefficients zero: Significant p-value (< 0.05) indicates model whole predicts survival Non-significant global test preclude significant individual     predictors Based score (log-rank) test Stratification Clustering: model includes stratification (strata()) clustering (cluster()): Stratified variables shown forest plot (    HRs) Clustering affects standard errors point estimates handled automatically function Proportional Hazards Assumption: forest plot assumes proportional hazards (constant HR time). Users verify assumption using: cox.zph(model) testing Stratification variables violating assumption Time-dependent coefficients needed

title: Create Forest Plot for Cox Proportional Hazards Models — coxforest
code: if (FALSE) { # Load example data data(clintrial) data(clintrial_labels) library(survival)  # Example 1: Basic Cox model forest plot model1 <- coxph(Surv(os_months, os_status) ~ age + sex + treatment,                 data = clintrial)  plot1 <- coxforest(model1, data = clintrial) print(plot1)    options(width = 180) # Example 2: With custom labels and title plot2 <- coxforest(     x = model1,     data = clintrial,     title = "Prognostic Factors for Overall Survival",     labels = clintrial_labels ) print(plot2)  # Example 3: Comprehensive multivariable model model3 <- coxph(     Surv(os_months, os_status) ~ age + sex + bmi + smoking +          treatment + stage + grade,     data = clintrial )  plot3 <- coxforest(     x = model3,     data = clintrial,     labels = clintrial_labels,     indent_groups = TRUE ) print(plot3)  # Example 4: Condensed layout for many binary predictors model4 <- coxph(     Surv(os_months, os_status) ~ age + sex + smoking +          hypertension + diabetes + surgery,     data = clintrial )  plot4 <- coxforest(     x = model4,     data = clintrial,     condense_table = TRUE,     labels = clintrial_labels ) print(plot4)  # Example 5: Custom color scheme plot5 <- coxforest(     x = model1,     data = clintrial,     color = "#E74C3C",  # Red     zebra_stripes = FALSE,     labels = clintrial_labels ) print(plot5)  # Example 6: Hide sample sizes, show only events plot6 <- coxforest(     x = model1,     data = clintrial,     show_n = FALSE,     show_events = TRUE,     labels = clintrial_labels ) print(plot6)  # Example 7: Adjust table width plot7 <- coxforest(     x = model3,     data = clintrial,     table_width = 0.65,  # More space for long variable names     labels = clintrial_labels ) print(plot7)  # Example 8: Specify exact dimensions plot8 <- coxforest(     x = model1,     data = clintrial,     plot_width = 14,     plot_height = 8,     labels = clintrial_labels )  # Example 9: Use recommended dimensions for saving plot9 <- coxforest(model1, data = clintrial) dims <- attr(plot9, "rec_dims")  # Save to PDF # ggsave("survival_forest.pdf", plot9, #        width = dims$width, height = dims$height)  # Example 10: Different units (centimeters) plot10 <- coxforest(     x = model1,     data = clintrial,     plot_width = 35,     plot_height = 25,     units = "cm",     labels = clintrial_labels )  # Example 11: Stratified Cox model # Stratification variable (site) won't appear in plot model11 <- coxph(     Surv(os_months, os_status) ~ age + sex + treatment + strata(site),     data = clintrial )  plot11 <- coxforest(     x = model11,     data = clintrial,     title = "Stratified by Study Site",     labels = clintrial_labels ) print(plot11)  # Example 12: With clustering for robust SE model12 <- coxph(     Surv(os_months, os_status) ~ age + sex + treatment + cluster(site),     data = clintrial )  plot12 <- coxforest(     x = model12,     data = clintrial,     title = "Clustered by Study Site",     labels = clintrial_labels ) print(plot12) # Standard errors account for site clustering  # Example 13: Custom reference label plot13 <- coxforest(     x = model1,     data = clintrial,     ref_label = "1.00 (ref)",     labels = clintrial_labels ) print(plot13)  # Example 14: Presentation-sized fonts plot14 <- coxforest(     x = model1,     data = clintrial,     font_size = 1.4,     title_size = 28,     labels = clintrial_labels ) print(plot14)  # Example 15: Publication-ready final plot final_model <- coxph(     Surv(os_months, os_status) ~ age + sex + bmi + smoking +          hypertension + diabetes + ecog + treatment + stage + grade,     data = clintrial )  final_plot <- coxforest(     x = final_model,     data = clintrial,     title = "Multivariable Cox Regression: Prognostic Factors for Overall Survival",     labels = clintrial_labels,     indent_groups = TRUE,     zebra_stripes = TRUE,     show_n = TRUE,     show_events = TRUE,     color = "#8A61D8" )  # Check model fit summary(final_model)  # Verify proportional hazards assumption # cox.zph(final_model)  # Save for publication dims <- attr(final_plot, "rec_dims") # ggsave("figure2_survival.pdf", final_plot, #        width = dims$width, height = dims$height) # ggsave("figure2_survival.png", final_plot, #        width = dims$width, height = dims$height, dpi = 300) }

what: Create Publication-Ready Descriptive Statistics Tables — desctable
title: Create Publication-Ready Descriptive Statistics Tables — desctable
text: Generates comprehensive descriptive statistics tables automatic variable type detection, group comparisons, appropriate statistical testing. function designed create "Table 1"-style summaries commonly used clinical epidemiological research, full support continuous, categorical, time--event variables.

title: Create Publication-Ready Descriptive Statistics Tables — desctable
code: desctable(   data,   by = NULL,   variables,   stats_continuous = c("median_iqr"),   stats_categorical = "n_percent",   digits = 1,   p_digits = 3,   conf_level = 0.95,   p_per_stat = FALSE,   na_include = FALSE,   na_label = "Unknown",   na_percent = FALSE,   test = TRUE,   test_continuous = "auto",   test_categorical = "auto",   total = TRUE,   total_label = "Total",   labels = NULL,   number_format = NULL,   ... )

title: Create Publication-Ready Descriptive Statistics Tables — desctable
text: data Data frame data.table containing dataset summarize. Automatically converted data.table efficient processing. Character string specifying column name grouping variable stratified analysis (e.g., treatment arm, exposure status). NULL (default), produces overall summaries without group comparisons statistical tests. variables Character vector variable names summarize. Can include standard column names continuous categorical variables, survival expressions using Surv() syntax (e.g., "Surv(os_months, os_status)"). Variables processed order provided. stats_continuous Character vector specifying statistics compute continuous variables. Multiple values create separate rows variable. Options: "mean_sd" - Mean ± standard deviation "median_iqr" - Median [interquartile range] "median_range" - Median (minimum–maximum) "range" - Minimum–maximum Default "median_iqr". stats_categorical Character string specifying format categorical variable summaries: "n" - Count "percent" - Percentage "n_percent" - Count (percentage) [default] digits Integer specifying number decimal places continuous statistics. Default 1. p_digits Integer specifying number decimal places p-values. Values smaller 10^(-p_digits) displayed "< 0.001" (p_digits = 3), "< 0.0001" (p_digits = 4), etc. Default 3. conf_level Numeric confidence level confidence intervals survival variable summaries (median survival time CI). Must 0 1. Default 0.95 (95% confidence intervals). p_per_stat Logical. TRUE, displays p-values row (per statistic) rather first row variable. Useful different statistics within variable warrant separate significance testing. Default FALSE. na_include Logical. TRUE, missing values (NAs) displayed separate category/row variable. FALSE, missing values silently excluded calculations. Default FALSE. na_label Character string used label missing values row na_include = TRUE. Default "Unknown". na_percent Logical. Controls percentages calculated categorical variables na_include = TRUE: TRUE, percentages include NAs denominator (    categories sum 100%) FALSE, percentages exclude NAs denominator     (non-missing categories sum 100%, missing shown separately) affects categorical variables. Default FALSE. test Logical. TRUE, performs appropriate statistical tests group comparisons adds p-value column. Requires specified. Tests automatically selected based variable type test parameters. Default TRUE. test_continuous Character string specifying statistical test continuous variables: "auto" - Automatic selection: t-test/ANOVA means,     Wilcoxon/Kruskal-Wallis medians [default] "t" - Independent samples t-test (2 groups ) "aov" - One-way ANOVA (2+ groups) "wrs" - Wilcoxon rank-sum test (2 groups ) "kwt" - Kruskal-Wallis test (2+ groups) test_categorical Character string specifying statistical test categorical variables: "auto" - Automatic selection: Fisher exact test     expected cell frequency < 5, otherwise χ² test [default] "fisher" - Fisher exact test "chisq" - χ² test total Logical character string controlling total column: TRUE "first" - Include total column first     column Variable/Group [default] "last" - Include total column last column     p-value FALSE - Exclude total column total_label Character string total column header. Default "Total". labels Named character vector list providing custom display labels variables. Names match variable names (Surv() expressions), values display labels. Variables labels use original names. Can also label grouping variable specified . Default NULL. number_format Character string two-element character vector   controlling thousand decimal separators formatted output. Named   presets: "us" - Comma thousands, period decimal: 1,234.56 [default] "eu" - Period thousands, comma decimal: 1.234,56 "space" - Thin-space thousands, period decimal: 1 234.56       (SI/ISO 31-0) "none" - thousands separator: 1234.56 provide custom two-element vector c(big.mark, decimal.mark),   e.g., c("'", ".") Swiss-style: 1'234.56. NULL (default), uses   getOption("summata.number_format", "us"). Set global option   per session avoid passing argument repeatedly: ... Additional arguments passed underlying statistical test functions (e.g., var.equal = TRUE t-tests, simulate.p.value = TRUE Fisher test).
code: options(summata.number_format = "eu")

title: Create Publication-Ready Descriptive Statistics Tables — desctable
text: data.table S3 class "desctable" containing formatted   descriptive statistics. table structure includes: Variable Variable name label (labels) Group continuous variables: statistic type (e.g.,       "Mean ± SD", "Median [IQR]"). categorical variables:       category level. Empty variable name rows. Total Statistics total sample (      total = TRUE) Group columns Statistics group level (      specified). Column names match group levels. p-value Formatted p-values statistical tests       (test = TRUE specified) first row always shows sample sizes column. numeric   output (counts, statistics, p-values) respects   number_format setting locale-appropriate formatting. returned object includes following attributes accessible via   attr(): raw_data data.table containing unformatted numeric values       suitable statistical analysis custom formatting.       Includes additional columns standard deviations, quartiles,       etc. by_variable grouping variable name used (value       ) variables variables analyzed (value       variables)

title: Create Publication-Ready Descriptive Statistics Tables — desctable
text: Variable Type Detection: function automatically detects variable types applies appropriate summaries: Continuous: Numeric variables (integer double) receive     statistics specified stats_continuous Categorical: Character, factor, logical variables     receive frequency counts percentages Time--Event: Variables specified     Surv(time, event) display median survival confidence     intervals (level controlled conf_level) Statistical Testing: test = TRUE specified: Continuous "auto": Parametric tests (t-test, ANOVA)     mean-based statistics; non-parametric tests (Wilcoxon,     Kruskal-Wallis) median-based statistics Categorical "auto": Fisher exact test     expected cell frequency < 5; χ² test otherwise Survival: Log-rank test comparing survival curves Range statistics: p-value computed (ranges     descriptive) Missing Data Handling: Missing values handled differently variable type: Continuous: NAs excluded calculations; optionally     shown count na_include = TRUE Categorical: NAs can included category     na_include = TRUE. na_percent parameter controls     whether percentages calculated without NAs     denominator Survival: NAs time event excluded analysis Formatting Conventions: numeric output respects number_format parameter. Separators within ranges confidence intervals adapt automatically avoid ambiguity: Mean ± SD: "45.2 ± 12.3" (US)     "45,2 ± 12,3" (EU) Median [IQR]: "38.0 [28.0-52.0]" (US)     "38,0 [28,0–52,0]" (EU, en-dash separator) Range: "18.0-75.0" (positive, US),     "-5.0 10.0" (bounds negative) Survival: "24.5 (21.2-28.9)" (US)     "24,5 (21,2–28,9)" (EU) Counts ≥ 1000: "1,234" (US) "1.234" (EU) p-values: "< 0.001" (US) "< 0,001" (EU)

what: Detect if model is univariable or multivariable — detect_model_type
title: Detect if model is univariable or multivariable — detect_model_type
text: Determines whether model contains one predictor (univariable) multiple predictors (multivariable) analyzing coefficient names factor structure. Handles interactions random effects appropriately.

title: Detect if model is univariable or multivariable — detect_model_type
code: detect_model_type(model)

title: Detect if model is univariable or multivariable — detect_model_type
text: model Fitted model object.

title: Detect if model is univariable or multivariable — detect_model_type
text: Character string: "Univariable" "Multivariable".

what: Auto-detect model type based on outcome and random effects — detect_model_type_auto
title: Auto-detect model type based on outcome and random effects — detect_model_type_auto
text: Determines appropriate model type based outcome variable characteristics presence random effects.

title: Auto-detect model type based on outcome and random effects — detect_model_type_auto
code: detect_model_type_auto(data, outcome, has_random_effects, family = "binomial")

title: Auto-detect model type based on outcome and random effects — detect_model_type_auto
text: data Data.frame data.table containing outcome variable. outcome Character string specifying outcome variable Surv() expression. has_random_effects Logical indicating random effects specified. family Character string GLM family (default "binomial").

title: Auto-detect model type based on outcome and random effects — detect_model_type_auto
text: Character string indicating detected model type.

what: Detect outcome type from data — detect_outcome_type
title: Detect outcome type from data — detect_outcome_type
text: Automatically determines whether outcome variable binary, continuous, count-based examining data values. Used automatic model type selection validation. Binary outcomes 2 unique values, continuous many values non-integers, counts integers >= 0.

title: Detect outcome type from data — detect_outcome_type
code: detect_outcome_type(data, outcome)

title: Detect outcome type from data — detect_outcome_type
text: data Data frame data.table containing outcome variable. outcome Character string naming outcome variable.

title: Detect outcome type from data — detect_outcome_type
text: Character string: "binary", "continuous", "count", "unknown".

what: Detect available sans-serif font for plots — detect_plot_font
title: Detect available sans-serif font for plots — detect_plot_font
text: Checks commonly available sans-serif fonts order preference (Helvetica, Arial, Helvetica Neue) returns first available one. Falls back "sans" none found systemfonts unavailable.

title: Detect available sans-serif font for plots — detect_plot_font
code: detect_plot_font()

title: Detect available sans-serif font for plots — detect_plot_font
text: Character string font family name use.

title: Detect available sans-serif font for plots — detect_plot_font
text: ragg used graphics device (detected via options knitr settings), font detection works non-interactive sessions since ragg handles font rendering independently R graphics system.

what: Determine alignment for exported tables — determine_alignment
title: Determine alignment for exported tables — determine_alignment
text: Creates column alignment string LaTeX tables. Variable Group columns left-aligned; others centered.

title: Determine alignment for exported tables — determine_alignment
code: determine_alignment(df)

title: Determine alignment for exported tables — determine_alignment
text: df Data.frame data.table determine alignment .

title: Determine alignment for exported tables — determine_alignment
text: Character string alignment codes (e.g., "rlcc").

title: Determine Effect Type Label — determine_effect_type
text: Identifies appropriate effect measure label (, HR, RR, Coefficient, aOR, aHR, aRR, Adj. Coefficient) based model type, exponentiation setting, whether estimate adjusted (multivariable) unadjusted (univariable).

title: Determine Effect Type Label — determine_effect_type
code: determine_effect_type(uni_raw, multi_raw, exponentiate, adjusted = FALSE)

title: Determine Effect Type Label — determine_effect_type
text: uni_raw Raw univariable data.table containing coefficient columns. Used detect effect type adjusted = FALSE. multi_raw Raw multivariable data.table containing coefficient columns. Used detect effect type adjusted = TRUE. exponentiate Logical NULL controlling label selection: TRUE Force exponentiated labels (, HR, RR, Exp(Coef)) FALSE Force coefficient labels (Coefficient, Adj. Coefficient) NULL Auto-detect column names raw data adjusted Logical. TRUE, returns adjusted effect labels (aOR, aHR, aRR, Adj. Coefficient) multivariable results. FALSE, returns unadjusted labels (, HR, RR, Coefficient) univariable results.

title: Determine Effect Type Label — determine_effect_type
text: Character string effect measure label:

what: Extract metrics for mixed-effects Cox models (coxme) — extract_coxme_metrics
title: Extract metrics for mixed-effects Cox models (coxme) — extract_coxme_metrics
text: Extracts quality metrics specific mixed-effects Cox proportional hazards models including concordance, pseudo-R-squared, ICC.

title: Extract metrics for mixed-effects Cox models (coxme) — extract_coxme_metrics
code: extract_coxme_metrics(model, raw_data, metrics)

title: Extract metrics for mixed-effects Cox models (coxme) — extract_coxme_metrics
text: model Fitted coxme object coxme package. raw_data Data.table raw model information. metrics Named list initialized metrics populate.

title: Extract metrics for mixed-effects Cox models (coxme) — extract_coxme_metrics

what: Fit Regression Model with Publication-Ready Output — fit
title: Fit Regression Model with Publication-Ready Output — fit
text: Provides unified interface fitting various types regression models automatic formatting results publication. Supports generalized linear models, linear models, survival models, mixed-effects models consistent syntax output formatting. Handles univariable multivariable models automatically.

title: Fit Regression Model with Publication-Ready Output — fit
code: fit(   data = NULL,   outcome = NULL,   predictors = NULL,   model = NULL,   model_type = "glm",   family = "binomial",   random = NULL,   interactions = NULL,   strata = NULL,   cluster = NULL,   weights = NULL,   conf_level = 0.95,   reference_rows = TRUE,   show_n = TRUE,   show_events = TRUE,   digits = 2,   p_digits = 3,   labels = NULL,   keep_qc_stats = TRUE,   exponentiate = NULL,   number_format = NULL,   verbose = NULL,   ... )

title: Fit Regression Model with Publication-Ready Output — fit
text: data Data frame data.table containing analysis dataset. Required formula-based workflow; optional model-based workflow (extracted model provided). outcome Character string specifying outcome variable name. survival analysis, use Surv() syntax survival package (e.g., "Surv(time, status)" "Surv(os_months, os_status)"). Required formula-based workflow; ignored model provided. predictors Character vector predictor variable names include model. predictors included simultaneously (multivariable model). univariable models, provide single predictor. Can include continuous, categorical (factor), binary variables. Required formula-based workflow; ignored model provided. model Optional pre-fitted model object format. provided, outcome predictors ignored model formatted directly. Supported model classes include: glm - Generalized linear models negbin - Negative binomial models MASS::glm.nb() lm - Linear models coxph - Cox proportional hazards models lmerMod, glmerMod - Mixed-effects models lme4 coxme - Mixed-effects Cox models model_type Character string specifying type regression model. Ignored model provided. Options include: "glm" - Generalized linear model (default). Supports multiple     distributions via family parameter including logistic, Poisson,     Gamma, Gaussian, quasi-likelihood models. "lm" - Linear regression continuous outcomes normally     distributed errors. Equivalent glm family = "gaussian". "coxph" - Cox proportional hazards model time--event     survival analysis. Requires Surv() outcome syntax. "clogit" - Conditional logistic regression matched     case-control studies stratified analyses. "negbin" - Negative binomial regression overdispersed count     data (requires MASS package). Estimates additional dispersion parameter     compared Poisson regression. "lmer" - Linear mixed-effects model hierarchical clustered     data continuous outcomes (requires lme4 package). "glmer" - Generalized linear mixed-effects model hierarchical     clustered data non-normal outcomes (requires lme4 package). "coxme" - Cox mixed-effects model clustered survival data     (requires coxme package). family GLM GLMER models, specifies error distribution link function. Can character string, family function, family object. Ignored non-GLM/GLMER models. Binary/Binomial outcomes: "binomial" binomial() - Logistic regression binary     outcomes (0/1, TRUE/FALSE). Returns odds ratios (). Default. "quasibinomial" quasibinomial() - Logistic regression     overdispersion. Use residual deviance >> residual df. binomial(link = "probit") - Probit regression (normal CDF link). binomial(link = "cloglog") - Complementary log-log link     asymmetric binary outcomes. Count outcomes: "poisson" poisson() - Poisson regression count     data. Returns rate ratios (RR). Assumes mean = variance. "quasipoisson" quasipoisson() - Poisson regression     overdispersion. Use variance > mean. Continuous outcomes: "gaussian" gaussian() - Normal/Gaussian distribution     continuous outcomes. Equivalent linear regression. gaussian(link = "log") - Log-linear model positive continuous     outcomes. Returns multiplicative effects. gaussian(link = "inverse") - Inverse link specific applications. Positive continuous outcomes: "Gamma" Gamma() - Gamma distribution positive,     right-skewed continuous data (e.g., costs, lengths stay). Default log link. Gamma(link = "inverse") - Gamma inverse (canonical) link. Gamma(link = "identity") - Gamma identity link additive     effects positive outcomes. "inverse.gaussian" inverse.gaussian() - Inverse Gaussian     positive, highly right-skewed data. negative binomial regression (overdispersed counts), use model_type = "negbin" instead family parameter. See family additional details options. random Character string specifying random-effects formula mixed-effects models (glmer, lmer, coxme). Use standard lme4/coxme syntax, e.g., "(1|site)" random intercepts site, "(1|site/patient)" nested random effects, "(1|site) + (1|doctor)" crossed random effects. Required model_type mixed-effects model type. Alternatively, random effects can included directly predictors vector using syntax. Default NULL. interactions Character vector interaction terms using colon notation (e.g., c("age:sex", "treatment:stage")). Interaction terms added model addition main effects. Default NULL (interactions). strata Cox conditional logistic models, character string naming stratification variable. Creates separate baseline hazards stratum level without estimating stratum effects. Default NULL. cluster Cox models, character string naming variable robust clustered standard errors. Accounts within-cluster correlation (e.g., patients within hospitals). Default NULL. weights Character string naming weights variable data. specified column contain numeric values observation weights. Used weighted regression, survey data, inverse probability weighting. Default NULL. conf_level Numeric confidence level confidence intervals. Must 0 1. Default 0.95 (95% confidence intervals). reference_rows Logical. TRUE, adds rows reference categories factor variables baseline values (/HR/RR = 1, coefficient = 0). Default TRUE. show_n Logical. TRUE, includes sample size column output. Default TRUE. show_events Logical. TRUE, includes events column output (survival logistic regression). Default TRUE. digits Integer specifying number decimal places effect estimates (, HR, RR, coefficients). Default 2. p_digits Integer specifying number decimal places p-values. Values smaller 10^(-p_digits) displayed "< 0.001" (p_digits = 3), "< 0.0001" (p_digits = 4), etc. Default 3. labels Named character vector list providing custom display labels variables. Names match variable names, values display labels. Default NULL. keep_qc_stats Logical. TRUE, includes model quality statistics (AIC, BIC, R-squared, concordance, etc.) raw data attribute model diagnostics comparison. Default TRUE. exponentiate Logical. Whether exponentiate coefficients. Default NULL, automatically exponentiates logistic, Poisson, Cox models, displays raw coefficients linear models. Set TRUE force exponentiation FALSE force coefficients. number_format Character string two-element character vector   controlling thousand decimal separators formatted output. Named   presets: "us" - Comma thousands, period decimal: 1,234.56 [default] "eu" - Period thousands, comma decimal: 1.234,56 "space" - Thin-space thousands, period decimal: 1 234.56       (SI/ISO 31-0) "none" - thousands separator: 1234.56 provide custom two-element vector c(big.mark, decimal.mark),   e.g., c("'", ".") Swiss-style: 1'234.56. NULL (default), uses   getOption("summata.number_format", "us"). Set global option   per session avoid passing argument repeatedly: verbose Logical. TRUE, displays model fitting warnings (e.g., singular fit, convergence issues). FALSE (default), routine fitting messages suppressed unexpected warnings preserved. NULL, uses getOption("summata.verbose", FALSE). ... Additional arguments passed underlying model fitting function (glm, lm, coxph, glmer, etc.). Common options include subset, na.action, model-specific control parameters.
code: options(summata.number_format = "eu")

title: Fit Regression Model with Publication-Ready Output — fit
text: data.table S3 class "fit_result" containing formatted   regression results. table structure includes: Variable Character. Predictor name custom label Group Character. factor variables: category level.       interactions: interaction term. continuous: typically empty n Integer. Total sample size (show_n = TRUE) n_group Integer. Sample size factor level events Integer. Total number events (show_events = TRUE) events_group Integer. Events factor level /HR/RR/Coefficient aOR/aHR/aRR/Adj. Coefficient (95% CI) Character.       Formatted effect estimate confidence interval. Column name depends       model type scope. Univariable models use: , HR, RR, Coefficient.       Multivariable models use adjusted notation: aOR, aHR, aRR, Adj. Coefficient p-value Character. Formatted p-value Wald test returned object includes following attributes accessible via attr(): model fitted model object (glm, lm, coxph, etc.). Access       diagnostics, predictions, analysis raw_data data.table. Unformatted numeric results columns       coefficients, standard errors, confidence bounds, quality statistics, etc. outcome Character. outcome variable name predictors Character vector. predictor variable names formula_str Character. complete model formula string model_scope Character. "Univariable" (one predictor)       "Multivariable" (multiple predictors) model_type Character. regression model type used interactions Character vector (interactions specified).       interaction terms included strata Character (stratification used). stratification variable cluster Character (clustering used). cluster variable weights Character (weighting used). weights variable significant Character vector. Names predictors p-value       0.05, suitable downstream variable selection workflows

title: Fit Regression Model with Publication-Ready Output — fit
text: Model Scope Detection: function automatically detects whether model : Univariable: Single predictor (e.g., predictors = "age").       Effect estimates labeled unadjusted ("", "HR", etc.), representing       crude (unadjusted) association Multivariable: Multiple predictors (e.g.,     predictors = c("age", "sex", "treatment"))       Effect estimates labeled adjusted ("aOR", "aHR", etc.), representing       associations adjusted confounding Interaction Terms: Interactions specified using colon notation added model: interactions = c("age:treatment") creates interaction     age treatment Main effects variables automatically included Multiple interactions can specified:     c("age:sex", "treatment:stage") interactions categorical variables, separate terms     created combination levels Stratification (Cox/Conditional Logistic): strata parameter creates separate baseline hazards: Allows baseline hazard vary across strata without estimating     stratum effects Useful proportional hazards assumption violated across strata Example: strata = "center" multicenter studies Stratification variable included predictor Clustering (Cox Models): cluster parameter computes robust standard errors: Accounts within-cluster correlation (e.g., multiple observations     per patient) Uses sandwich variance estimator change point estimates, standard errors p-values Weighting: weights parameter enables weighted regression: survey data sampling weights Inverse probability weighting causal inference Frequency weights aggregated data Weights column data Mixed-Effects Models (lmer/glmer/coxme): Mixed effects models handle hierarchical clustered data: Use model_type = "lmer" continuous/normal outcomes Use model_type = "glmer" appropriate family GLM outcomes Use model_type = "coxme" survival outcomes clustering Random effects specified predictors using lme4 syntax: "(1|site)" - Random intercepts site "(treatment|site)" - Random slopes treatment site "(1 + treatment|site)" - random intercepts slopes Include random effects part predictors vector Example: predictors = c("age", "treatment", "(1|site)") Effect Measures Model Type: Logistic (family = "binomial"/"quasibinomial"): Odds ratios (/aOR) Cox (model_type = "coxph"): Hazard ratios (HR/aHR) Poisson/Count (family = "poisson"/"quasipoisson"): Rate ratios (RR/aRR) Negative binomial (model_type = "negbin"): Rate ratios (RR/aRR) Gamma/Log-link: Ratios (multiplicative effects) Linear/Gaussian: Raw coefficient estimates (additive effects) Confidence Intervals: Confidence intervals computed using Wald method: β̂ ± z(1−α/2) × SE(β̂) standard approach GLM, Cox, mixed-effects regression, appropriate standard sample sizes. Wald interval computed directly coefficient standard error, avoiding redundant matrix operations. small samples, sparse data, parameters near boundary values, profile likelihood confidence intervals may preferred. can obtained underlying model object:
code: result <- fit(data, outcome, predictors) confint(attr(result, "model"))

title: Fit Regression Model with Publication-Ready Output — fit
code:
# Load example data data(clintrial) data(clintrial_labels) library(survival)  # Example 1: Univariable logistic regression uni_model <- fit(     data = clintrial,     outcome = "os_status",     predictors = "age" ) print(uni_model) #>  #> Univariable Logistic Model #> Formula: os_status ~ age #> n = 850, Events = 609 #>  #>    Variable  Group      n Events      OR (95% CI) p-value #>      <char> <char> <char> <char>           <char>  <char> #> 1:      age      -    850    609 1.05 (1.03-1.06) < 0.001 # Labeled as "Univariable OR"  # \donttest{ # Example 2: Multivariable logistic regression multi_model <- fit(     data = clintrial,     outcome = "os_status",     predictors = c("age", "sex", "bmi", "treatment"),     labels = clintrial_labels ) print(multi_model) #>  #> Multivariable Logistic Model #> Formula: os_status ~ age + sex + bmi + treatment #> n = 838, Events = 599 #>  #>                   Variable   Group      n Events     aOR (95% CI) p-value #>                     <char>  <char> <char> <char>           <char>  <char> #> 1:             Age (years)       -    838    599 1.05 (1.04-1.07) < 0.001 #> 2:                     Sex  Female    445    294        reference       - #> 3:                            Male    393    305 1.86 (1.35-2.56) < 0.001 #> 4: Body Mass Index (kg/m²)       -    838    599 1.03 (0.99-1.06)   0.115 #> 5:         Treatment Group Control    194    149        reference       - #> 6:                          Drug A    288    181 0.48 (0.31-0.73) < 0.001 #> 7:                          Drug B    356    269 0.86 (0.56-1.32)   0.494  # Example 3: Cox proportional hazards model cox_model <- fit(     data = clintrial,     outcome = "Surv(os_months, os_status)",     predictors = c("age", "sex", "treatment", "stage"),     model_type = "coxph",     labels = clintrial_labels ) print(cox_model) #>  #> Multivariable Cox PH Model #> Formula: Surv(os_months, os_status) ~ age + sex + treatment + stage #> n = 847, Events = 606 #>  #>            Variable   Group      n Events     aHR (95% CI) p-value #>              <char>  <char> <char> <char>           <char>  <char> #>  1:     Age (years)       -    847    606 1.04 (1.03-1.04) < 0.001 #>  2:             Sex  Female    450    298        reference       - #>  3:                    Male    400    311 1.33 (1.13-1.56) < 0.001 #>  4: Treatment Group Control    196    151        reference       - #>  5:                  Drug A    292    184 0.56 (0.45-0.70) < 0.001 #>  6:                  Drug B    362    274 0.83 (0.67-1.01)   0.062 #>  7:   Disease Stage       I    211    127        reference       - #>  8:                      II    263    172 1.16 (0.92-1.46)   0.214 #>  9:                     III    241    186 1.90 (1.51-2.38) < 0.001 #> 10:                      IV    132    121 3.57 (2.77-4.60) < 0.001  # Example 4: Model with interaction terms interact_model <- fit(     data = clintrial,     outcome = "os_status",     predictors = c("age", "treatment", "sex"),     interactions = c("age:treatment"),     labels = clintrial_labels ) print(interact_model) #>  #> Multivariable Logistic Model #> Formula: os_status ~ age + treatment + sex + age:treatment #> n = 850, Events = 609 #>  #>                                  Variable   Group      n Events     aOR (95% CI) p-value #>                                    <char>  <char> <char> <char>           <char>  <char> #> 1:                            Age (years)       -    850    609 1.04 (1.01-1.07)   0.012 #> 2:                        Treatment Group Control    196    151        reference       - #> 3:                                         Drug A    292    184 0.48 (0.06-4.21)   0.511 #> 4:                                         Drug B    362    274 0.18 (0.02-1.68)   0.133 #> 5:                                    Sex  Female    450    298        reference       - #> 6:                                           Male    400    311 1.86 (1.35-2.56) < 0.001 #> 7: Age (years) × Treatment Group (Drug A)       -    292    184 1.00 (0.96-1.04)   0.993 #> 8: Age (years) × Treatment Group (Drug B)       -    362    274 1.03 (0.99-1.07)   0.164  # Example 5: Cox model with stratification strat_model <- fit(     data = clintrial,     outcome = "Surv(os_months, os_status)",     predictors = c("age", "sex", "treatment"),     model_type = "coxph",     strata = "site",  # Separate baseline hazards by site     labels = clintrial_labels ) print(strat_model) #>  #> Multivariable Cox PH Model #> Formula: Surv(os_months, os_status) ~ age + sex + treatment + strata( site ) #> n = 850, Events = 609 #>  #>           Variable   Group      n Events     aHR (95% CI) p-value #>             <char>  <char> <char> <char>           <char>  <char> #> 1:     Age (years)       -    850    609 1.04 (1.03-1.04) < 0.001 #> 2:             Sex  Female    450    298        reference       - #> 3:                    Male    400    311 1.28 (1.09-1.50)   0.003 #> 4: Treatment Group Control    196    151        reference       - #> 5:                  Drug A    292    184 0.62 (0.50-0.77) < 0.001 #> 6:                  Drug B    362    274 1.00 (0.81-1.22)   0.976  # Example 6: Cox model with clustering cluster_model <- fit(     data = clintrial,     outcome = "Surv(os_months, os_status)",     predictors = c("age", "treatment"),     model_type = "coxph",     cluster = "site",  # Robust SEs accounting for site clustering     labels = clintrial_labels ) print(cluster_model) #>  #> Multivariable Cox PH Model #> Formula: Surv(os_months, os_status) ~ age + treatment #> n = 850, Events = 609 #>  #>           Variable   Group      n Events     aHR (95% CI) p-value #>             <char>  <char> <char> <char>           <char>  <char> #> 1:     Age (years)       -    850    609 1.03 (1.03-1.04) < 0.001 #> 2: Treatment Group Control    196    151        reference       - #> 3:                  Drug A    292    184 0.61 (0.45-0.83)   0.001 #> 4:                  Drug B    362    274 0.91 (0.68-1.22)   0.534  # Example 7: Linear regression linear_model <- fit(     data = clintrial,     outcome = "bmi",     predictors = c("age", "sex", "smoking"),     model_type = "lm",     labels = clintrial_labels ) print(linear_model) #>  #> Multivariable Linear Model #> Formula: bmi ~ age + sex + smoking #> n = 833 #>  #>          Variable   Group      n Adj. Coefficient (95% CI) p-value #>            <char>  <char> <char>                    <char>  <char> #> 1:    Age (years)       -    833     -0.02 (-0.05 to 0.01)   0.141 #> 2:            Sex  Female    443                 reference       - #> 3:                   Male    390     -0.33 (-1.00 to 0.34)   0.337 #> 4: Smoking Status   Never    337                 reference       - #> 5:                 Former    311      0.15 (-0.61 to 0.92)   0.692 #> 6:                Current    185      0.25 (-0.63 to 1.14)   0.577  # Example 8: Poisson regression for equidispersed count data # fu_count has variance ≈ mean, appropriate for standard Poisson poisson_model <- fit(     data = clintrial,     outcome = "fu_count",     predictors = c("age", "stage", "treatment", "surgery"),     model_type = "glm",     family = "poisson",     labels = clintrial_labels ) print(poisson_model) #>  #> Multivariable Poisson Model #> Formula: fu_count ~ age + stage + treatment + surgery #> n = 839, Events = 5517 #>  #>               Variable   Group      n Events     aRR (95% CI) p-value #>                 <char>  <char> <char> <char>           <char>  <char> #>  1:        Age (years)       - #>  7:                     Drug A    290  1,910 1.06 (0.99-1.14)   0.101 #>  8:                     Drug B    358  2,438 1.12 (1.04-1.20)   0.002 #>  9: Surgical Resection      No    476  3,091        reference       - #> 10:                        Yes    363  2,426 1.09 (1.03-1.16)   0.003 # Returns rate ratios (RR/aRR)  # Example 9: Negative binomial regression for overdispersed counts # ae_count has variance > mean (overdispersed), use negbin or quasipoisson if (requireNamespace("MASS", quietly = TRUE)) {     nb_result <- fit(         data = clintrial,         outcome = "ae_count",         predictors = c("age", "treatment", "diabetes", "surgery"),         model_type = "negbin",         labels = clintrial_labels     )     print(nb_result) } #>  #> Multivariable Negative Binomial Model #> Formula: ae_count ~ age + treatment + diabetes + surgery #> n = 824, Events = 4506 #>  #>              Variable   Group      n Events     aRR (95% CI) p-value #>                <char>  <char> <char> <char>           <char>  <char> #> 1:        Age (years)       -
