text: ggplot object containing complete forest plot. plot   can : Displayed directly: print(plot) Saved file: ggsave("forest.pdf", plot, width = 12, height = 8) customized ggplot2 functions returned object includes attribute "rec_dims"   accessible via attr(plot, "rec_dims"), list   containing: width Numeric. Recommended plot width specified units height Numeric. Recommended plot height specified units recommendations automatically calculated based number   variables, text sizes, layout parameters, printed console   plot_width plot_height specified.

title: Create Forest Plot for Linear Models — lmforest
text: Linear Model-Specific Features: linear model forest plot differs logistic Cox plots several ways: Coefficients: Raw regression coefficients shown (exponentiated) Reference line: coefficient = 0 (1) Linear scale: Forest plot uses linear scale (log scale) events column: sample sizes shown (event counts) R² statistics: Model fit assessed R² adjusted R² F-test: Overall model significance F-statistic Plot Components: Title: Centered top Data Table (left): Contains: Variable: Predictor names Group: Factor levels (applicable) n: Sample sizes group Coefficient (95% CI); p-value: Raw coefficients CIs p-values Forest Plot (right): Point estimates (squares sized sample size) 95% confidence intervals (error bars) Reference line coefficient = 0 Linear scale Model Statistics (footer): Observations analyzed (percentage total data) R² adjusted R² F-statistic degrees freedom p-value AIC Interpreting Coefficients: Linear regression coefficients represent change outcome variable one-unit change predictor: Continuous predictors: Coefficient = change Y per unit X Binary predictors: Coefficient = difference Y groups Factor predictors: Coefficients = differences reference     category Sign matters: Positive = increase Y, Negative = decrease Y Zero crossing: CI crossing zero suggests significant effect Example: coefficient "age" 0.50 predicting BMI, BMI increases 0.50 kg/m² additional year age. Model Fit Statistics: footer displays key diagnostics: R²: Proportion variance explained (0 1) 0.0-0.3: Weak explanatory power 0.3-0.5: Moderate 0.5-0.7: Good > 0.7: Strong (rare social/biological sciences) Adjusted R²: R² penalized number predictors Always ≤ R² Preferred model comparison Accounts model complexity F-statistic: Tests null hypothesis coefficients = 0 Degrees freedom: df1 = # predictors, df2 = # observations - # predictors - 1 Significant p-value indicates model explains variance better intercept-AIC: model comparison (lower better) Assumptions: Linear regression assumes: Linearity relationships Independence observations Homoscedasticity (constant variance) Normality residuals multicollinearity Check assumptions using: plot(model) diagnostic plots car::vif(model) multicollinearity lmtest::bptest(model) heteroscedasticity shapiro.test(residuals(model)) normality Reference Categories: factor variables: First level reference (coefficient = 0) levels show difference reference Reference displayed ref_label Relevel factors modeling needed:     factor(x, levels = c("desired_ref", ...)) Sample Size Reporting: "n" column shows: continuous variables: Total observations non-missing data factor variables: Number observations category Footer shows total observations analyzed percentage original     data (accounting missing values)

title: Create Forest Plot for Linear Models — lmforest
code: if (FALSE) { # Load example data data(clintrial) data(clintrial_labels)  # Example 1: Basic linear model forest plot model1 <- lm(bmi ~ age + sex + smoking,              data = clintrial)  plot1 <- lmforest(model1, data = clintrial) print(plot1)    options(width = 180) # Example 2: With custom labels and title plot2 <- lmforest(     x = model1,     data = clintrial,     title = "Predictors of Body Mass Index",     effect_label = "Change in BMI (kg/m²)",     labels = clintrial_labels ) print(plot2)  # Example 3: More comprehensive model model3 <- lm(     bmi ~ age + sex + smoking + hypertension + diabetes + creatinine,     data = clintrial )  plot3 <- lmforest(     x = model3,     data = clintrial,     labels = clintrial_labels,     indent_groups = TRUE ) print(plot3)  # Example 4: Check model diagnostics summary(model3) plot(model3)  # Diagnostic plots  # Example 5: Condensed layout model5 <- lm(     bmi ~ age + sex + smoking + hypertension + diabetes,     data = clintrial )  plot5 <- lmforest(     x = model5,     data = clintrial,     condense_table = TRUE,     labels = clintrial_labels ) print(plot5)  # Example 6: Custom color plot6 <- lmforest(     x = model1,     data = clintrial,     color = "#2ECC71",  # Bright green     labels = clintrial_labels ) print(plot6)  # Example 7: Hide sample sizes plot7 <- lmforest(     x = model1,     data = clintrial,     show_n = FALSE,     labels = clintrial_labels ) print(plot7)  # Example 8: Adjust table width plot8 <- lmforest(     x = model3,     data = clintrial,     table_width = 0.55,     labels = clintrial_labels ) print(plot8)  # Example 9: Get recommended dimensions plot9 <- lmforest(model1, data = clintrial) dims <- attr(plot9, "rec_dims") cat("Recommended:", dims$width, "x", dims$height, dims$units, "\n")  # Example 10: Specify exact dimensions plot10 <- lmforest(     x = model1,     data = clintrial,     plot_width = 12,     plot_height = 7,     labels = clintrial_labels )  # Example 11: Different units plot11 <- lmforest(     x = model1,     data = clintrial,     plot_width = 30,     plot_height = 20,     units = "cm",     labels = clintrial_labels )  # Example 12: No zebra stripes plot12 <- lmforest(     x = model1,     data = clintrial,     zebra_stripes = FALSE,     labels = clintrial_labels ) print(plot12)  # Example 13: Custom reference label plot13 <- lmforest(     x = model1,     data = clintrial,     ref_label = "0.00 (ref)",     labels = clintrial_labels ) print(plot13)  # Example 14: Larger fonts for presentation plot14 <- lmforest(     x = model1,     data = clintrial,     font_size = 1.3,     title_size = 26,     labels = clintrial_labels ) print(plot14)  # Example 15: More decimal places plot15 <- lmforest(     x = model1,     data = clintrial,     digits = 3,     labels = clintrial_labels ) print(plot15)  # Example 16: Hemoglobin as outcome model16 <- lm(     hemoglobin ~ age + sex + bmi + smoking + creatinine,     data = clintrial )  plot16 <- lmforest(     x = model16,     data = clintrial,     title = "Predictors of Baseline Hemoglobin",     effect_label = "Change in Hemoglobin (g/dL)",     labels = clintrial_labels ) print(plot16)  # Check assumptions par(mfrow = c(2, 2)) plot(model16) par(mfrow = c(1, 1))  # Example 17: Publication-ready final plot final_model <- lm(     bmi ~ age + sex + race + smoking + hypertension +          diabetes + creatinine + hemoglobin,     data = clintrial )  # Check model fit summary(final_model)  final_plot <- lmforest(     x = final_model,     data = clintrial,     title = "Multivariable Linear Regression: Predictors of Body Mass Index",     effect_label = "Change in BMI (kg/m²)",     labels = clintrial_labels,     indent_groups = TRUE,     zebra_stripes = TRUE,     show_n = TRUE,     color = "#5A8F5A",     digits = 2 )  # Save for publication dims <- attr(final_plot, "rec_dims") # ggsave("figure3_linear.pdf", final_plot, #        width = dims$width, height = dims$height) # ggsave("figure3_linear.png", final_plot, #        width = dims$width, height = dims$height, dpi = 300) }

what: Convert Model to Data Table — m2dt
title: Convert Model to Data Table — m2dt
text: Extracts coefficients, confidence intervals, comprehensive model statistics fitted regression models converts standardized data.table format suitable analysis publication. core utility function frequently used internally summata regression functions, although can used standalone function well.

title: Convert Model to Data Table — m2dt
code: m2dt(   data,   model,   conf_level = 0.95,   keep_qc_stats = TRUE,   include_intercept = TRUE,   terms_to_exclude = NULL,   reference_rows = TRUE,   reference_label = "reference",   skip_counts = FALSE )

title: Convert Model to Data Table — m2dt
text: data Data frame data.table containing dataset used fit model. Required computing group-level sample sizes event counts. model Fitted model object. Supported classes include: glm - Generalized linear models (logistic, Poisson, etc.) lm - Linear models coxph - Cox proportional hazards models clogit - Conditional logistic regression coxme - Mixed effects Cox models lmerMod - Linear mixed effects models glmerMod - Generalized linear mixed effects models conf_level Numeric confidence level confidence intervals. Must 0 1. Default 0.95 (95% CI). keep_qc_stats Logical. TRUE, includes model quality statistics AIC, BIC, R², concordance, model fit tests. appear additional columns output. Default TRUE. include_intercept Logical. TRUE, includes model intercept output. FALSE, removes intercept row results. Useful creating cleaner presentation tables. Default TRUE. terms_to_exclude Character vector term names exclude output. Useful removing specific unwanted parameters (e.g., nuisance variables, spline terms). Default NULL. Note: include_intercept = FALSE, "(Intercept)" automatically added list. reference_rows Logical. TRUE, adds rows reference categories factor variables appropriate labels baseline values (/HR = 1, Coefficient = 0). makes tables complete easier interpret. Default TRUE. reference_label Character string used label reference category rows output. Appears reference column. Default "reference". skip_counts Logical. TRUE, skips computation group-level sample sizes event counts (faster less informative). Default FALSE.

title: Convert Model to Data Table — m2dt
text: data.table containing extracted model information   following standard columns: model_scope Character. Either "Univariable" (unadjusted model       single predictor) "Multivariable" (adjusted model multiple predictors) model_type Character. Type regression (e.g., "Logistic", "Linear",       "Cox PH", "Poisson", etc.) variable Character. Variable name (factor variables, base       variable name without level) group Character. Group/level name factor variables; empty string       continuous variables n Integer. Total sample size used model n_group Integer. Sample size specific variable level       (factor variables ) events Integer. Total number events model (survival       logistic models) events_group Integer. Number events specific variable       level (survival logistic models factor variables) coefficient Numeric. Raw regression coefficient (log odds, log hazard,       etc.) se Numeric. Standard error coefficient /HR/RR/Coefficient Numeric. Effect estimate - column name depends       model type: logistic regression (odds ratio) HR Cox models (hazard ratio) RR Poisson regression (rate/risk ratio) Coefficient linear models GLMs ci_lower Numeric. Lower bound confidence interval effect estimate ci_upper Numeric. Upper bound confidence interval effect estimate statistic Numeric. Test statistic (z-value GLM/Cox, t-value LM) p_value Numeric. p-value coefficient test sig Character. Significance markers: *** (p < 0.001), **       (p < 0.01), * (p < 0.05), . (p < 0.10). sig_binary Logical. Binary indicator: TRUE p < 0.05,       FALSE otherwise reference Character. Contains reference_label reference       category rows reference_rows = TRUE, empty string otherwise

title: Convert Model to Data Table — m2dt
text: function core extraction utility used fit() regression functions. handles complexities different model classes provides consistent output format suitable tables forest plots. Model Type Detection: function automatically detects model type applies appropriate: Effect measure naming (, HR, RR, Coefficient) Confidence interval calculation method Event counting binary/survival outcomes Mixed Effects Models: lme4 models (glmer, lmer), function extracts fixed effects . Random effects variance components included output table, represent clustering structure rather predictor effects.

title: Convert Model to Data Table — m2dt
Multivariate Regression Analysis — multifit
text: Performs regression analyses single predictor (exposure) across multiple outcomes. function designed studies single exposure variable tested multiple endpoints, complication screening, biomarker associations, phenome-wide association studies. Returns publication-ready formatted results optional covariate adjustment. Supports interactions, mixed-effects models, stratification, clustered standard errors.

title: Multivariate Regression Analysis — multifit
code: multifit(   data,   outcomes,   predictor,   covariates = NULL,   interactions = NULL,   random = NULL,   strata = NULL,   cluster = NULL,   model_type = "glm",   family = "binomial",   columns = "adjusted",   p_threshold = 1,   conf_level = 0.95,   show_n = TRUE,   show_events = TRUE,   digits = 2,   p_digits = 3,   labels = NULL,   predictor_label = NULL,   include_predictor = TRUE,   keep_models = FALSE,   exponentiate = NULL,   parallel = TRUE,   n_cores = NULL,   number_format = NULL,   verbose = NULL,   ... )

title: Multivariate Regression Analysis — multifit
text: data Data frame data.table containing analysis dataset. function automatically converts data frames data.tables efficient processing. outcomes Character vector outcome variable names analyze. outcome tested model predictor. time--event analysis, use Surv() syntax outcome variable (e.g., c("Surv(time1, status1)", "Surv(time2, status2)")). predictor Character string specifying predictor (exposure) variable name. variable tested outcome. Can continuous categorical (factor). covariates Optional character vector covariate variable names include adjusted models. specified, models fit outcome ~ predictor + covariate1 + covariate2 + ..., predictor effect reported. Default NULL (unadjusted models). interactions Optional character vector interaction terms include adjusted models, using colon notation (e.g., c("predictor:sex")). Interactions involving predictor effects extracted reported. Default NULL. random Optional character string specifying random effects formula mixed effects models (e.g., "(1|hospital)" "(1|site/patient)"). Required model_type "glmer", "lmer", "coxme" unless random effects included covariates vector. Alternatively, random effects can included directly covariates vector using syntax (e.g., covariates = c("age", "sex", "(1|site)")). Default NULL. strata Optional character string naming stratification variable Cox conditional logistic models. Creates separate baseline hazards stratum. Default NULL. cluster Optional character string naming clustering variable Cox models. Computes robust clustered standard errors. Default NULL. model_type Character string specifying type regression model fit. Options include: "glm" - Generalized linear model (default). Supports multiple     distributions via family parameter including logistic, Poisson,     Gamma, Gaussian, quasi-likelihood models. "lm" - Linear regression continuous outcomes normally     distributed errors. "coxph" - Cox proportional hazards model time--event     survival analysis. Requires Surv() outcome syntax. "clogit" - Conditional logistic regression matched     case-control studies. "negbin" - Negative binomial regression overdispersed count     data (requires MASS package). Estimates additional dispersion parameter     compared Poisson regression. "glmer" - Generalized linear mixed-effects model hierarchical     clustered data non-normal outcomes (requires lme4 package     random parameter). "lmer" - Linear mixed-effects model hierarchical clustered     data continuous outcomes (requires lme4 package random     parameter). "coxme" - Cox mixed-effects model clustered survival data     (requires coxme package random parameter). family GLM GLMER models, specifies error distribution link function. Can character string, family function, family object. Ignored non-GLM/GLMER models. Binary/Binomial outcomes: "binomial" binomial() - Logistic regression binary     outcomes (0/1, TRUE/FALSE). Returns odds ratios (). Default. "quasibinomial" quasibinomial() - Logistic regression     overdispersion. Use residual deviance >> residual df. binomial(link = "probit") - Probit regression (normal CDF link). binomial(link = "cloglog") - Complementary log-log link     asymmetric binary outcomes. Count outcomes: "poisson" poisson() - Poisson regression count     data. Returns rate ratios (RR). Assumes mean = variance. "quasipoisson" quasipoisson() - Poisson regression     overdispersion. Use variance > mean. Continuous outcomes: "gaussian" gaussian() - Normal/Gaussian distribution     continuous outcomes. Equivalent linear regression. gaussian(link = "log") - Log-linear model positive continuous     outcomes. Returns multiplicative effects. Positive continuous outcomes: "Gamma" Gamma() - Gamma distribution positive,     right-skewed continuous data (e.g., costs, lengths stay). Default log link. Gamma(link = "inverse") - Gamma inverse (canonical) link. Gamma(link = "identity") - Gamma identity link additive     effects positive outcomes. "inverse.gaussian" inverse.gaussian() - Inverse Gaussian     positive, highly right-skewed data. negative binomial regression (overdispersed counts), use model_type = "negbin" instead family parameter. See family additional details options. columns Character string specifying result columns display unadjusted adjusted models fit (.e., covariates specified): "adjusted" - Show adjusted (covariate-controlled) results [default] "unadjusted" - Show unadjusted (crude) results "" - Show unadjusted adjusted results side--side Ignored covariates = NULL. p_threshold Numeric value 0 1 specifying p-value threshold filtering results. outcomes p-value less equal threshold included output. Default 1 (filtering; outcomes returned). conf_level Numeric confidence level confidence intervals. Must 0 1. Default 0.95 (95% confidence intervals). show_n Logical. TRUE, includes sample size column output table. Default TRUE. show_events Logical. TRUE, includes events column output table (relevant survival logistic regression). Default TRUE. digits Integer specifying number decimal places effect estimates (, HR, RR, coefficients). Default 2. p_digits Integer specifying number decimal places p-values. Values smaller 10^(-p_digits) displayed "< 0.001" (p_digits = 3), "< 0.0001" (p_digits = 4), etc. Default 3. labels Named character vector list providing custom display labels variables. Can include labels outcomes, predictors, covariates. Names match variable names, values display labels. Labels applied : (1) outcome names Outcome column, (2) predictor variable name displayed, (3) variable names formatted interaction terms. Variables labels use original names. Default NULL. predictor_label Optional character string providing custom display label predictor variable. Takes precedence labels predictor. Default NULL (uses label labels original name). include_predictor Logical. TRUE (default), includes predictor column output table showing level factor predictor compared. FALSE, omits predictor column, may useful predictor information explained table caption figure legend. keep_models Logical. TRUE, stores fitted model objects output attribute. Models accessible via attr(result, "models"). Default FALSE. exponentiate Logical. Whether exponentiate coefficients (display /HR/RR instead log odds/log hazards). Default NULL, automatically displays raw coefficients linear models exponentiates logistic, Poisson, Cox models. parallel Logical. TRUE (default), fits models parallel improved performance many outcomes. n_cores Integer specifying number CPU cores use parallel processing. Default NULL (auto-detect: uses detectCores() - 1). Ignored parallel = FALSE. number_format Character string two-element character vector   controlling thousand decimal separators formatted output. Named   presets: "us" - Comma thousands, period decimal: 1,234.56 [default] "eu" - Period thousands, comma decimal: 1.234,56 "space" - Thin-space thousands, period decimal: 1 234.56       (SI/ISO 31-0) "none" - thousands separator: 1234.56 provide custom two-element vector c(big.mark, decimal.mark),   e.g., c("'", ".") Swiss-style: 1'234.56. NULL (default), uses   getOption("summata.number_format", "us"). Set global option   per session avoid passing argument repeatedly: verbose Logical. TRUE, displays model fitting warnings (e.g., singular fit, convergence issues). FALSE (default), routine fitting messages suppressed unexpected warnings preserved. NULL, uses getOption("summata.verbose", FALSE). ... Additional arguments passed underlying model fitting functions.
code: options(summata.number_format = "eu")

title: Multivariate Regression Analysis — multifit
text: data.table S3 class "multifit_result" containing formatted   multivariate regression results. table structure includes: Outcome Character. Outcome variable name custom label Predictor Character. factor predictors: formatted       "Variable (Level)" showing level compared reference.       binary variables non-reference level affirmative       value (Yes, 1, True, Present, Positive, +), shows just "Variable".       continuous predictors: variable name. interactions:       formatted interaction term (e.g., "Treatment (Drug ) × Sex (Male)") n Integer. Sample size used model (show_n = TRUE) Events Integer. Number events (show_events = TRUE) /HR/RR/Coefficient (95% CI) Character. Unadjusted effect       estimate CI (columns = "unadjusted" "") aOR/aHR/aRR/Adj. Coefficient (95% CI) Character. Adjusted       effect estimate CI (columns = "adjusted" "") Uni p / Multi p / p-value Character. Formatted p-value(s). Column       names depend columns setting returned object includes following attributes accessible via attr(): raw_data data.table. Unformatted numeric results separate       columns effect estimates, standard errors, confidence intervals,       p-values. Suitable custom analysis visualization models list (keep_models = TRUE). Named list fitted       model objects, outcome names list names. element contains       $unadjusted /$adjusted models depending settings predictor Character. predictor variable name outcomes Character vector. outcome variable names covariates Character vector NULL. covariate variable names interactions Character vector NULL. interaction terms random Character NULL. random effects formula strata Character NULL. stratification variable cluster Character NULL. clustering variable model_type Character. regression model type used columns Character. columns displayed analysis_type Character. "multi_outcome" identify analysis type significant Character vector. Names outcomes p < 0.05       predictor (uses adjusted p-values available)

title: Multivariate Regression Analysis — multifit
text: Analysis Approach: function implements multivariate (multi-outcome) screening workflow inverts typical regression paradigm: outcome outcomes, fits separate model     predictor main exposure covariates specified, fits adjusted model:     outcome ~ predictor + covariates + interactions Extracts predictor effect(s) model, ignoring     covariate coefficients Combines results single table comparison across outcomes Optionally filters p-value threshold conceptually opposite uniscreen(), tests multiple predictors single outcome. Use multifit() one exposure interest want screen across multiple endpoints. Use Multivariate Regression Analysis: Complication screening: Test one exposure (e.g., operative time,     BMI, biomarker level) multiple postoperative complications Treatment effects: Test one treatment multiple efficacy     safety endpoints simultaneously Biomarker studies: Test one biomarker multiple clinical     outcomes understand prognostic value Phenome-wide association studies (PheWAS): Test genetic variants     exposures many phenotypes Risk factor profiling: Understand one risk factor relates     spectrum outcomes Handling Categorical Predictors: predictor factor variable multiple levels: non-reference level gets row outcome Reference category determined factor level ordering Predictor column shows "Variable (Level)" format     (e.g., "Treatment (Drug )", "Treatment (Drug B)") binary variables affirmative non-reference levels     (Yes, 1, True, Present, Positive, +), shows just "Variable"     (e.g., "Diabetes" instead "Diabetes (Yes)") Effect estimates compare level reference Adjusted vs. Unadjusted Results: covariates specified, function fits models extracts predictor effects: columns = "adjusted": Reports covariate-adjusted effects.     Column labeled "aOR/aHR," etc. columns = "unadjusted": Reports crude effects. Column     labeled "/HR," etc. columns = "": Reports side--side. Useful     identifying confounding (large change effect) independent effects     (similar estimates) Interaction Terms: interactions includes terms involving predictor: Main effect predictor always reported Interaction effects extracted displayed formatted names Format: Variable (Level) × Variable (Level) using multiplication sign notation Useful testing effect modification (e.g., treatment effect     differ sex?) Mixed-Effects Models: clustered hierarchical data (e.g., patients within hospitals): Use model_type = "glmer" random = "(1|cluster)"     random intercept models Nested random effects: random = "(1|site/patient)" Crossed random effects: random = "(1|site) + (1|doctor)" survival outcomes, use model_type = "coxme" Stratification Clustering (Cox models): Cox proportional hazards models: strata: Creates separate baseline hazards stratum level.     Use hazards non-proportional across strata stratum effects     need estimated cluster: Computes robust (sandwich) standard errors accounting     within-cluster correlation. Alternative mixed effects     robust SEs needed Filtering based p-value: p_threshold parameter filters results fitting models: outcomes p less equal threshold retained output factor predictors, outcome kept level significant Useful focusing significant associations exploratory analyses Default 1 (filtering) - recommended confirmatory analyses Outcome Homogeneity: outcomes single multifit() call type (binary, continuous, survival). Mixing outcome types produces tables incompatible effect measures (e.g., odds ratios alongside regression coefficients), can mislead readers. function validates outcome compatibility issues warning mixed types detected. analyses involving multiple outcome types, run separate multifit() calls type: Effect Measures Model Type: Logistic (model_type = "glm", family = "binomial"):     Odds ratios (/aOR) Cox (model_type = "coxph"): Hazard ratios (HR/aHR) Poisson (model_type = "glm", family = "poisson"):     Rate ratios (RR/aRR) Linear (model_type = "lm"): Coefficient estimates Mixed effects: fixed-effects counterparts Memory Performance: parallel = TRUE (default) uses multiple cores faster fitting keep_models = FALSE (default) discards model objects save memory many outcomes, parallel processing provides substantial speedup Set keep_models = TRUE need model diagnostics
code: # Binary outcomes binary_results <- multifit(data, outcomes = c("death", "readmission"),                            predictor = "treatment", model_type = "glm")  # Continuous outcomes continuous_results <- multifit(data, outcomes = c("los_days", "cost"),                                predictor = "treatment", model_type = "lm")

title: Multivariate Regression Analysis — multifit
code: # Load example data data(clintrial) data(clintrial_labels)  # Example 1: Basic multivariate analysis (unadjusted) # Test treatment effect on multiple binary outcomes result1 <- multifit(     data = clintrial,     outcomes = c("surgery", "pfs_status", "os_status"),     predictor = "treatment",     labels = clintrial_labels,     parallel = FALSE ) print(result1) #>  #> Multivariate Analysis Results #> Predictor: treatment #> Outcomes: 3 #> Model Type: glm #> Display: unadjusted #>  #>                       Outcome                Predictor      n Events      OR (95% CI) p-value #>                        <char>                   <char> <char> <char>           <char>  <char> #> 1:         Surgical Resection Treatment Group (Drug A)    292    173 1.58 (1.10-2.27)   0.014 #> 2:         Surgical Resection Treatment Group (Drug B)    362    103 0.43 (0.30-0.62) < 0.001 #> 3: Progression or Death Event Treatment Group (Drug A)    292    227 0.44 (0.26-0.74)   0.002 #> 4: Progression or Death Event Treatment Group (Drug B)    362    322 1.02 (0.59-1.77)   0.950 #> 5:                Death Event Treatment Group (Drug A)    292    184 0.51 (0.34-0.76)   0.001 #> 6:                Death Event Treatment Group (Drug B)    362    274 0.93 (0.62-1.40)   0.721 # Shows odds ratios comparing Drug A and Drug B to Control  # \donttest{ # Example 2: Adjusted analysis with covariates # Adjust for age, sex, and disease stage result2 <- multifit(     data = clintrial,     outcomes = c("surgery", "pfs_status", "os_status"),     predictor = "treatment",     covariates = c("age", "sex", "stage"),     labels = clintrial_labels,     parallel = FALSE ) print(result2) #>  #> Multivariate Analysis Results #> Predictor: treatment #> Outcomes: 3 #> Model Type: glm #> Covariates: age, sex, stage #> Display: adjusted #>  #>                       Outcome                Predictor      n Events     aOR (95% CI) p-value #>                        <char>                   <char> <char> <char>           <char>  <char> #> 1:         Surgical Resection Treatment Group (Drug A)    292    173 1.84 (1.23-2.75)   0.003 #> 2:         Surgical Resection Treatment Group (Drug B)    361    102 0.45 (0.30-0.66) < 0.001 #> 3: Progression or Death Event Treatment Group (Drug A)    292    227 0.36 (0.21-0.63) < 0.001 #> 4: Progression or Death Event Treatment Group (Drug B)    361    321 0.77 (0.43-1.38)   0.374 #> 5:                Death Event Treatment Group (Drug A)    292    184 0.44 (0.28-0.68) < 0.001 #> 6:                Death Event Treatment Group (Drug B)    361    273 0.74 (0.48-1.16)   0.192 # Shows adjusted odds ratios (aOR)  # Example 3: Compare unadjusted and adjusted results result3 <- multifit(     data = clintrial,     outcomes = c("surgery", "pfs_status", "os_status"),     predictor = "treatment",     covariates = c("age", "sex", "stage"),     columns = "both",     labels = clintrial_labels,     parallel = FALSE ) print(result3) #>  #> Multivariate Analysis Results #> Predictor: treatment #> Outcomes: 3 #> Model Type: glm #> Covariates: age, sex, stage #> Display: both #>  #>                       Outcome                Predictor      n Events      OR (95% CI)   Uni p     aOR (95% CI) Multi p #>                        <char>                   <char> <char> <char>           <char>  <char>           <char>  <char> #> 1:                Death Event Treatment Group (Drug A)    292    184 0.51 (0.34-0.76)   0.001 0.44 (0.28-0.68) < 0.001 #> 2:                Death Event Treatment Group (Drug B)    362    274 0.93 (0.62-1.40)   0.721 0.74 (0.48-1.16)   0.192 #> 3: Progression or Death Event Treatment Group (Drug A)    292    227 0.44 (0.26-0.74)   0.002 0.36 (0.21-0.63) < 0.001 #> 4: Progression or Death Event Treatment Group (Drug B)    362    322 1.02 (0.59-1.77)   0.950 0.77 (0.43-1.38)   0.374 #> 5:         Surgical Resection Treatment Group (Drug A)    292    173 1.58 (1.10-2.27)   0.014 1.84 (1.23-2.75)   0.003 #> 6:         Surgical Resection Treatment Group (Drug B)    362    103 0.43 (0.30-0.62) < 0.001 0.45 (0.30-0.66) < 0.001 # Useful for identifying confounding effects  # Example 4: Continuous predictor across outcomes # Test age effect on multiple outcomes result4 <- multifit(     data = clintrial,     outcomes = c("surgery", "pfs_status", "os_status"),     predictor = "age",     covariates = c("sex", "treatment", "stage"),     labels = clintrial_labels,     parallel = FALSE ) print(result4) #>  #> Multivariate Analysis Results #> Predictor: age #> Outcomes: 3 #> Model Type: glm #> Covariates: sex, treatment, stage #> Display: adjusted #>  #>                       Outcome      n Events     aOR (95% CI) p-value #>                        <char> <char> <char>           <char>  <char> #> 1:         Surgical Resection    847    368 0.96 (0.94-0.97) < 0.001 #> 2: Progression or Death Event    847    721 1.02 (1.01-1.04)   0.006 #> 3:                Death Event    847    606 1.05 (1.04-1.07) < 0.001 # One row per outcome for continuous predictor  # Example 5: Cox regression for survival outcomes library(survival) cox_result <- multifit(     data = clintrial,     outcomes = c("Surv(pfs_months, pfs_status)",                   "Surv(os_months, os_status)"),     predictor = "treatment",     covariates = c("age", "sex", "stage"),     model_type = "coxph",     labels = clintrial_labels,     parallel = FALSE ) print(cox_result) #>  #> Multivariate Analysis Results #> Predictor: treatment #> Outcomes: 2 #> Model Type: coxph #> Covariates: age, sex, stage #> Display: adjusted #>  #>                               Outcome                Predictor      n Events     aHR (95% CI) p-value #>                                <char>                   <char> <char> <char>           <char>  <char> #> 1: Progression-Free Survival (months) Treatment Group (Drug A)    721    721 0.54 (0.44-0.66) < 0.001 #> 2: Progression-Free Survival (months) Treatment Group (Drug B)    721    721 0.82 (0.68-0.98)   0.033 #> 3:          Overall Survival (months) Treatment Group (Drug A)    606    606 0.56 (0.45-0.70) < 0.001 #> 4:          Overall Survival (months) Treatment Group (Drug B)    606    606 0.83 (0.67-1.01)   0.062 # Returns hazard ratios (HR/aHR)  # Example 6: Cox with stratification by site cox_strat <- multifit(     data = clintrial,     outcomes = c("Surv(os_months, os_status)"),     predictor = "treatment",     covariates = c("age", "sex"),     strata = "site",     model_type = "coxph",     labels = clintrial_labels,     parallel = FALSE ) print(cox_strat) #>  #> Multivariate Analysis Results #> Predictor: treatment #> Outcomes: 1 #> Model Type: coxph #> Covariates: age, sex #> Strata: site #> Display: adjusted #>  #>                      Outcome                Predictor      n Events     aHR (95% CI) p-value #>                       <char>                   <char> <char> <char>           <char>  <char> #> 1: Overall Survival (months) Treatment Group (Drug A)    609    609 0.62 (0.50-0.77) < 0.001 #> 2: Overall Survival (months) Treatment Group (Drug B)    609    609 1.00 (0.81-1.22)   0.976  # Example 7: Cox with clustered standard errors cox_cluster <- multifit(     data = clintrial,     outcomes = c("Surv(os_months, os_status)"),     predictor = "treatment",     covariates = c("age", "sex", "stage"),     cluster = "site",     model_type = "coxph",     labels = clintrial_labels,     parallel = FALSE ) print(cox_cluster) #>  #> Multivariate Analysis Results #> Predictor: treatment #> Outcomes: 1 #> Model Type: coxph #> Covariates: age, sex, stage #> Cluster: site #> Display: adjusted #>  #>                      Outcome                Predictor      n Events     aHR (95% CI) p-value #>                       <char>                   <char> <char> <char>           <char>  <char> #> 1: Overall Survival (months) Treatment Group (Drug A)    606    606 0.56 (0.45-0.70) < 0.001 #> 2: Overall Survival (months) Treatment Group (Drug B)    606    606 0.83 (0.67-1.01)   0.167  # Example 8: Interaction between predictor and covariate # Test if treatment effect differs by sex result_int <- multifit(     data = clintrial,     outcomes = c("surgery", "os_status"),     predictor = "treatment",     covariates = c("age", "sex", "stage"),     interactions = c("treatment:sex"),     labels = clintrial_labels,     parallel = FALSE ) print(result_int) #>  #> Multivariate Analysis Results #> Predictor: treatment #> Outcomes: 2 #> Model Type: glm #> Covariates: age, sex, stage #> Interactions: treatment:sex #> Display: adjusted #>  #>               Outcome                             Predictor      n Events     aOR (95% CI) p-value #>                <char>                                <char> <char> <char>           <char>  <char> #> 1: Surgical Resection              Treatment Group (Drug A)    292    173 2.09 (1.20-3.62)   0.009 #> 2: Surgical Resection              Treatment Group (Drug B)    361    102 0.50 (0.29-0.87)   0.013 #> 3: Surgical Resection Treatment Group (Drug A) × Sex (Male)      -      - 0.77 (0.34-1.71)   0.515 #> 4: Surgical Resection Treatment Group (Drug B) × Sex (Male)      -      - 0.79 (0.36-1.73)   0.554 #> 5:        Death Event              Treatment Group (Drug A)    292    184 0.39 (0.22-0.70)   0.002 #> 6:        Death Event              Treatment Group (Drug B)    361    273 0.61 (0.34-1.10)   0.099 #> 7:        Death Event Treatment Group (Drug A) × Sex (Male)      -      - 1.29 (0.53-3.18)   0.575 #> 8:        Death Event Treatment Group (Drug B) × Sex (Male)      -      - 1.59 (0.65-3.91)   0.308 # Shows main effects and interaction terms with × notation  # Example 9: Linear model for continuous outcomes linear_result <- multifit(     data = clintrial,     outcomes = c("los_days", "biomarker_x"),     predictor = "treatment",     covariates = c("age", "sex"),     model_type = "lm",     labels = clintrial_labels,     parallel = FALSE ) print(linear_result) #>  #> Multivariate Analysis Results #> Predictor: treatment #> Outcomes: 2 #> Model Type: lm #> Covariates: age, sex #> Display: adjusted #>  #>                           Outcome                Predictor      n Adj. Coefficient (95% CI) p-value #>                            <char>                   <char> <char>                    <char>  <char> #> 1: Length of Hospital Stay (days) Treatment Group (Drug A)    288    -0.80 (-1.58 to -0.02)   0.045 #> 2: Length of Hospital Stay (days) Treatment Group (Drug B)    350       2.66 (1.91 to 3.41) < 0.001 #> 3:            Biomarker X (ng/mL) Treatment Group (Drug A)    290     -0.39 (-0.94 to 0.16)   0.164 #> 4:            Biomarker X (ng/mL) Treatment Group (Drug B)    358     -0.01 (-0.53 to 0.52)   0.983 # Returns coefficient estimates, not ratios  # Example 10: Poisson regression for equidispersed count outcomes # fu_count has variance ≈ mean, appropriate for standard Poisson poisson_result <- multifit(     data = clintrial,     outcomes = c("fu_count"),     predictor = "treatment",     covariates = c("age", "stage"),     model_type = "glm",     family = "poisson",     labels = clintrial_labels,     parallel = FALSE ) print(poisson_result) #>  #> Multivariate Analysis Results #> Predictor: treatment #> Outcomes: 1 #> Model Type: glm #> Covariates: age, stage #> Display: adjusted #>  #>                  Outcome                Predictor      n Events     aRR (95% CI) p-value #>                   <char>                   <char> <char> <char>           <char>  <char> #> 1: Follow-Up Visit Count Treatment Group (Drug A)    290  1,910 1.08 (1.00-1.16)   0.052 #> 2: Follow-Up Visit Count Treatment Group (Drug B)    358  2,438 1.11 (1.03-1.19)   0.005 # Returns rate ratios (RR) # For overdispersed counts (ae_count), use model_type = "negbin" instead  # Example 11: Filter to significant results only sig_results <- multifit(     data = clintrial,     outcomes = c("surgery", "pfs_status", "os_status"),     predictor = "stage",     p_threshold = 0.05,     labels = clintrial_labels,     parallel = FALSE ) print(sig_results) #>  #> Multivariate Analysis Results #> Predictor: stage #> Outcomes: 3 #> Model Type: glm #> Display: unadjusted #>  #>                       Outcome           Predictor      n Events        OR (95% CI) p-value #>                        <char>              <char> <char> <char>             <char>  <char> #> 1:         Surgical Resection Disease Stage (III)    241     91   0.42 (0.29-0.61) < 0.001 #> 2:         Surgical Resection  Disease Stage (IV)    132     19   0.12 (0.07-0.20) < 0.001 #> 3: Progression or Death Event  Disease Stage (II)    263    223   2.37 (1.52-3.71) < 0.001 #> 4: Progression or Death Event Disease Stage (III)    241    222   4.97 (2.86-8.65) < 0.001 #> 5: Progression or Death Event  Disease Stage (IV)    132    128 13.62 (4.82-38.46) < 0.001 #> 6:                Death Event Disease Stage (III)    241    186   2.24 (1.49-3.36) < 0.001 #> 7:                Death Event  Disease Stage (IV)    132    121  7.28 (3.70-14.30) < 0.001 # Only outcomes with significant associations shown  # Example 12: Custom outcome labels result_labeled <- multifit(     data = clintrial,     outcomes = c("surgery", "pfs_status", "os_status"),     predictor = "treatment",     labels = c(         surgery = "Surgical Resection",         pfs_status = "Disease Progression",         os_status = "Death",         treatment = "Treatment Group"     ),     parallel = FALSE ) print(result_labeled) #>  #> Multivariate Analysis Results #> Predictor: treatment #> Outcomes: 3 #> Model Type: glm #> Display: unadjusted #>  #>                Outcome                Predictor      n Events      OR (95% CI) p-value #>                 <char>                   <char> <char> <char>           <char>  <char> #> 1:  Surgical Resection Treatment Group (Drug A)    292    173 1.58 (1.10-2.27)   0.014 #> 2:  Surgical Resection Treatment Group (Drug B)    362    103 0.43 (0.30-0.62) < 0.001 #> 3: Disease Progression Treatment Group (Drug A)    292    227 0.44 (0.26-0.74)   0.002 #> 4: Disease Progression Treatment Group (Drug B)    362    322 1.02 (0.59-1.77)   0.950 #> 5:               Death Treatment Group (Drug A)    292    184 0.51 (0.34-0.76)   0.001 #> 6:               Death Treatment Group (Drug B)    362    274 0.93 (0.62-1.40)   0.721  # Example 13: Keep models for diagnostics result_models <- multifit(     data = clintrial,     outcomes = c("surgery", "os_status"),     predictor = "treatment",     covariates = c("age", "sex"),     keep_models = TRUE,     parallel = FALSE )  # Access stored models models <- attr(result_models, "models") names(models) #> [1] "surgery"   "os_status"  # Get adjusted model for surgery outcome surgery_model <- models$surgery$adjusted summary(surgery_model) #>  #> Call: #> stats::glm(formula = adj_formula, family = family, data = .data,  #>     model = keep_models, x = FALSE, y = TRUE) #>  #> Coefficients: #>                 Estimate Std. Error z value Pr(>|z|)     #> (Intercept)      2.20552    0.41603   5.301 1.15e-07 *** #> treatmentDrug A  0.49773    0.19099   2.606  0.00916 **  #> treatmentDrug B -0.83000    0.18854  -4.402 1.07e-05 *** #> age             -0.03765    0.00647  -5.820 5.89e-09 *** #> sexMale         -0.12698    0.14757  -0.861  0.38950     #> --- #> Signif. codes:  0 ‘***’ 0.001 ‘**’ 0.01 ‘*’ 0.05 ‘.’ 0.1 ‘ ’ 1 #>  #> (Dispersion parameter for binomial family taken to be 1) #>  #>     Null deviance: 1164.1  on 849  degrees of freedom #> Residual deviance: 1061.7  on 845  degrees of freedom #> AIC: 1071.7 #>  #> Number of Fisher Scoring iterations: 4 #>   # Example 14: Access raw numeric data result <- multifit(     data = clintrial,     outcomes = c("surgery", "os_status"),     predictor = "age",     parallel = FALSE )  # Get unformatted results for custom analysis raw_data <- attr(result, "raw_data") print(raw_data) #>      outcome predictor  group     n events coefficient          se exp_coef  ci_lower  ci_upper statistic      p_value effect_type adjusted #>       <char>    <char> <char> <int>  <num>       <num>       <num>    <num>     <num>     <num>     <num>        <num>      <char>   <lgcl> #> 1:   surgery       age      -   850    370 -0.03608738 0.006211760 0.964556 0.9528839 0.9763711 -5.809525 6.265023e-09          OR    FALSE #> 2: os_status       age      -   850    609  0.04695122 0.006987363 1.048071 1.0338154 1.0625229  6.719447 1.824154e-11          OR    FALSE # Contains exp_coef, ci_lower, ci_upper, p_value, \emph{etc.}  # Example 15: Hide sample size and event columns result_minimal <- multifit(     data = clintrial,     outcomes = c("surgery", "os_status"),     predictor = "treatment",     show_n = FALSE,     show_events = FALSE,     parallel = FALSE ) print(result_minimal) #>  #> Multivariate Analysis Results #> Predictor: treatment #> Outcomes: 2 #> Model Type: glm #> Display: unadjusted #>  #>      Outcome          Predictor      OR (95% CI) p-value #>       <char>             <char>           <char>  <char> #> 1:   surgery treatment (Drug A) 1.58 (1.10-2.27)   0.014 #> 2:   surgery treatment (Drug B) 0.43 (0.30-0.62) < 0.001 #> 3: os_status treatment (Drug A) 0.51 (0.34-0.76)   0.001 #> 4: os_status treatment (Drug B) 0.93 (0.62-1.40)   0.721  # Example 16: Customize decimal places result_digits <- multifit(     data = clintrial,     outcomes = c("surgery", "os_status"),     predictor = "age",     digits = 3,     p_digits = 4,     parallel = FALSE ) print(result_digits) #>  #> Multivariate Analysis Results #> Predictor: age #> Outcomes: 2 #> Model Type: glm #> Display: unadjusted #>  #>      Outcome      n Events         OR (95% CI)  p-value #>       <char> <char> <char>              <char>   <char> #> 1:   surgery    850    370 0.965 (0.953-0.976) < 0.0001 #> 2: os_status    850    609 1.048 (1.034-1.063) < 0.0001  # Example 17: Force coefficient display (no exponentiation) result_coef <- multifit(     data = clintrial,     outcomes = c("surgery"),     predictor = "age",     exponentiate = FALSE,     parallel = FALSE ) print(result_coef) #>  #> Multivariate Analysis Results #> Predictor: age #> Outcomes: 1 #> Model Type: glm #> Display: unadjusted #>  #>    Outcome      n Events      OR (95% CI) p-value #>     <char> <char> <char>           <char>  <char> #> 1: surgery    850    370 0.96 (0.95-0.98) < 0.001  # Example 18: Complete publication workflow final_table <- multifit(     data = clintrial,     outcomes = c("surgery", "pfs_status", "os_status"),     predictor = "treatment",     covariates = c("age", "sex", "stage", "grade"),     columns = "both",     labels = clintrial_labels,     digits = 2,     p_digits = 3,     parallel = FALSE ) print(final_table) #>  #> Multivariate Analysis Results #> Predictor: treatment #> Outcomes: 3 #> Model Type: glm #> Covariates: age, sex, stage, grade #> Display: both #>  #>                       Outcome                Predictor      n Events      OR (95% CI)   Uni p     aOR (95% CI) Multi p #>                        <char>                   <char> <char> <char>           <char>  <char>           <char>  <char> #> 1:                Death Event Treatment Group (Drug A)    292    184 0.51 (0.34-0.76)   0.001 0.41 (0.26-0.65) < 0.001 #> 2:                Death Event Treatment Group (Drug B)    362    274 0.93 (0.62-1.40)   0.721 0.69 (0.44-1.08)   0.105 #> 3: Progression or Death Event Treatment Group (Drug A)    292    227 0.44 (0.26-0.74)   0.002 0.34 (0.20-0.60) < 0.001 #> 4: Progression or Death Event Treatment Group (Drug B)    362    322 1.02 (0.59-1.77)   0.950 0.73 (0.40-1.31)   0.291 #> 5:         Surgical Resection Treatment Group (Drug A)    292    173 1.58 (1.10-2.27)   0.014 1.78 (1.19-2.67)   0.005 #> 6:         Surgical Resection Treatment Group (Drug B)    362    103 0.43 (0.30-0.62) < 0.001 0.43 (0.29-0.64) < 0.001  # Export to various formats for publication # table2pdf(final_table, "treatment_effects.pdf",  #         caption = "Treatment effects across outcomes") # table2docx(final_table, "treatment_effects.docx")  # Example 19: Create forest plot from results result_forest <- multifit(     data = clintrial,     outcomes = c("surgery", "pfs_status", "os_status"),     predictor = "treatment",     covariates = c("age", "sex", "stage"),     labels = clintrial_labels,     parallel = FALSE )  # Create forest plot (requires \pkg{ggplot2}) # p <- multiforest(result_forest,  #                  title = "Treatment Effects Across Outcomes") # print(p)  # Example 20: Gamma regression for positive continuous outcomes gamma_result <- multifit(     data = clintrial,     outcomes = c("los_days", "recovery_days"),     predictor = "treatment",     covariates = c("age", "surgery"),     model_type = "glm",     family = Gamma(link = "log"),     labels = clintrial_labels,     parallel = FALSE ) print(gamma_result) #>  #> Multivariate Analysis Results #> Predictor: treatment #> Outcomes: 2 #> Model Type: glm #> Covariates: age, surgery #> Display: adjusted #>  #>                           Outcome                Predictor      n Adj. Coefficient (95% CI) p-value #>                            <char>                   <char> <char>                    <char>  <char> #> 1: Length of Hospital Stay (days) Treatment Group (Drug A)    288    -0.06 (-0.10 to -0.02)       - #> 2: Length of Hospital Stay (days) Treatment Group (Drug B)    350       0.15 (0.11 to 0.19)       - #> 3:    Days to Functional Recovery Treatment Group (Drug A)    288    -0.10 (-0.17 to -0.03)       - #> 4:    Days to Functional Recovery Treatment Group (Drug B)    352       0.17 (0.10 to 0.23)       - # Returns multiplicative effects on positive continuous data  # Example 21: Quasipoisson for overdispersed counts quasi_result <- multifit(     data = clintrial,     outcomes = c("ae_count"),     predictor = "treatment",     covariates = c("age", "diabetes"),     model_type = "glm",     family = "quasipoisson",     labels = clintrial_labels,     parallel = FALSE ) print(quasi_result) #>  #> Multivariate Analysis Results #> Predictor: treatment #> Outcomes: 1 #> Model Type: glm #> Covariates: age, diabetes #> Display: adjusted #>  #>                Outcome                Predictor      n Events     aRR (95% CI) p-value #> what: Create Forest Plot for Multivariate Regression — multiforest
title: Create Forest Plot for Multivariate Regression — multiforest
text: Generates publication-ready forest plot multifit() output object. plot displays effect estimates (, HR, RR, coefficients) confidence intervals across multiple outcomes, organized outcome predictor levels shown .

title: Create Forest Plot for Multivariate Regression — multiforest
code: multiforest(   x,   title = "Multivariate Analysis",   effect_label = NULL,   column = "adjusted",   digits = 2,   p_digits = 3,   conf_level = 0.95,   font_size = 1,   annot_size = 3.88,   header_size = 5.82,   title_size = 23.28,   plot_width = NULL,   plot_height = NULL,   table_width = 0.6,   show_n = TRUE,   show_events = NULL,   show_predictor = NULL,   covariates_footer = TRUE,   indent_predictor = FALSE,   bold_variables = TRUE,   center_padding = 4,   zebra_stripes = TRUE,   color = NULL,   null_line = NULL,   log_scale = NULL,   labels = NULL,   units = "in",   number_format = NULL )

title: Create Forest Plot for Multivariate Regression — multiforest
text: x Multifit result object (data.table class attributes multifit()). title Character string specifying plot title. Default "Multivariate Analysis". Use descriptive titles publication. effect_label Character string effect measure label forest plot axis. Default NULL, auto-detects based model type (e.g., "Odds Ratio", "Hazard Ratio", "Rate Ratio", "Estimate"). column Character string specifying results plot multifit() called columns = "". Options "adjusted" (default) "unadjusted". Ignored multifit result contains one column type. digits Integer specifying number decimal places effect estimates confidence intervals. Default 2. p_digits Integer specifying number decimal places p-values. Values smaller 10^(-p_digits) displayed "< 0.001" (p_digits = 3), "< 0.0001" (p_digits = 4), etc. Default 3. conf_level Numeric confidence level confidence intervals. Must 0 1. Default 0.95 (95% confidence intervals). CI percentage automatically displayed column headers (e.g., "90% CI" conf_level = 0.90). font_size Numeric multiplier controlling base font size text elements. Default 1.0. annot_size Numeric value controlling relative font size data annotations. Default 3.88. header_size Numeric value controlling relative font size column headers. Default 5.82. title_size Numeric value controlling relative font size main plot title. Default 23.28. plot_width Numeric value specifying intended output width specified units. Used optimizing layout. Default NULL (automatic). Recommended: 10-16 inches. plot_height Numeric value specifying intended output height specified units. Default NULL (automatic based number rows). table_width Numeric value 0 1 specifying proportion total plot width allocated data table. Default 0.6 (60% table, 40% forest plot). show_n Logical. TRUE, includes column showing sample sizes. Default TRUE. show_events Logical. TRUE, includes column showing number events row. Default TRUE binomial survival models, FALSE linear models. show_predictor Logical. TRUE, includes "Predictor" column showing level factor predictor compared. FALSE, omits column (useful predictor info caption). Default NULL, uses include_predictor setting multifit() available, otherwise TRUE. covariates_footer Logical. TRUE (default), displays footer listing covariates used adjusted models. Covariate names formatted using labels parameter provided. shown displaying adjusted results. indent_predictor Logical. TRUE, indents predictor levels outcome names, creating hierarchical display. FALSE (default), shows outcome predictor level separate columns. bold_variables Logical. TRUE, variable names displayed bold. FALSE (default), variable names displayed plain text. center_padding Numeric value specifying horizontal spacing table forest plot. Default 4. zebra_stripes Logical. TRUE, applies alternating gray background shading different outcomes improved readability. Default TRUE. color Character string specifying color point estimates forest plot. Default NULL, auto-selects based effect type: purple ("#8A61D8") hazard ratios (Cox), teal ("#4BA6B6") odds ratios (logistic), blue ("#3F87EE") rate/risk ratios (Poisson, Gamma, etc.), green ("#5A8F5A") coefficients (linear models). Use hex codes R color names custom colors. null_line Numeric value reference line position. Default NULL, uses 1 ratio measures (, HR, RR) 0 coefficients. log_scale Logical. TRUE, uses log scale x-axis. Default NULL, auto-detects (TRUE /HR/RR, FALSE coefficients). labels Named character vector providing custom display labels outcomes variables. Applied outcome names plot. Default NULL (uses labels already applied multifit, original names). units Character string specifying units plot dimensions: "" (inches), "cm", "mm". Default "". number_format Character string two-element character vector   controlling thousand decimal separators formatted output. Named   presets: "us" - Comma thousands, period decimal: 1,234.56 [default] "eu" - Period thousands, comma decimal: 1.234,56 "space" - Thin-space thousands, period decimal: 1 234.56       (SI/ISO 31-0) "none" - thousands separator: 1234.56 provide custom two-element vector c(big.mark, decimal.mark),   e.g., c("'", ".") Swiss-style: 1'234.56. NULL (default), uses   getOption("summata.number_format", "us"). Set global option   per session avoid passing argument repeatedly:
code: options(summata.number_format = "eu")

title: Create Forest Plot for Multivariate Regression — multiforest
text: ggplot object containing complete forest plot. plot   can : Displayed directly: print(plot) Saved file: ggsave("forest.pdf", plot, width = 12, height = 8) customized ggplot2 functions returned object includes attribute "rec_dims"   accessible via attr(plot, "rec_dims"), list   containing: width Numeric. Recommended plot width specified units height Numeric. Recommended plot height specified units recommendations automatically calculated based number   variables, text sizes, layout parameters, printed console   plot_width plot_height specified.

title: Create Forest Plot for Multivariate Regression — multiforest
text: Plot Layout: forest plot organized outcomes grouping headers predictor levels (interaction terms) rows within outcome. provides clear visual comparison single predictor affects multiple outcomes. Title: Centered top Data Table (left): Contains: Outcome column (grouped headers) Predictor/Group column n: Sample sizes (optional) Events: Event counts (optional, applicable models) Effect (95% CI); p-value Forest Plot (right): Point estimates (squares) 95% confidence intervals Reference line null value (1 0) Log scale ratio measures Data Source: function extracts effect estimates directly multifit output object's raw_data attribute, contains numeric values needed plotting. approach efficient ensures consistency formatted table output.

title: Create Forest Plot for Multivariate Regression — multiforest
code: if (FALSE) { # Load example data data(clintrial) data(clintrial_labels)  # Example 1: Basic multivariate forest plot outcomes <- c("surgery", "pfs_status", "os_status") result <- multifit(     data = clintrial,     outcomes = outcomes,     predictor = "treatment",     covariates = c("age", "sex", "stage"),     parallel = FALSE )  plot1 <- multiforest(result) print(plot1)    options(width = 180) # Example 2: With custom title and labels plot2 <- multiforest(     result,     title = "Treatment Effects Across Clinical Outcomes",     labels = clintrial_labels ) print(plot2)  # Example 3: Cox survival outcomes surv_result <- multifit(     data = clintrial,     outcomes = c("Surv(pfs_months, pfs_status)", "Surv(os_months, os_status)"),     predictor = "treatment",     covariates = c("age", "sex", "stage"),     model_type = "coxph",     parallel = FALSE )  plot3 <- multiforest(     surv_result,     title = "Treatment Effects on Survival Outcomes" ) print(plot3)  # Example 4: Customize appearance plot4 <- multiforest(     result,     color = "#E74C3C",     zebra_stripes = TRUE,     show_n = FALSE,     font_size = 1.2 ) print(plot4)  # Example 5: Save with recommended dimensions plot5 <- multiforest(result) dims <- attr(plot5, "rec_dims") # ggsave("multioutcome_forest.pdf", plot5, #        width = dims$width, height = dims$height) }

title: Normalize model type names — normalize_model_type
text: Converts model class names standardized model type strings.

title: Normalize model type names — normalize_model_type
code: normalize_model_type(model_type)

title: Normalize model type names — normalize_model_type
text: model_type Character string model type class name.

title: Normalize model type names — normalize_model_type
text: Normalized character string (e.g., "lmerMod" becomes "lmer").

title: Number Formatting Utilities — number_format
text: Internal utilities locale-aware number formatting across summata output functions. Supports preset locales (US, European, SI/ISO, plain) fully custom separator definitions.

title: Number Formatting Utilities — number_format
text: default number format can set per session: avoids passing number_format every function call.
code: options(summata.number_format = "eu")

what: Order comparison columns based on model type — order_comparison_columns
title: Order comparison columns based on model type — order_comparison_columns
text: Reorders columns comparison table follow logical sequence appropriate model type.

title: Order comparison columns based on model type — order_comparison_columns
code: order_comparison_columns(comparison, model_type)

title: Order comparison columns based on model type — order_comparison_columns
text: comparison Data.table comparison metrics. model_type Character string indicating model type.

title: Order comparison columns based on model type — order_comparison_columns
text: Data.table reordered columns.

what: Parse term into variable and group — parse_term
title: Parse term into variable and group — parse_term
text: Splits coefficient term names base variable names factor levels. example, "sexMale" becomes variable="sex" group="Male". Handles interaction terms continuous variables appropriately.

title: Parse term into variable and group — parse_term

what: Create Publication-Ready Survival Summary Tables — survtable
title: Create Publication-Ready Survival Summary Tables — survtable
text: Generates comprehensive survival summary tables survival probabilities specified time points, median survival times, optional group comparisons statistical testing. Designed creating survival summaries commonly used clinical epidemiological research publications.

title: Create Publication-Ready Survival Summary Tables — survtable
code: survtable(   data,   outcome,   by = NULL,   times = NULL,   probs = 0.5,   stats = c("survival", "ci"),   type = "survival",   conf_level = 0.95,   conf_type = "log",   digits = 0,   time_digits = 1,   p_digits = 3,   percent = TRUE,   test = TRUE,   test_type = "logrank",   total = TRUE,   total_label = "Total",   time_unit = NULL,   time_label = NULL,   median_label = NULL,   labels = NULL,   by_label = NULL,   na_rm = TRUE,   number_format = NULL,   ... )

title: Create Publication-Ready Survival Summary Tables — survtable
text: data Data frame data.table containing survival dataset. Automatically converted data.table efficient processing. outcome Character string character vector specifying one survival outcomes using Surv() syntax (e.g., "Surv(os_months, os_status)"). multiple outcomes provided, results stacked single table outcome labels row headers. Character string specifying column name stratifying variable group comparisons (e.g., treatment arm, risk group). NULL (default), produces overall survival summaries . times Numeric vector time points estimate survival probabilities. example, c(12, 24, 36) 1-, 2-, 3-year survival time measured months. Default NULL. probs Numeric vector survival probabilities estimate corresponding survival times (quantiles). Values must 0 1. example, c(0.5) returns median survival time, c(0.25, 0.5, 0.75) returns quartiles. Default 0.5 (median ). stats Character vector specifying statistics display: "survival" - Survival probability specified times "ci" - Confidence interval survival probability "n_risk" - Number risk time point "n_event" - Cumulative number events time     point Default c("survival", "ci"). type Character string specifying type probability report: "survival" - Survival probability S(t) [default] "risk" - Cumulative incidence/risk 1 - S(t) "cumhaz" - Cumulative hazard -log(S(t)) conf_level Numeric confidence level confidence intervals. Must 0 1. Default 0.95 (95% confidence intervals). conf_type Character string specifying confidence interval type survival estimates: "log" - Log transformation (default, recommended) "log-log" - Log-log transformation "plain" - Linear/identity (can produce CIs outside     [0, 1]) "logit" - Logit transformation "arcsin" - Arcsin square root transformation digits Integer specifying number decimal places survival probabilities (percentages). Default 0 (whole percentages). time_digits Integer specifying number decimal places survival time estimates (median, quantiles). Default 1. p_digits Integer specifying number decimal places p-values. Values smaller 10^(-p_digits) displayed "< 0.001" (p_digits = 3), "< 0.0001" (p_digits = 4), etc. Default 3. percent Logical. TRUE (default), displays survival probabilities percentages (e.g., "85%"). FALSE, displays proportions (e.g., "0.85"). test Logical. TRUE (default), performs survival curve comparison test adds p-value column. Requires specified. test_type Character string specifying statistical test comparing survival curves: "logrank" - Log-rank test (default) "wilcoxon" - Wilcoxon (Breslow) test "tarone" - Tarone-Ware test "petopeto" - Peto-Peto test total Logical character string controlling total/overall column: TRUE "first" - Include total column first     [default] "last" - Include total column last (    p-value) FALSE - Exclude total column total_label Character string total/overall row label. Default "Total". time_unit Character string specifying time unit display column headers labels (e.g., "months", "days", "years"). specified, time column headers become "{time} {time_unit}" (e.g., "12 months"). Default NULL (unit shown). time_label Character string template time column headers times specified. Use "\{time\}" placeholder time value "\{unit\}" time unit. Default "\{time\} \{unit\}" time_unit specified, otherwise just "\{time\}". median_label Character string median survival row label. Default NULL, auto-constructs conf_level (e.g., "Median (95% CI)" conf_level = 0.95). labels Named character vector list providing custom display labels. stratified analyses, names match levels variable. multiple outcomes, names match Surv() expressions. Default NULL. by_label Character string providing custom label stratifying variable (used output attributes headers). Default NULL (uses variable name). na_rm Logical. TRUE (default), observations missing values time, status, stratifying variable excluded. number_format Character string two-element character vector   controlling thousand decimal separators formatted output. Named   presets: "us" - Comma thousands, period decimal: 1,234.56 [default] "eu" - Period thousands, comma decimal: 1.234,56 "space" - Thin-space thousands, period decimal: 1 234.56       (SI/ISO 31-0) "none" - thousands separator: 1234.56 provide custom two-element vector c(big.mark, decimal.mark),   e.g., c("'", ".") Swiss-style: 1'234.56. NULL (default), uses   getOption("summata.number_format", "us"). Set global option   per session avoid passing argument repeatedly: ... Additional arguments passed survfit.
code: options(summata.number_format = "eu")

title: Create Publication-Ready Survival Summary Tables — survtable
text: data.table S3 class "survtable" containing formatted   survival statistics. table structure depends parameters: times specified (survival time points): Variable/Group Row identifier – stratifying variable levels Time columns Survival statistics requested time point p-value Test p-value (test = TRUE       specified) probs specified (survival quantiles): Variable/Group Row identifier – stratifying variable levels Quantile columns Time reach survival probability p-value Test p-value (test = TRUE       specified) numeric output (probabilities, times, counts, p-values)   respects number_format setting locale-appropriate   formatting. returned object includes following attributes: raw_data Data.table unformatted numeric values survfit_objects List survfit objects stratum by_variable stratifying variable name times time points requested probs probability quantiles requested test_result Full test result object (test performed)

title: Create Publication-Ready Survival Summary Tables — survtable
text: Survival Probability Estimation: Survival probabilities estimated using Kaplan-Meier method via survfit. specified time point, function reports estimated probability surviving beyond time. Confidence Intervals: default "log" transformation confidence intervals recommended ensures intervals remain within [0, 1] good statistical properties. "log-log" transformation also commonly used may perform better tails. Statistical Testing: log-rank test (default) tests null hypothesis survival curves identical across groups. Alternative tests weight different parts survival curve: Log-rank: Equal weights (best proportional hazards) Wilcoxon: Weights number risk (sensitive early     differences) Tarone-Ware: Weights square root number risk Peto-Peto: Modified Wilcoxon weights Formatting: numeric output respects number_format parameter. Separators within confidence intervals adapt automatically avoid ambiguity: Survival probabilities: "85% (80%-89%)" (US)     "85% (80%–89%)" (EU, en-dash separator) Median survival: "24.5 (21.2-28.9)" (US)     "24,5 (21,2–28,9)" (EU) Counts ≥ 1000: "1,234" (US)     "1.234" (EU) p-values: "< 0.001" (US)     "< 0,001" (EU)

title: Create Publication-Ready Survival Summary Tables — survtable
code: # Load example data data(clintrial)  # Example 1: Survival at specific time points by treatment survtable(     data = clintrial,     outcome = "Surv(os_months, os_status)",     by = "treatment",     times = c(12, 24, 36),     time_unit = "months" ) #>  #> Survival Summary Table #> Outcome: Surv(os_months, os_status) #> Stratified by: treatment #> Time points: 12, 24, 36 months #> Quantiles: 50% #> Statistic: Survival probability #> Test: Log-rank (p = < 0.001) #>  #>    Variable    12 months    24 months    36 months  Median (95% CI) p-value #>      <char>       <char>       <char>       <char>           <char>  <char> #> 1:    Total 59% (56-62%) 46% (43-49%) 36% (33-40%) 19.4 (16.2-23.4) < 0.001 #> 2:  Control 55% (49-63%) 37% (31-44%) 26% (20-33%) 14.7 (10.5-19.2)         #> 3:   Drug A 68% (63-74%) 56% (51-62%) 48% (42-54%) 33.6 (24.5-42.2)         #> 4:   Drug B 54% (49-59%) 43% (38-48%) 33% (28-38%) 14.7 (11.0-21.8)          # \donttest{ # Example 2: Median survival only survtable(     data = clintrial,     outcome = "Surv(os_months, os_status)",     by = "treatment",     times = NULL,     probs = 0.5 ) #>  #> Survival Summary Table #> Outcome: Surv(os_months, os_status) #> Stratified by: treatment #> Quantiles: 50% #> Statistic: Survival probability #> Test: Log-rank (p = < 0.001) #>  #>    Variable  Median (95% CI) p-value #>      <char>           <char>  <char> #> 1:    Total 19.4 (16.2-23.4) < 0.001 #> 2:  Control 14.7 (10.5-19.2)         #> 3:   Drug A 33.6 (24.5-42.2)         #> 4:   Drug B 14.7 (11.0-21.8)          # Example 3: Multiple quantiles (quartiles) survtable(     data = clintrial,     outcome = "Surv(os_months, os_status)",     by = "stage",     times = NULL,     probs = c(0.25, 0.5, 0.75) ) #>  #> Survival Summary Table #> Outcome: Surv(os_months, os_status) #> Stratified by: stage #> Quantiles: 25%, 50%, 75% #> Statistic: Survival probability #> Test: Log-rank (p = < 0.001) #>  #>    Variable 75% Survival Time (95% CI)  Median (95% CI) 25% Survival Time (95% CI) p-value #>      <char>                     <char>           <char>                     <char>  <char> #> 1:    Total              5.1 (4.3-6.1) 19.5 (16.1-23.5)                         NR < 0.001 #> 2:        I             9.0 (7.4-12.5) 33.7 (22.4-45.2)                         NR         #> 3:       II             7.7 (6.1-12.3) 32.2 (24.8-42.0)                         NR         #> 4:      III              3.9 (3.0-5.2) 13.7 (10.4-22.3)             44.7 (35.8-NR)         #> 5:       IV              2.0 (1.4-2.8)    5.9 (4.3-8.2)           15.6 (10.8-26.7)          # Example 4: Both time points and median survtable(     data = clintrial,     outcome = "Surv(os_months, os_status)",     by = "treatment",     times = c(12, 24),     probs = 0.5,     time_unit = "months" ) #>  #> Survival Summary Table #> Outcome: Surv(os_months, os_status) #> Stratified by: treatment #> Time points: 12, 24 months #> Quantiles: 50% #> Statistic: Survival probability #> Test: Log-rank (p = < 0.001) #>  #>    Variable    12 months    24 months  Median (95% CI) p-value #>      <char>       <char>       <char>           <char>  <char> #> 1:    Total 59% (56-62%) 46% (43-49%) 19.4 (16.2-23.4) < 0.001 #> 2:  Control 55% (49-63%) 37% (31-44%) 14.7 (10.5-19.2)         #> 3:   Drug A 68% (63-74%) 56% (51-62%) 33.6 (24.5-42.2)         #> 4:   Drug B 54% (49-59%) 43% (38-48%) 14.7 (11.0-21.8)          # Example 5: Cumulative incidence (1 - survival) survtable(     data = clintrial,     outcome = "Surv(os_months, os_status)",     by = "treatment",     times = c(12, 24),     type = "risk" ) #>  #> Survival Summary Table #> Outcome: Surv(os_months, os_status) #> Stratified by: treatment #> Time points: 12, 24 #> Quantiles: 50% #> Statistic: Cumulative incidence #> Test: Log-rank (p = < 0.001) #>  #>    Variable           12           24  Median (95% CI) p-value #>      <char>       <char>       <char>           <char>  <char> #> 1:    Total 41% (38-44%) 54% (51-57%) 19.4 (16.2-23.4) < 0.001 #> 2:  Control 45% (37-51%) 63% (56-69%) 14.7 (10.5-19.2)         #> 3:   Drug A 32% (26-37%) 44% (38-49%) 33.6 (24.5-42.2)         #> 4:   Drug B 46% (41-51%) 57% (52-62%) 14.7 (11.0-21.8)          # Example 6: Include number at risk survtable(     data = clintrial,     outcome = "Surv(os_months, os_status)",     by = "treatment",     times = c(12, 24),     stats = c("survival", "ci", "n_risk") ) #>  #> Survival Summary Table #> Outcome: Surv(os_months, os_status) #> Stratified by: treatment #> Time points: 12, 24 #> Quantiles: 50% #> Statistic: Survival probability #> Test: Log-rank (p = < 0.001) #>  #>    Variable                     12                     24  Median (95% CI) p-value #>      <char>                 <char>                 <char>           <char>  <char> #> 1:    Total 59% (56-62%) [n = 488] 46% (43-49%) [n = 369] 19.4 (16.2-23.4) < 0.001 #> 2:  Control 55% (49-63%) [n = 106]  37% (31-44%) [n = 70] 14.7 (10.5-19.2)         #> 3:   Drug A 68% (63-74%) [n = 192] 56% (51-62%) [n = 154] 33.6 (24.5-42.2)         #> 4:   Drug B 54% (49-59%) [n = 190] 43% (38-48%) [n = 145] 14.7 (11.0-21.8)          # Example 7: Overall survival without stratification survtable(     data = clintrial,     outcome = "Surv(os_months, os_status)",     times = c(12, 24, 36, 48) ) #>  #> Survival Summary Table #> Outcome: Surv(os_months, os_status) #> Time points: 12, 24, 36, 48 #> Quantiles: 50% #> Statistic: Survival probability #>  #>    Variable           12           24           36           48  Median (95% CI) #>      <char>       <char>       <char>       <char>       <char>           <char> #> 1:    Total 59% (56-62%) 46% (43-49%) 36% (33-40%) 30% (27-33%) 19.4 (16.2-23.4)  # Example 8: Without total row survtable(     data = clintrial,     outcome = "Surv(os_months, os_status)",     by = "treatment",     times = c(12, 24),     total = FALSE ) #>  #> Survival Summary Table #> Outcome: Surv(os_months, os_status) #> Stratified by: treatment #> Time points: 12, 24 #> Quantiles: 50% #> Statistic: Survival probability #> Test: Log-rank (p = < 0.001) #>  #>    Variable           12           24  Median (95% CI) p-value #>      <char>       <char>       <char>           <char>  <char> #> 1:  Control 55% (49-63%) 37% (31-44%) 14.7 (10.5-19.2) < 0.001 #> 2:   Drug A 68% (63-74%) 56% (51-62%) 33.6 (24.5-42.2)         #> 3:   Drug B 54% (49-59%) 43% (38-48%) 14.7 (11.0-21.8)          # Example 9: Custom labels survtable(     data = clintrial,     outcome = "Surv(os_months, os_status)",     by = "treatment",     times = c(12, 24),     labels = c("Drug A" = "Treatment A", "Drug B" = "Treatment B"),     time_unit = "months" ) #>  #> Survival Summary Table #> Outcome: Surv(os_months, os_status) #> Stratified by: treatment #> Time points: 12, 24 months #> Quantiles: 50% #> Statistic: Survival probability #> Test: Log-rank (p = < 0.001) #>  #>       Variable    12 months    24 months  Median (95% CI) p-value #>         <char>       <char>       <char>           <char>  <char> #> 1:       Total 59% (56-62%) 46% (43-49%) 19.4 (16.2-23.4) < 0.001 #> 2:     Control 55% (49-63%) 37% (31-44%) 14.7 (10.5-19.2)         #> 3: Treatment A 68% (63-74%) 56% (51-62%) 33.6 (24.5-42.2)         #> 4: Treatment B 54% (49-59%) 43% (38-48%) 14.7 (11.0-21.8)          # Example 10: Different confidence interval type survtable(     data = clintrial,     outcome = "Surv(os_months, os_status)",     by = "treatment",     times = c(12, 24),     conf_type = "log-log" ) #>  #> Survival Summary Table #> Outcome: Surv(os_months, os_status) #> Stratified by: treatment #> Time points: 12, 24 #> Quantiles: 50% #> Statistic: Survival probability #> Test: Log-rank (p = < 0.001) #>  #>    Variable           12           24  Median (95% CI) p-value #>      <char>       <char>       <char>           <char>  <char> #> 1:    Total 59% (56-62%) 46% (42-49%) 19.4 (16.1-22.9) < 0.001 #> 2:  Control 55% (48-62%) 37% (30-44%) 14.7 (10.2-19.0)         #> 3:   Drug A 68% (63-73%) 56% (50-62%) 33.6 (24.4-40.9)         #> 4:   Drug B 54% (48-59%) 43% (38-48%) 14.7 (10.9-21.7)          # Example 11: Wilcoxon test instead of log-rank survtable(     data = clintrial,     outcome = "Surv(os_months, os_status)",     by = "treatment",     times = c(12, 24),     test_type = "wilcoxon" ) #>  #> Survival Summary Table #> Outcome: Surv(os_months, os_status) #> Stratified by: treatment #> Time points: 12, 24 #> Quantiles: 50% #> Statistic: Survival probability #> Test: Wilcoxon (p = < 0.001) #>  #>    Variable           12           24  Median (95% CI) p-value #>      <char>       <char>       <char>           <char>  <char> #> 1:    Total 59% (56-62%) 46% (43-49%) 19.4 (16.2-23.4) < 0.001 #> 2:  Control 55% (49-63%) 37% (31-44%) 14.7 (10.5-19.2)         #> 3:   Drug A 68% (63-74%) 56% (51-62%) 33.6 (24.5-42.2)         #> 4:   Drug B 54% (49-59%) 43% (38-48%) 14.7 (11.0-21.8)          # Example 12: Access raw data for custom analysis result <- survtable(     data = clintrial,     outcome = "Surv(os_months, os_status)",     by = "treatment",     times = c(12, 24) ) raw <- attr(result, "raw_data") print(raw) #>    Variable 12_estimate  12_lower  12_upper 12_n_risk 12_n_event 24_estimate  24_lower  24_upper 24_n_risk 24_n_event Median (95% CI)_estimate Median (95% CI)_lower #>      <char>       <num>     <num>     <num>     <num>      <num>       <num>     <num>     <num>     <num>      <num>                    <num>                 <num> #> 1:    Total   0.5897650 0.5573977 0.6240118       488        345   0.4589077 0.4262187 0.4941037       369        107                     19.4                  16.2 #> 2:  Control   0.5518225 0.4860549 0.6264890       106         87   0.3686484 0.3063818 0.4435696        70         35                     14.7                  10.5 #> 3:   Drug A   0.6829195 0.6310704 0.7390285       192         91   0.5604338 0.5054384 0.6214131       154         34                     33.6                  24.5 #> 4:   Drug B   0.5361330 0.4869622 0.5902688       190        167   0.4272844 0.3788226 0.4819458       145         38                     14.7                  11.0 #>    Median (95% CI)_upper #>                    <num> #> 1:                  23.4 #> 2:                  19.2 #> 3:                  42.2 #> 4:                  21.8  # Example 13: Access survfit objects for plotting fits <- attr(result, "survfit_objects") # plot(fits$overall)  # Plot overall survival curve  # Example 14: Multiple survival outcomes stacked survtable(     data = clintrial,     outcome = c("Surv(pfs_months, pfs_status)", "Surv(os_months, os_status)"),     by = "treatment",     times = c(12, 24),     probs = 0.5,     time_unit = "months",     total = FALSE,     labels = c(         "Surv(pfs_months, pfs_status)" = "Progression-Free Survival",         "Surv(os_months, os_status)" = "Overall Survival"     ) ) #>  #> Survival Summary Table #> Outcomes: 2 #> Stratified by: treatment #> Time points: 12, 24 months #> Quantiles: 50% #> Statistic: Survival probability #> Test: Log-rank #>  #>                     Variable   Group    12 months    24 months  Median (95% CI) p-value #>                       <char>  <char>       <char>       <char>           <char>  <char> #> 1: Progression-Free Survival Control 29% (23-36%)  14% (9-20%)    4.7 (3.6-6.9) < 0.001 #> 2:                            Drug A 44% (39-51%) 30% (25-36%)   9.0 (7.5-12.4)         #> 3:                            Drug B 30% (26-36%) 18% (14-22%)    4.7 (3.7-6.2)         #> 4:          Overall Survival Control 55% (49-63%) 37% (31-44%) 14.7 (10.5-19.2) < 0.001 #> 5:                            Drug A 68% (63-74%) 56% (51-62%) 33.6 (24.5-42.2)         #> 6:                            Drug B 54% (49-59%) 43% (38-48%) 14.7 (11.0-21.8)          #
