Example 15: European number formatting survtable(     data = clintrial,     outcome = "Surv(os_months, os_status)",     by = "treatment",     times = c(12, 24),     number_format = "eu" ) #>  #> Survival Summary Table #> Outcome: Surv(os_months, os_status) #> Stratified by: treatment #> Time points: 12, 24 #> Quantiles: 50% #> Statistic: Survival probability #> Test: Log-rank (p = < 0.001) #>  #>    Variable           12           24  Median (95% CI) p-value #>      <char>       <char>       <char>           <char>  <char> #> 1:    Total 59% (56–62%) 46% (43–49%) 19,4 (16,2–23,4) < 0,001 #> 2:  Control 55% (49–63%) 37% (31–44%) 14,7 (10,5–19,2)         #> 3:   Drug A 68% (63–74%) 56% (51–62%) 33,6 (24,5–42,2)         #> 4:   Drug B 54% (49–59%) 43% (38–48%) 14,7 (11,0–21,8)          # }

what: Export Table to Microsoft Word Format (DOCX) — table2docx
title: Export Table to Microsoft Word Format (DOCX) — table2docx
text: Converts data frame, data.table, matrix fully editable Microsoft Word document (.docx) using flextable officer packages. Creates publication-ready tables extensive formatting options including typography, alignment, colors, page layout. Tables can edited Microsoft Word creation.

title: Export Table to Microsoft Word Format (DOCX) — table2docx
code: table2docx(   table,   file,   caption = NULL,   font_size = 8,   font_family = "Arial",   format_headers = TRUE,   bold_significant = TRUE,   bold_variables = FALSE,   p_threshold = 0.05,   indent_groups = FALSE,   condense_table = FALSE,   condense_quantitative = FALSE,   zebra_stripes = FALSE,   dark_header = FALSE,   paper = "letter",   orientation = "portrait",   width = NULL,   align = NULL,   return_ft = FALSE,   ... )

title: Export Table to Microsoft Word Format (DOCX) — table2docx
text: table Data frame, data.table, matrix export. Can output desctable(), survtable(), fit(), uniscreen(), fullfit(), compfit(), multifit(), tabular data. file Character string specifying output DOCX filename. Must .docx extension. Example: "results.docx", "Table1.docx". caption Character string. Optional caption displayed table Word document. Default NULL. font_size Numeric. Base font size points table content. Default 8. Typical range: 8-12 points. Headers use slightly larger size. font_family Character string. Font family name table. Must font installed system. Default "Arial". Common options: "Times New Roman", "Calibri", "Helvetica". format_headers Logical. TRUE, formats column headers italicizing statistical notation ("n", "p"), converting underscores spaces, improving readability. Default TRUE. bold_significant Logical. TRUE, applies bold formatting p-values significance threshold. Makes significant results stand . Default TRUE. bold_variables Logical. TRUE, variable names displayed bold. Default FALSE. p_threshold Numeric. Threshold bold p-value formatting. used bold_significant = TRUE. Default 0.05. indent_groups Logical. TRUE, indents factor levels parent variable using horizontal spacing, creating hierarchical display. Useful categorical variables regression tables. Default FALSE. condense_table Logical. TRUE, condenses table showing essential rows (single row continuous, non-reference binary). Automatically sets indent_groups = TRUE. Significantly reduces table height. Default FALSE. condense_quantitative Logical. TRUE, condenses continuous survival variables single rows preserving categorical variable rows (including binary). applies descriptive tables desctable(). Automatically sets indent_groups = TRUE. Unlike condense_table, collapse binary categorical variables. Default FALSE. zebra_stripes Logical. TRUE, applies alternating row shading different variables (individual rows) visual grouping. Default FALSE. dark_header Logical. TRUE, creates dark background light text header row, providing strong visual contrast. Default FALSE. paper Character string specifying paper size: "letter" - US Letter (8.5" × 11") [default] "a4" - A4 (210 mm × 297 mm) "legal" - US Legal (8.5" × 14") orientation Character string specifying page orientation: "portrait" - Vertical [default] "landscape" - Horizontal (wide tables) width Numeric. Table width inches. NULL (default), automatically fits content page width. Specify control exactly. align Character vector specifying column alignment column. Options: "left", "center", "right". NULL (default), automatically determines based content (text left, numbers right). Example: c("left", "left", "center", "right", "right"). return_ft Logical. TRUE, returns flextable object directly customization. FALSE (default), returns invisibly flextable object attribute. See Details usage. Default FALSE. ... Additional arguments passed read_docx document initialization.

title: Export Table to Microsoft Word Format (DOCX) — table2docx
text: Behavior depends return_ft: return_ft = FALSE Invisibly returns list components: file - Path created file caption - Caption text (provided) flextable object accessible via attr(result, "flextable") return_ft = TRUE Directly returns flextable object       immediate customization cases, creates .docx file specified location.

title: Export Table to Microsoft Word Format (DOCX) — table2docx
text: Package Requirements: function requires: flextable - creating formatted tables officer - Word document manipulation Install needed: Output Features: generated Word document contains: Fully editable table (native Word table, image) Professional typography spacing Proper page setup (size, orientation, margins) Caption (provided) separate paragraph table formatting preserved editable Compatible Word 2007 later Customization: programmatic customization beyond built-options, access flextable object: Method 1: Via attribute (default) Method 2: Direct return Page Layout: function automatically sets Word document : Specified paper size orientation Standard margins (1 inch default) Continuous section (page breaks table) Left-aligned table placement landscape orientation: Automatically swaps page width height Applies landscape property section Useful wide tables many columns Table Width Management: Width behavior: width = NULL - Auto-fits content page width width = 6 - Exactly 6 inches wide Width distributed evenly across columns default Can adjust individual column widths Word creation wide tables: Use orientation = "landscape" Use paper = "legal" extra width Reduce font_size Use condense_table = TRUE Consider breaking across multiple tables Typography: function applies professional typography: Column headers: Bold, slightly larger font Body text: Regular weight, specified font size Numbers: Right-aligned easy comparison Text: Left-aligned readability Consistent spacing: Adequate padding cells Font family must installed system Word opens document. Common cross-platform choices: Arial - Sans-serif, highly readable Times New Roman - Serif, traditional Calibri - Microsoft default, modern Helvetica - Sans-serif, professional Zebra Striping: zebra_stripes = TRUE: Alternating variables receive light gray background rows variable share shading Improves visual grouping Particularly useful tables many factor variables Color can changed Word creation Dark Header: dark_header = TRUE: Header row: Dark gray/black background Header text: White high contrast Modern, professional appearance Draws attention column names Integration R Markdown/Quarto: R Markdown/Quarto Word output: use flextable directly chunks:
code: install.packages(c("flextable", "officer")) result <- table2docx(table, "output.docx") ft <- attr(result, "flextable")  # Customize flextable ft <- flextable::bold(ft, i = 1, j = 1, part = "body") ft <- flextable::color(ft, i = 2, j = 3, color = "red")  # Re-save if needed doc <- officer::read_docx() doc <- flextable::body_add_flextable(doc, ft) print(doc, target = "customized.docx") ft <- table2docx(table, "output.docx", return_ft = TRUE)  # Customize immediately ft <- flextable::bg(ft, bg = "yellow", part = "header") ft <- flextable::autofit(ft)  # Save to new document doc <- officer::read_docx() doc <- flextable::body_add_flextable(doc, ft) print(doc, target = "custom.docx") # Create flextable for inline display ft <- table2docx(results, "temp.docx", return_ft = TRUE)  # Display in R Markdown chunk ft  # Renders in Word output flextable::flextable(results)

title: Export Table to Microsoft Word Format (DOCX) — table2docx
code: if (FALSE) { options(width = 180) # Load data data(clintrial) data(clintrial_labels)  # Create regression table results <- fit(    data = clintrial,    outcome = "os_status",    predictors = c("age", "sex", "treatment", "stage"),    labels = clintrial_labels )  # Example 1: Basic Word export table2docx(results, "results.docx")  # Example 2: With caption table2docx(results, "captioned.docx",         caption = "Table 1: Multivariable Logistic Regression Results")  # Example 3: Landscape orientation for wide tables table2docx(results, "wide.docx",         orientation = "landscape")  # Example 4: Custom font and size table2docx(results, "custom_font.docx",         font_family = "Times New Roman",         font_size = 11)  # Example 5: Hierarchical display table2docx(results, "indented.docx",         indent_groups = TRUE)  # Example 6: Condensed table table2docx(results, "condensed.docx",         condense_table = TRUE)  # Example 7: With zebra stripes table2docx(results, "striped.docx",         zebra_stripes = TRUE)  # Example 8: Dark header style table2docx(results, "dark.docx",         dark_header = TRUE)  # Example 9: A4 paper for international journals table2docx(results, "a4.docx",         paper = "a4")  # Example 10: Get flextable for customization result <- table2docx(results, "base.docx") ft <- attr(result, "flextable")  # Customize the flextable ft <- flextable::bold(ft, i = 1, part = "body") ft <- flextable::color(ft, j = "p-value", color = "blue")  # Example 11: Direct flextable return ft <- table2docx(results, "direct.docx", return_ft = TRUE) ft <- flextable::bg(ft, bg = "yellow", part = "header")  # Example 12: Publication-ready table table2docx(results, "publication.docx",         caption = "Table 2: Adjusted Odds Ratios for Mortality",         font_family = "Times New Roman",         font_size = 10,         indent_groups = TRUE,         zebra_stripes = FALSE,         bold_significant = TRUE)  # Example 13: Custom column alignment table2docx(results, "aligned.docx",         align = c("left", "left", "center", "right", "right"))  # Example 14: Disable significance bolding table2docx(results, "no_bold.docx",         bold_significant = FALSE)  # Example 15: Stricter significance threshold table2docx(results, "strict.docx",         bold_significant = TRUE,         p_threshold = 0.01) }

what: Export Table to HTML Format — table2html
title: Export Table to HTML Format — table2html
text: Converts data frame, data.table, matrix HTML format optional CSS styling web display, HTML documents, embedding web applications. Generates clean, standards-compliant HTML professional styling options including responsive design support, color schemes, interactive features. Requires xtable export.

title: Export Table to HTML Format — table2html
code: table2html(   table,   file,   caption = NULL,   format_headers = TRUE,   variable_padding = FALSE,   bold_significant = TRUE,   bold_variables = FALSE,   p_threshold = 0.05,   indent_groups = FALSE,   condense_table = FALSE,   condense_quantitative = FALSE,   zebra_stripes = FALSE,   stripe_color = "#EEEEEE",   dark_header = FALSE,   include_css = TRUE,   ... )

title: Export Table to HTML Format — table2html
text: table Data frame, data.table, matrix export. Can output desctable(), survtable(), fit(), uniscreen(), fullfit(), compfit(), multifit(), tabular data. file Character string specifying output HTML filename. Must .html .htm extension. Example: "results.html". caption Character string. Optional caption displayed table. Supports basic HTML formatting. Default NULL. format_headers Logical. TRUE, formats column headers converting underscores spaces applying proper casing. Wraps statistical notation ("n", "p") <> tags. Default TRUE. variable_padding Logical. TRUE, adds vertical spacing around variable groups improved readability. Particularly useful multi-row factor variables. Default FALSE. bold_significant Logical. TRUE, wraps significant p-values <b> tags bold display. Makes important results stand visually. Default TRUE. bold_variables Logical. TRUE, variable names displayed bold. Default FALSE. p_threshold Numeric. Threshold bold p-value formatting. used bold_significant = TRUE. Default 0.05. indent_groups Logical. TRUE, indents grouped rows using non-breaking spaces ( ) hierarchical display. Useful factor variables regression output. Default FALSE. condense_table Logical. TRUE, condenses table showing essential rows. Automatically sets indent_groups = TRUE. Default FALSE. condense_quantitative Logical. TRUE, condenses continuous survival variables single rows preserving categorical variable rows (including binary). applies descriptive tables desctable(). Automatically sets indent_groups = TRUE. Unlike condense_table, collapse binary categorical variables. Default FALSE. zebra_stripes Logical. TRUE, applies alternating background shading different variables (individual rows) visual grouping. Default FALSE. stripe_color Character string. HTML color specification zebra stripes. Can use hex codes ("#EEEEEE"), RGB ("rgb(238,238,238)"), color names ("lightgray"). Default "#EEEEEE". dark_header Logical. TRUE, creates black background white text header row. Provides strong visual contrast. Default FALSE. include_css Logical. TRUE, includes embedded CSS styling output file standalone HTML. Set FALSE embedding existing HTML stylesheet. Default TRUE. ... Additional arguments passed xtable.

title: Export Table to HTML Format — table2html
text: Invisibly returns NULL. Creates HTML file specified   location can opened web browsers embedded HTML documents.

title: Export Table to HTML Format — table2html
text: Output Format: function generates standards-compliant HTML5 markup : Semantic <table> structure Proper <thead> <tbody> sections Accessible header cells (<th>) Clean, readable markup Optional embedded CSS styling Standalone vs. Embedded: Standalone HTML (include_css = TRUE): Can opened directly web browsers Includes necessary styling Self-contained, portable Suitable sharing via email web hosting Embedded HTML (include_css = FALSE): inclusion existing HTML documents CSS included (use parent document's styles) Smaller file size Integrates web frameworks (Shiny, R Markdown, Quarto) CSS Styling: include_css = TRUE, function applies professional styling: Table: Border-collapse, sans-serif font (Arial), 20px margin Cells: 8px vertical × 12px horizontal padding, left-aligned text Borders: 1px solid #DDD (light gray) Headers: Bold text, light gray background (#F2F2F2) Numeric columns: Center-aligned (auto-detected) Caption: Bold, 1.1em font, positioned table dark_header = TRUE: Header background: Black (#000000) Header text: White (#FFFFFF) Creates high contrast, modern appearance zebra_stripes = TRUE: Alternating variable groups receive background color Default color: #EEEEEE (light gray) Applied via CSS class .zebra-stripe Groups entire variable (factor levels together) Hierarchical Display: indent_groups option creates visual hierarchy using HTML non-breaking spaces: Integration R Markdown/Quarto: R Markdown Quarto documents: include document chunk results='asis': directly render without file: Integration Shiny: Shiny applications: Accessibility: generated HTML follows accessibility best practices: Semantic table structure Proper header cells (<th>) scope attributes Clear visual hierarchy Adequate color contrast (using default styles) Screen reader friendly markup
code: Treatment     Control     Active # Generate HTML fragment (no CSS) table2html(results, "table.html", include_css = FALSE) cat(readLines("table.html"), sep = "\n") # For inline display htmltools::HTML( capture.output( print(xtable::xtable(results), type = "html") ) ) # In server function output$results_table <- renderUI({ table2html(results_data(), "temp.html", include_css = FALSE) HTML(readLines("temp.html")) }) # Or use directly with DT package for interactive tables output$interactive_table <- DT::renderDT({ results_data() })

title: Export Table to HTML Format — table2html
code: if (FALSE) { # Load data and create table data(clintrial) data(clintrial_labels)  results <- fit(      data = clintrial,      outcome = "os_status",      predictors = c("age", "sex", "treatment", "stage"),      labels = clintrial_labels )  # Example 1: Basic HTML export (standalone) table2html(results, "results.html") # Open results.html in web browser  # Example 2: With caption table2html(results, "captioned.html",           caption = "Table 1: Multivariable Logistic Regression Results")  # Example 3: For embedding (no CSS) table2html(results, "embed.html",           include_css = FALSE) # Include in your HTML document  # Example 4: Hierarchical display table2html(results, "indented.html",           indent_groups = TRUE)  # Example 5: Condensed table table2html(results, "condensed.html",           condense_table = TRUE)  # Example 6: With zebra stripes table2html(results, "striped.html",           zebra_stripes = TRUE,           stripe_color = "#F0F0F0")  # Example 7: Dark header style table2html(results, "dark.html",           dark_header = TRUE)  # Example 8: Combination styling table2html(results, "styled.html",           zebra_stripes = TRUE,           dark_header = TRUE,           bold_significant = TRUE)  # Example 9: Custom stripe color table2html(results, "blue_stripes.html",           zebra_stripes = TRUE,           stripe_color = "#E3F2FD")  # Light blue  # Example 10: Disable significance bolding table2html(results, "no_bold.html",           bold_significant = FALSE)  # Example 11: Stricter significance threshold table2html(results, "strict.html",           bold_significant = TRUE,           p_threshold = 0.01)  # Example 12: No header formatting table2html(results, "raw_headers.html",           format_headers = FALSE)  # Example 13: Descriptive statistics table desc_table <- desctable(      data = clintrial,      by = "treatment",      variables = c("age", "sex", "bmi"),      labels = clintrial_labels )  table2html(desc_table, "baseline.html",           caption = "Table 1: Baseline Characteristics by Treatment Group")  # Example 14: For R Markdown (no CSS, for inline display) table2html(results, "rmd_table.html",           include_css = FALSE,           indent_groups = TRUE)  # Then in R Markdown: # ```{r results='asis', echo=FALSE} # cat(readLines("rmd_table.html"), sep = "\n") # ```  # Example 15: Email-friendly version table2html(results, "email.html",           include_css = TRUE,  # Self-contained           zebra_stripes = TRUE,           caption = "Regression Results - See Attached") # Can be directly included in HTML emails  # Example 16: Publication-ready web version table2html(results, "publication.html",           caption = "Table 2: Multivariable Analysis of Risk Factors",           indent_groups = TRUE,           zebra_stripes = FALSE,  # Clean look           bold_significant = TRUE,           dark_header = FALSE)  # Example 17: Modern dark theme table2html(results, "dark_theme.html",           dark_header = TRUE,           stripe_color = "#2A2A2A",  # Dark gray stripes           zebra_stripes = TRUE)  # Example 18: Minimal styling for custom CSS table2html(results, "minimal.html",           include_css = FALSE,           format_headers = FALSE,           bold_significant = FALSE) # Apply your own CSS classes and styling  # Example 19: Model comparison table models <- list(      base = c("age", "sex"),      full = c("age", "sex", "treatment", "stage") )  comparison <- compfit(      data = clintrial,      outcome = "os_status",      model_list = models )  table2html(comparison, "comparison.html",           caption = "Model Comparison Statistics") }

what: Export Table to PDF Format — table2pdf
title: Export Table to PDF Format — table2pdf
text: Converts data frame, data.table, matrix professionally formatted PDF document using LaTeX intermediate format. Provides extensive control page layout, typography, formatting publication-ready output. Particularly well-suited tables regression analyses, descriptive statistics, model comparisons. Requires xtable export.

title: Export Table to PDF Format — table2pdf
code: table2pdf(   table,   file,   orientation = "portrait",   paper = "letter",   margins = NULL,   fit_to_page = TRUE,   font_size = 8,   caption = NULL,   caption_size = NULL,   format_headers = TRUE,   variable_padding = FALSE,   cell_padding = "normal",   bold_significant = TRUE,   bold_variables = FALSE,   p_threshold = 0.05,   align = NULL,   indent_groups = FALSE,   condense_table = FALSE,   condense_quantitative = FALSE,   zebra_stripes = FALSE,   stripe_color = "gray!20",   dark_header = FALSE,   show_logs = FALSE,   ... )

title: Export Table to PDF Format — table2pdf
text: table Data frame, data.table, matrix export. Can output desctable(), survtable(), fit(), uniscreen(), fullfit(), compfit(), tabular data structure. file Character string specifying output PDF filename. Must .pdf extension. Example: "results.pdf", "Table1.pdf". orientation Character string specifying page orientation: "portrait" - Vertical orientation [default] "landscape" - Horizontal orientation (recommended wide tables) paper Character string specifying paper size: "letter" - US Letter (8.5" x 11") [default] "a4" - A4 (210 mm x 297 mm) "legal" - US Legal (8.5" x 14") "auto" - Auto-size content (margins, crops fit) margins Numeric vector length 4 specifying margins inches c(top, right, bottom, left). Default c(1, 1, 1, 1). Ignored paper = "auto". fit_to_page Logical. TRUE, scales table fit within text width (respects margins). Useful wide tables otherwise overflow. Default TRUE. font_size Numeric. Base font size points. Default 8. Smaller values accommodate content; larger values improve readability. Typical range: 6-12 points. caption Character string. Optional caption displayed table. Supports LaTeX formatting multi-line captions, superscripts, italics, etc. See Details formatting guidance. Default NULL. caption_size Numeric. Caption font size points. NULL (default), uses base font_size. Set specific value (e.g., 6, 7, 8) control caption size independently table font size. Useful fitting captions constrained page sizes. Typical range: 6-10 points. format_headers Logical. TRUE, applies automatic formatting column headers: converts underscores spaces, italicizes statistical notation ("n", "p"), improves readability. Default TRUE. variable_padding Logical. TRUE, adds vertical spacing different variables table, creating visual grouping. Particularly useful regression tables multiple predictors. Default FALSE. cell_padding Character string numeric specifying vertical padding within table cells: "none" - extra padding (compact) "normal" - Standard padding [default] "relaxed" - Increased padding "loose" - Maximum padding Numeric value - Custom multiplier (e.g., 1.5) Adjusts \arraystretch LaTeX. bold_significant Logical. TRUE, applies bold formatting p-values significance threshold, making significant results stand visually. Default TRUE. bold_variables Logical. TRUE, variable names displayed bold. Default FALSE. p_threshold Numeric. Threshold bold p-value formatting. used bold_significant = TRUE. Default 0.05. align Character string vector specifying column alignment. Options: "l" - Left aligned "c" - Center aligned "r" - Right aligned NULL (default), automatically determines alignment based content (text left, numbers right). Can specify per-column: c("l", "l", "r", "r"). indent_groups Logical. TRUE, indents factor levels/groups parent variable using horizontal space, creating hierarchical display. Useful factor variables regression tables. Default FALSE. condense_table Logical. TRUE, condenses table : Showing one row per continuous variable (estimate + CI) Showing non-reference categories binary factors Automatically setting indent_groups = TRUE Significantly reduces table height. Default FALSE. condense_quantitative Logical. TRUE, condenses continuous survival variables single rows preserving categorical variable rows (including binary). applies descriptive tables desctable(). Automatically sets indent_groups = TRUE. Unlike condense_table, collapse binary categorical variables. Default FALSE. zebra_stripes Logical. TRUE, applies alternating gray background shading different variables (individual rows) improved visual grouping readability. Default FALSE. stripe_color Character string. LaTeX color specification zebra stripes. Default "gray!20" (20% gray). Can use colors like "blue!10", "red!15". Requires zebra_stripes = TRUE. dark_header Logical. TRUE, creates dark (black) background white text header row. Provides strong visual contrast. Default FALSE. show_logs Logical. TRUE, retains LaTeX log auxiliary files PDF compilation troubleshooting. FALSE, deletes files. Default FALSE. ... Additional arguments passed xtable advanced LaTeX table customization.

title: Export Table to PDF Format — table2pdf
text: Invisibly returns NULL. Creates PDF file specified   location. compilation fails, check .log file (  show_logs = TRUE) error details.

title: Export Table to PDF Format — table2pdf
text: LaTeX Requirements: function requires working LaTeX installation. function checks LaTeX availability provides installation guidance missing. Recommended LaTeX distributions: TinyTeX (lightweight, R-integrated): Install via     tinytex::install_tinytex() TeX Live (comprehensive, cross-platform) MiKTeX (Windows) MacTeX (macOS) Required LaTeX packages (auto-installed distributions): fontenc, inputenc - Character encoding array, booktabs, longtable - Table formatting graphicx - Scaling tables geometry - Page layout pdflscape, lscape - Landscape orientation helvet - Sans-serif fonts standalone, varwidth - Auto-sizing (paper = "auto") float, caption - Floats captions xcolor, colortable - Colors (zebra_stripes dark_header) Caption Formatting: Captions support LaTeX commands rich formatting: Auto-Sizing (paper = "auto"): paper = "auto", function attempts create minimal PDF sized exactly table content: Using standalone LaTeX class (cleanest output) Fallback pdfcrop utility standalone unavailable Fallback minimal margins neither available Table Width Management: wide tables fit page: Use orientation = "landscape" Use fit_to_page = TRUE (default) auto-scale Reduce font_size (e.g., 7 6) Consider paper = "auto" maximum flexibility Troubleshooting: PDF compilation fails: Check LaTeX installed: Run Sys.("pdflatex") Set show_logs = TRUE examine .log file Common issues: Missing LaTeX packages: Install via package manager Special characters text: Escape properly wide tables: Use landscape reduce font size Caption formatting: Check LaTeX syntax
code: # Multi-line caption with line breaks caption = "Table 1: Multivariable Analysis\\           OR = odds ratio; CI = confidence interval"  # With superscripts (using LaTeX syntax) caption = "Table 1: Results\\           Adjusted for age and sex\\           p-values from Wald tests"  # With special characters (must escape percent signs) caption = "Results for income (in thousands)"

title: Export Table to PDF Format — table2pdf
code: if (FALSE) { # These examples require a LaTeX installation (pdflatex) # Install TinyTeX with: tinytex::install_tinytex()  # Load example data and create a regression table data(clintrial) data(clintrial_labels) library(survival)  # Create a regression table results <- fit(      data = clintrial,      outcome = "os_status",      predictors = c("age", "sex", "treatment", "stage"),      labels = clintrial_labels )  # Example 1: Basic PDF export table2pdf(results, "basic_results.pdf")  # Example 2: Landscape orientation for wide tables table2pdf(results, "wide_results.pdf",          orientation = "landscape")  # Example 3: With caption table2pdf(results, "captioned.pdf",          caption = "Table 1: Multivariable logistic regression results")  # Example 4: Multi-line caption with formatting table2pdf(results, "formatted_caption.pdf",          caption = "Table 1: Risk Factors for Mortality\\\\                    aOR = adjusted odds ratio; CI = confidence interval")  # Example 5: Auto-sized PDF (no fixed page dimensions) table2pdf(results, "autosize.pdf",          paper = "auto")  # Example 6: A4 paper with custom margins table2pdf(results, "a4_custom.pdf",          paper = "a4",          margins = c(0.75, 0.75, 0.75, 0.75))  # Example 7: Larger font for readability table2pdf(results, "large_font.pdf",          font_size = 11)  # Example 8: Indented hierarchical display table2pdf(results, "indented.pdf",          indent_groups = TRUE)  # Example 9: Condensed table (reduced height) table2pdf(results, "condensed.pdf",          condense_table = TRUE)  # Example 10: With zebra stripes table2pdf(results, "striped.pdf",          zebra_stripes = TRUE,          stripe_color = "gray!15")  # Example 11: Dark header style table2pdf(results, "dark_header.pdf",          dark_header = TRUE)  # Example 12: Combination of formatting options table2pdf(results, "publication_ready.pdf",          orientation = "portrait",          paper = "letter",          font_size = 9,          caption = "Table 2: Multivariable Analysis\\\\                    Model adjusted for age, sex, and clinical factors",          indent_groups = TRUE,          zebra_stripes = TRUE,          bold_significant = TRUE,          p_threshold = 0.05)  # Example 13: Adjust cell padding table2pdf(results, "relaxed_padding.pdf",          cell_padding = "relaxed")  # More spacious  # Example 14: No scaling (natural table width) table2pdf(results, "no_scale.pdf",          fit_to_page = FALSE,          font_size = 10)  # Example 15: Hide significance bolding table2pdf(results, "no_bold.pdf",          bold_significant = FALSE)  # Example 16: Custom column alignment table2pdf(results, "custom_align.pdf",          align = c("c", "c", "c", "c", "c", "c", "c"))  # Example 17: Descriptive statistics table desc_table <- desctable(      data = clintrial,      by = "treatment",      variables = c("age", "sex", "bmi", "stage"),      labels = clintrial_labels )  table2pdf(desc_table, "descriptive.pdf",          caption = "Table 1: Baseline Characteristics by Treatment Group",          orientation = "landscape")  # Example 18: Model comparison table models <- list(      base = c("age", "sex"),      full = c("age", "sex", "bmi", "treatment") )  comparison <- compfit(      data = clintrial,      outcome = "os_status",      model_list = models )  table2pdf(comparison, "model_comparison.pdf",          caption = "Table 3: Model Comparison Statistics")  # Example 19: Very wide table with aggressive fitting wide_model <- fit(      data = clintrial,      outcome = "os_status",      predictors = c("age", "sex", "race", "bmi", "smoking",                     "hypertension", "diabetes", "treatment", "stage") )  table2pdf(wide_model, "very_wide.pdf",          orientation = "landscape",          paper = "legal",  # Even wider than letter          font_size = 7,          fit_to_page = TRUE,          condense_table = TRUE)  # Example 20: With caption size control table2pdf(results, "caption_size.pdf",          font_size = 8,          caption_size = 6,          caption = "Table 4: Results with Compact Caption\\\\                    Smaller caption fits better on constrained pages")  # Example 21: Troubleshooting - keep logs table2pdf(results, "debug.pdf",          show_logs = TRUE) # If it fails, check debug.log for error messages }

what: Export Table to Microsoft PowerPoint Format (PPTX) — table2pptx
title: Export Table to Microsoft PowerPoint Format (PPTX) — table2pptx
text: Converts data frame, data.table, matrix Microsoft PowerPoint slide (.pptx) formatted table using flextable officer packages. Creates presentation-ready slides extensive control table formatting, positioning, layout. Tables can edited PowerPoint creation. Ideal creating data-driven presentations conference talks.

title: Export Table to Microsoft PowerPoint Format (PPTX) — table2pptx
code: table2pptx(   table,   file,   caption = NULL,   font_size = 10,   font_family = "Arial",   format_headers = TRUE,   bold_significant = TRUE,   bold_variables = FALSE,   p_threshold = 0.05,   indent_groups = FALSE,   condense_table = FALSE,   condense_quantitative = FALSE,   zebra_stripes = FALSE,   dark_header = FALSE,   width = NULL,   align = NULL,   template = NULL,   layout = "Title and Content",   master = "Office Theme",   left = 0.5,   top = 1.5,   return_ft = FALSE,   ... )

title: Export Table to Microsoft PowerPoint Format (PPTX) — table2pptx
text: table Data frame, data.table, matrix export. Can output desctable(), survtable(), fit(), uniscreen(), fullfit(), compfit(), multifit(), tabular data. file Character string specifying output PPTX filename. Must .pptx extension. Example: "results.pptx", "Slide1.pptx". caption Character string. Optional title displayed slide's title placeholder text box table. Default NULL. font_size Numeric. Base font size points table content. Default 10. Typical range presentations: 10-14 points. Larger print documents visibility distance. font_family Character string. Font family name table. Must installed system. Default "Arial". Common presentation fonts: "Calibri", "Helvetica", "Arial". format_headers Logical. TRUE, formats column headers italicizing statistical notation ("n", "p") improving readability. Default TRUE. bold_significant Logical. TRUE, applies bold formatting p-values significance threshold. Makes important results stand presentations. Default TRUE. bold_variables Logical. TRUE, variable names displayed bold. Default FALSE. p_threshold Numeric. Threshold bold p-value formatting. used bold_significant = TRUE. Default 0.05. indent_groups Logical. TRUE, indents factor levels parent variable, creating hierarchical display. Useful categorical variables. Default FALSE. condense_table Logical. TRUE, condenses table essential rows . Automatically sets indent_groups = TRUE. Crucial fitting content slides. Default FALSE. condense_quantitative Logical. TRUE, condenses continuous survival variables single rows preserving categorical variable rows (including binary). applies descriptive tables desctable(). Automatically sets indent_groups = TRUE. Unlike condense_table, collapse binary categorical variables. Default FALSE. zebra_stripes Logical. TRUE, applies alternating row shading different variables visual grouping. Improves readability presentations. Default FALSE. dark_header Logical. TRUE, creates dark background light text header row. Provides strong contrast visible distance. Default FALSE. width Numeric. Table width inches. NULL (default), auto-fits slide width (approximately 9 inches standard 10-inch slide margins). Specify exact control. align Character vector specifying column alignment. Options: "left", "center", "right". NULL (default), automatically determines based content. template Character string. Path custom PPTX template file. NULL (default), uses officer's default blank template. Use match corporate branding conference themes. layout Character string. Name slide layout use template. Default "Title Content". Common layouts: "Title Content" - Standard layout [default] "Blank" - Empty slide maximum control "Title " - Title area "Two Content" - Title two content areas master Character string. Name slide master use. Default "Office Theme". Varies template. Check template available masters. left Numeric. Horizontal position left edge slide inches. Default 0.5. Standard slide 10 inches wide. top Numeric. Vertical position top edge slide inches. Default 1.5 (leaves room title). Standard slide 7.5 inches tall. Adjust based table size layout. return_ft Logical. TRUE, returns flextable object directly customization. FALSE (default), returns invisibly flextable object attribute. See Details usage. Default FALSE. ... Additional arguments passed read_pptx.

title: Export Table to Microsoft PowerPoint Format (PPTX) — table2pptx
text: Behavior depends return_ft: return_ft = FALSE Invisibly returns list : file - Path created file caption - Caption/title text layout - Layout name used master - Master name used template - Template path (provided) position - List left top coordinates Flextable accessible via attr(result, "flextable") return_ft = TRUE Directly returns flextable object Always creates .pptx file specified location.

title: Export Table to Microsoft PowerPoint Format (PPTX) — table2pptx
text: Package Requirements: Requires: flextable - Table creation formatting officer - PowerPoint manipulation Install: install.packages(c("flextable", "officer")) Slide Dimensions: Standard PowerPoint slide: Width: 10 inches (25.4 cm) Height: 7.5 inches (19.05 cm) Aspect ratio: 4:3 (standard) 16:9 (widescreen) Safe content area (margins): Width: ~9 inches Height: ~6 inches (accounting title) Positioning: left top parameters control table placement: (0, 0) = Top-left corner slide Default (0.5, 1.5) = Standard position title room Center: left = (10 - table_width) / 2 caption provided: Attempts use title placeholder (layout supports) Falls back text box table Automatically adjusts table position downward Slide Layouts: Different layouts serve different purposes: Title Content (default): title content placeholders Caption goes title area Table content area common data slides Blank: predefined areas Maximum flexibility Use absolute positioning (left, top) Good custom layouts Title-: Title area Large space table Good data-heavy slides Custom Templates: Use organizational conference templates: find layout master names template: Multiple Slides: Creating presentations multiple tables: Customization: Access flextable object advanced formatting: Editability PowerPoint:
code: table2pptx(table, "branded.pptx",          template = "company_template.pptx",          layout = "Content Layout",  # Name from template          master = "Company Theme")   # Name from template pres <- officer::read_pptx("template.pptx") officer::layout_summary(pres) # Each call creates new presentation - combine after table2pptx(table1, "slide1.pptx", caption = "Results Part 1") table2pptx(table2, "slide2.pptx", caption = "Results Part 2")  # Then manually combine in PowerPoint, or: # Use officer to create multi-slide presentation pres <- officer::read_pptx()  # Add first table ft1 <- table2pptx(table1, "temp1.pptx", return_ft = TRUE) pres <- officer::add_slide(pres) pres <- officer::ph_with(pres, ft1,                          location = officer::ph_location(left = 0.5, top = 1.5))  # Add second table ft2 <- table2pptx(table2, "temp2.pptx", return_ft = TRUE) pres <- officer::add_slide(pres) pres <- officer::ph_with(pres, ft2,                          location = officer::ph_location(left = 0.5, top = 1.5))  print(pres, target = "combined.pptx") ft <- table2pptx(table, "base.pptx", return_ft = TRUE)  # Customize ft <- flextable::color(ft, j = "\emph{p}-value", color = "red") ft <- flextable::bg(ft, i = 1, bg = "yellow") ft <- flextable::bold(ft, i = ~ estimate > 0, j = "estimate")  # Save to new slide pres <- officer::read_pptx() pres <- officer::add_slide(pres) pres <- officer::ph_with(pres, ft,                          location = officer::ph_location(left = 0.5, top = 1.5)) print(pres, target = "custom.pptx")

title: Export Table to Microsoft PowerPoint Format (PPTX) — table2pptx
code: if (FALSE) { options(width = 180) # Load data data(clintrial) data(clintrial_labels)  # Create regression table results <- fit(    data = clintrial,    outcome = "os_status",    predictors = c("age", "sex", "treatment"),    labels = clintrial_labels )  # Example 1: Basic PowerPoint slide table2pptx(results, "results.pptx")  # Example 2: With title table2pptx(results, "titled.pptx",         caption = "Multivariable Regression Results")  # Example 3: Larger font for visibility table2pptx(results, "large_font.pptx",         font_size = 12,         caption = "Main Findings")  # Example 4: Condensed for slide space table2pptx(results, "condensed.pptx",         condense_table = TRUE,         caption = "Key Results")  # Example 5: Dark header for emphasis table2pptx(results, "dark.pptx",         dark_header = TRUE,         caption = "Risk Factors")  # Example 6: With zebra stripes table2pptx(results, "striped.pptx",         zebra_stripes = TRUE)  # Example 7: Blank layout with custom positioning table2pptx(results, "blank.pptx",         layout = "Blank",         left = 1,         top = 1.5,         width = 8)  # Example 8: Using company template table2pptx(results, "branded.pptx",         template = "company_template.pptx",         layout = "Data Slide",         caption = "Q4 Results")  # Example 9: Get flextable for customization ft <- table2pptx(results, "base.pptx", return_ft = TRUE)  # Customize colors ft <- flextable::color(ft, j = "p-value", color = "darkred") ft <- flextable::bold(ft, i = ~ p_value < 0.01)  # Example 10: Presentation-optimized table table2pptx(results, "presentation.pptx",         caption = "Main Analysis Results",         font_size = 11,         condense_table = TRUE,         zebra_stripes = TRUE,         dark_header = TRUE,         bold_significant = TRUE)  # Example 11: Descriptive statistics slide desc <- desctable(    data = clintrial,    by = "treatment",    variables = c("age", "sex", "bmi"),    labels = clintrial_labels )  table2pptx(desc, "baseline.pptx",         caption = "Baseline Characteristics",         font_size = 10)  # Example 12: Conference presentation style table2pptx(results, "conference.pptx",         caption = "Study Outcomes",         font_family = "Calibri",         font_size = 14,  # Large for big rooms         dark_header = TRUE,         condense_table = TRUE) }

what: Export Table to Rich Text Format (RTF) — table2rtf
title: Export Table to Rich Text Format (RTF) — table2rtf
text: Converts data frame, data.table, matrix Rich Text Format (.rtf) document using flextable officer packages. Creates widely compatible tables extensive formatting options. RTF files can opened edited Microsoft Word, LibreOffice, WordPad, many word processors. Particularly useful regulatory submissions, cross-platform compatibility, maximum editability required.

title: Export Table to Rich Text Format (RTF) — table2rtf
code: table2rtf(   table,   file,   caption = NULL,   font_size = 8,   font_family = "Arial",   format_headers = TRUE,   bold_significant = TRUE,   bold_variables = FALSE,   p_threshold = 0.05,   indent_groups = FALSE,   condense_table = FALSE,   condense_quantitative = FALSE,   zebra_stripes = FALSE,   dark_header = FALSE,   paper = "letter",   orientation = "portrait",   width = NULL,   align = NULL,   return_ft = FALSE,   ... )

title: Export Table to Rich Text Format (RTF) — table2rtf
text: table Data frame, data.table, matrix export. Can output desctable(), survtable(), fit(), uniscreen(), fullfit(), compfit(), multifit(), tabular data. file Character string specifying output RTF filename. Must .rtf extension. Example: "results.rtf", "Table1.rtf". caption Character string. Optional caption displayed table RTF document. Default NULL. font_size Numeric. Base font size points table content. Default 8. Typical range: 8-12 points. Headers use slightly larger size. font_family Character string. Font family name table. Must font installed system. Default "Arial". Common options: "Times New Roman", "Calibri", "Courier New". format_headers Logical. TRUE, formats column headers italicizing statistical notation ("n", "p"), converting underscores spaces, improving readability. Default TRUE. bold_significant Logical. TRUE, applies bold formatting p-values significance threshold. Makes significant results stand . Default TRUE. bold_variables Logical. TRUE, variable names displayed bold. Default FALSE. p_threshold Numeric. Threshold bold p-value formatting. used bold_significant = TRUE. Default 0.05. indent_groups Logical. TRUE, indents factor levels parent variable using horizontal spacing, creating hierarchical display. Useful categorical variables regression tables. Default FALSE. condense_table Logical. TRUE, condenses table showing essential rows (single row continuous, non-reference binary). Automatically sets indent_groups = TRUE. Significantly reduces table height. Default FALSE. condense_quantitative Logical. TRUE, condenses continuous survival variables single rows preserving categorical variable rows (including binary). applies descriptive tables desctable(). Automatically sets indent_groups = TRUE. Unlike condense_table, collapse binary categorical variables. Default FALSE. zebra_stripes Logical. TRUE, applies alternating row shading different variables (individual rows) visual grouping. Default FALSE. dark_header Logical. TRUE, creates dark background light text header row, providing strong visual contrast. Default FALSE. paper Character string specifying paper size: "letter" - US Letter (8.5" × 11") [default] "a4" - A4 (210 mm × 297 mm) "legal" - US Legal (8.5" × 14") orientation Character string specifying page orientation: "portrait" - Vertical [default] "landscape" - Horizontal (wide tables) width Numeric. Table width inches. NULL (default), automatically fits content page width. Specify control exactly. align Character vector specifying column alignment column. Options: "left", "center", "right". NULL (default), automatically determines based content (text left, numbers right). Example: c("left", "left", "center", "right", "right"). return_ft Logical. TRUE, returns flextable object directly customization. FALSE (default), returns invisibly flextable attribute. See Details usage. Default FALSE. ... Additional arguments (currently unused, reserved future extensions).

title: Export Table to Rich Text Format (RTF) — table2rtf
text: Behavior depends return_ft: return_ft = FALSE Invisibly returns list components: file - Path created file caption - Caption text (provided) flextable object accessible via attr(result, "flextable") return_ft = TRUE Directly returns flextable object       immediate customization cases, creates .rtf file specified location.

title: Export Table to Rich Text Format (RTF) — table2rtf
text: Package Requirements: function requires: flextable - creating formatted tables officer - RTF document generation Install needed: RTF Format Advantages: RTF (Rich Text Format) universal document format several advantages: Maximum compatibility - Opens virtually word processors Cross-platform - Works Windows, Mac, Linux without conversion Fully editable - Native text format, embedded objects Lightweight - Smaller file sizes DOCX Regulatory compliance - Widely accepted submissions (FDA, EMA) Long-term accessibility - Simple text-based format Version control friendly - Text-based, works diff tools Applications can open RTF files: Microsoft Word (Windows, Mac) LibreOffice Writer Apache OpenOffice Writer WordPad (Windows built-) TextEdit (Mac built-) Google Docs (import) Pages (Mac) Many word processors Output Features: generated RTF document contains: Fully editable table (native RTF table, image) Professional typography spacing Proper page setup (size, orientation, margins) Caption (provided) separate paragraph table formatting preserved editable Compatible RTF 1.5 specification Customization: programmatic customization beyond built-options, access flextable object: Method 1: Via attribute (default) Method 2: Direct return Page Layout: function automatically sets RTF document : Specified paper size orientation Standard margins (1 inch default) Table positioned document start Left-aligned table placement landscape orientation: Automatically swaps page dimensions Applies landscape property Useful wide tables many columns Table Width Management: Width behavior: width = NULL - Auto-fits content page width width = 6 - Exactly 6 inches wide Width distributed evenly across columns default Can adjust individual column widths word processor creation wide tables: Use orientation = "landscape" Use paper = "legal" extra width Reduce font_size Use condense_table = TRUE Consider breaking across multiple tables Typography: function applies professional typography: Column headers: Bold, slightly larger font Body text: Regular weight, specified font size Numbers: Right-aligned easy comparison Text: Left-aligned readability Consistent spacing: Adequate padding cells Statistical notation: Italicized appropriately
code: install.packages(c("flextable", "officer")) result <- table2rtf(table, "output.rtf") ft <- attr(result, "flextable")  # Customize flextable ft <- flextable::bold(ft, i = 1, j = 1, part = "body") ft <- flextable::color(ft, i = 2, j = 3, color = "red")  # Re-save if needed flextable::save_as_rtf(ft, path = "customized.rtf") ft <- table2rtf(table, "output.rtf", return_ft = TRUE)  # Customize immediately ft <- flextable::bg(ft, bg = "yellow", part = "header") ft <- flextable::autofit(ft)  # Save to new file flextable::save_as_rtf(ft, path = "custom.rtf")

title: Export Table to Rich Text Format (RTF) — table2rtf
code: if (FALSE) { options(width = 180) # Load data data(clintrial) data(clintrial_labels)  # Create regression table results <- fit(    data = clintrial,    outcome = "os_status",    predictors = c("age", "sex", "treatment", "stage"),    labels = clintrial_labels )  # Example 1: Basic RTF export table2rtf(results, "results.rtf")  # Example 2: With caption table2rtf(results, "captioned.rtf",        caption = "Table 1: Multivariable Logistic Regression Results")  # Example 3: Landscape orientation for wide tables table2rtf(results, "wide.rtf",        orientation = "landscape")  # Example 4: Custom font and size table2rtf(results, "custom_font.rtf",        font_family = "Times New Roman",        font_size = 11)  # Example 5: Hierarchical display table2rtf(results, "indented.rtf",        indent_groups = TRUE)  # Example 6: Condensed table table2rtf(results, "condensed.rtf",        condense_table = TRUE)  # Example 7: With zebra stripes table2rtf(results, "striped.rtf",        zebra_stripes = TRUE)  # Example 8: Dark header style table2rtf(results, "dark.rtf",        dark_header = TRUE)  # Example 9: A4 paper for international submissions table2rtf(results, "a4.rtf",        paper = "a4")  # Example 10: Get flextable for customization result <- table2rtf(results, "base.rtf") ft <- attr(result, "flextable")  # Customize the flextable ft <- flextable::bold(ft, i = 1, part = "body") ft <- flextable::color(ft, j = "p-value", color = "blue")  # Re-save flextable::save_as_rtf(ft, path = "customized.rtf")  # Example 11: Direct flextable return ft <- table2rtf(results, "direct.rtf", return_ft = TRUE) ft <- flextable::bg(ft, bg = "yellow", part = "header")  # Example 12: Regulatory submission table table2rtf(results, "submission.rtf",        caption = "Table 2: Adjusted Odds Ratios for Mortality",        font_family = "Times New Roman",        font_size = 10,        indent_groups = TRUE,        zebra_stripes = FALSE,        bold_significant = TRUE)  # Example 13: Custom column alignment table2rtf(results, "aligned.rtf",        align = c("left", "left", "center", "right", "right"))  # Example 14: Disable significance bolding table2rtf(results, "no_bold.rtf",        bold_significant = FALSE)  # Example 15: Stricter significance threshold table2rtf(results, "strict.rtf",        bold_significant = TRUE,        p_threshold = 0.01)  # Example 16: Descriptive statistics for baseline characteristics desc <- desctable(    data = clintrial,    by = "treatment",    variables = c("age", "sex", "bmi", "stage"),    labels = clintrial_labels )  table2rtf(desc, "baseline.rtf",        caption = "Table 1: Baseline Patient Characteristics",        zebra_stripes = TRUE)  # Example 17: Clinical trial efficacy table table2rtf(results, "efficacy.rtf",        caption = "Table 3: Primary Efficacy Analysis - Intent to Treat Population",        font_family = "Courier New",  # Monospace for alignment        paper = "letter",        orientation = "landscape",        condense_table = TRUE) }

what: Export Table to LaTeX Format — table2tex
title: Export Table to LaTeX Format — table2tex
text: Converts data frame, data.table, matrix LaTeX source code suitable inclusion LaTeX documents. Generates publication-quality table markup extensive formatting options including booktabs styling, color schemes, hierarchical displays. Output can directly \input{} \include{} LaTeX manuscripts. Requires xtable export.

title: Export Table to LaTeX Format — table2tex
code: table2tex(   table,   file,   format_headers = TRUE,   variable_padding = FALSE,   cell_padding = "normal",   bold_significant = TRUE,   bold_variables = FALSE,   p_threshold = 0.05,   align = NULL,   indent_groups = FALSE,   condense_table = FALSE,   condense_quantitative = FALSE,   booktabs = FALSE,   zebra_stripes = FALSE,   stripe_color = "gray!20",   dark_header = FALSE,   caption = NULL,   caption_size = NULL,   label = NULL,   show_logs = FALSE,   ... )

title: Export Table to LaTeX Format — table2tex
text: table Data frame, data.table, matrix export. Can output desctable(), survtable(), fit(), uniscreen(), fullfit(), compfit(), multifit(), tabular data. file Character string specifying output .tex filename. Must .tex extension. Example: "results.tex", "table1.tex". format_headers Logical. TRUE, formats column headers converting underscores spaces, italicizing statistical notation ("n", "p"), applying title case. Default TRUE. variable_padding Logical. TRUE, adds vertical spacing around variable groups using \addlinespace improved readability. Default FALSE. cell_padding Character string numeric. Vertical padding within cells: "none" - extra padding "normal" - Standard padding [default] "relaxed" - Increased padding "loose" - Maximum padding Numeric - Custom \arraystretch value bold_significant Logical. TRUE, wraps significant p-values textbf commands bold display. Default TRUE. bold_variables Logical. TRUE, variable names displayed bold. Default FALSE. p_threshold Numeric. Threshold bold p-value formatting. used bold_significant = TRUE. Default 0.05. align Character string vector specifying column alignment: "l" - Left "c" - Center "r" - Right Paragraph column specified width (p-type) NULL, automatically determines based content. Can specify per-column vector. Default NULL. indent_groups Logical. TRUE, uses hspace indent grouped rows, creating hierarchical display. Useful factor variables regression tables. Default FALSE. condense_table Logical. TRUE, condenses table showing essential rows (single row continuous, non-reference binary). Automatically sets indent_groups = TRUE. Default FALSE. condense_quantitative Logical. TRUE, condenses continuous survival variables single rows preserving categorical variable rows (including binary). applies descriptive tables desctable(). Automatically sets indent_groups = TRUE. Unlike condense_table, collapse binary categorical variables. Default FALSE. booktabs Logical. TRUE, uses booktabs package commands (toprule, midrule, bottomrule) professional table rules. Requires booktabs package LaTeX preamble. Default FALSE. zebra_stripes Logical. TRUE, adds alternating row colors variable groups using rowcolor command. Requires xcolor package table option preamble. Default FALSE. stripe_color Character string. LaTeX color specification zebra stripes (e.g., "gray!20", "blue!10"). used zebra_stripes = TRUE. Default "gray!20". dark_header Logical. TRUE, creates white text black background header row. Requires xcolor package table option. Default FALSE. caption Character string. Table caption LaTeX caption command. Supports multi-line captions using double backslash. Default NULL. caption_size Numeric. Caption font size points. NULL (default), caption use document's default caption size (typically slightly smaller body text). Set specific value (e.g., 6, 7, 8, 9) control caption size explicitly. generates LaTeX comment can use wrapping table. Typical range: 6-10 points. label Character string. LaTeX label cross-references. Example: "tab:regression". Default NULL. show_logs Logical. TRUE, displays informational messages required LaTeX packages formatting options applied. FALSE, suppresses messages. Default FALSE. ... Additional arguments passed xtable.

title: Export Table to LaTeX Format — table2tex
text: Invisibly returns NULL. Creates .tex file specified   location containing LaTeX tabular environment.

title: Export Table to LaTeX Format — table2tex
text: Output Format: function generates standalone LaTeX tabular environment can : Included documents \input command Embedded table/figure environments Used manuscript classes (article, report, etc.) output includes: Complete tabular environment proper alignment Horizontal rules (\hline booktabs rules) Column headers optional formatting Data rows automatic escaping special characters Optional caption label commands Required LaTeX Packages: Add LaTeX document preamble: Always required: Optional (based parameters): Booktabs Style: booktabs = TRUE, table uses publication-quality rules: \toprule - Heavy rule top \midrule - Medium rule headers \bottomrule - Heavy rule bottom vertical rules (booktabs style) Better spacing around rules preferred style academic journals. Color Features: Zebra Stripes: Creates alternating background colors visual grouping: Dark Header: Creates high-contrast header row: require xcolor package table option document. Integration LaTeX Documents: Basic inclusion: resizing: Landscape orientation: Caption Formatting: Captions caption parameter written LaTeX comments output file reference. actual LaTeX captions, wrap table table environment (see examples ). Special Characters: function automatically escapes LaTeX special characters data: Ampersand, percent, dollar sign, hash, underscore Left right braces Tilde caret (using textasciitilde textasciicircum) Variable names labels include characters unless intentionally using LaTeX commands.
code: \usepackage[T1]{fontenc} \usepackage[utf8]{inputenc} \usepackage{array} \usepackage{graphicx} \usepackage{booktabs}   \usepackage[table]{xcolor} zebra_stripes = TRUE stripe_color = "gray!20"  # 20% gray stripe_color = "blue!10"  # 10% blue dark_header = TRUE  # Black background, white text \begin{table}[htbp]   \centering   \caption{Regression Results}   \label{tab:regression}   \input{results.tex} \end{table} \begin{table}[htbp]   \centering   \caption{Results}   \resizebox{\textwidth}{!}{\input{results.tex}} \end{table} \usepackage{pdflscape} \begin{landscape}   \begin{table}[htbp]     \centering     \input{wide_results.tex}   \end{table} \end{landscape}

title: Export Table to LaTeX Format — table2tex
code: if (FALSE) { # Load data and create regression table data(clintrial) data(clintrial_labels)  results <- fit(    data = clintrial,    outcome = "os_status",    predictors = c("age", "sex", "treatment", "stage"),    labels = clintrial_labels )  # Example 1: Basic LaTeX export table2tex(results, "basic.tex")  # Example 2: With booktabs for publication table2tex(results, "publication.tex",        booktabs = TRUE,        caption = "Multivariable logistic regression results",        label = "tab:regression")  # Example 3: Multi-line caption with abbreviations table2tex(results, "detailed.tex",        booktabs = TRUE,        caption = "Table 1: Risk Factors for Mortality\\\\                  aOR = adjusted odds ratio; CI = confidence interval\\\\                  Model adjusted for age, sex, treatment, and disease stage",        label = "tab:mortality")  # Example 4: Hierarchical display with indentation table2tex(results, "indented.tex",        indent_groups = TRUE,        booktabs = TRUE)  # Example 5: Condensed table (reduced height) table2tex(results, "condensed.tex",        condense_table = TRUE,        booktabs = TRUE)  # Example 6: With zebra stripes table2tex(results, "striped.tex",        zebra_stripes = TRUE,        stripe_color = "gray!15",        booktabs = TRUE) # Remember to add \usepackage[table]{xcolor} to the LaTeX document  # Example 7: Dark header style table2tex(results, "dark_header.tex",        dark_header = TRUE,        booktabs = TRUE) # Requires \usepackage[table]{xcolor}  # Example 8: Custom cell padding table2tex(results, "relaxed.tex",        cell_padding = "relaxed",        booktabs = TRUE)  # Example 9: Custom column alignment (auto-detected by default) table2tex(results, "custom_align.tex",        align = c("c", "c", "c", "c", "c", "c", "c"))  # Example 10: No header formatting (keep original names) table2tex(results, "raw_headers.tex",        format_headers = FALSE)  # Example 11: Disable significance bolding table2tex(results, "no_bold.tex",        bold_significant = FALSE,        booktabs = TRUE)  # Example 12: Stricter significance threshold table2tex(results, "strict_sig.tex",        bold_significant = TRUE,        p_threshold = 0.01,  # Bold only if p < 0.01        booktabs = TRUE)  # Example 13: With caption size control table2tex(results, "caption_size.tex",        caption_size = 6,        caption = "Table 1 - Results with Compact Caption\\\\                  Smaller caption fits better on constrained pages")  # Example 14: Complete publication-ready table table2tex(results, "final_table1.tex",        booktabs = TRUE,        caption = "Table 1: Multivariable Analysis of Mortality Risk Factors",        label = "tab:main_results",        indent_groups = TRUE,        zebra_stripes = FALSE,  # Many journals prefer no stripes        bold_significant = TRUE,        cell_padding = "normal")  # Example 15: Descriptive statistics table desc_table <- desctable(    data = clintrial,    by = "treatment",    variables = c("age", "sex", "bmi"),    labels = clintrial_labels )  table2tex(desc_table, "table1_descriptive.tex",        booktabs = TRUE,        caption = "Table 1: Baseline Characteristics",        label = "tab:baseline")  # Example 16: Model comparison table models <- list(    base = c("age", "sex"),    full = c("age", "sex", "treatment", "stage") )  comparison <- compfit(    data = clintrial,    outcome = "os_status",    model_list = models )  table2tex(comparison, "model_comparison.tex",        booktabs = TRUE,        caption = "Model Comparison Statistics",        label = "tab:models")  # Example 17: For integration in LaTeX document # After running table2tex(), use in LaTeX: # # \begin{table}[htbp] #   \centering #   \caption{Caption} #   \label{tab:label} #   \input{final_table1.tex} # \end{table}  # Example 18: With resizing in LaTeX # \begin{table}[htbp] #   \centering #   \caption{Wide Table} #   \resizebox{\textwidth}{!}{\input{wide_results.tex}} # \end{table}  # Example 19: Landscape table in LaTeX # \usepackage{pdflscape} # ... # \begin{landscape} #   \begin{table}[htbp] #     \centering #     \input{landscape_table.tex} #   \end{table} # \end{landscape} }

what: Create Forest Plot for Univariable Screening — uniforest
title: Create Forest Plot for Univariable Screening — uniforest
text: Generates publication-ready forest plot uniscreen() output object. plot displays effect estimates (, HR, RR, coefficients) confidence intervals predictor tested univariable analysis single outcome.

title: Create Forest Plot for Univariable Screening — uniforest
code: uniforest(   x,   title = "Univariable Screening",   effect_label = NULL,   digits = 2,   p_digits = 3,   conf_level = 0.95,   font_size = 1,   annot_size = 3.88,   header_size = 5.82,   title_size = 23.28,   plot_width = NULL,   plot_height = NULL,   table_width = 0.6,   show_n = TRUE,   show_events = NULL,   indent_groups = FALSE,   condense_table = FALSE,   bold_variables = FALSE,   center_padding = 4,   zebra_stripes = TRUE,   color = NULL,   null_line = NULL,   log_scale = NULL,   labels = NULL,   show_footer = TRUE,   units = "in",   number_format = NULL )

title: Create Forest Plot for Univariable Screening — uniforest
text: x Univariable screen result object (data.table class attributes uniscreen()). title Character string specifying plot title. Default "Univariable Screening". Use descriptive titles publication. effect_label Character string effect measure label forest plot axis. Default NULL, auto-detects based model type (e.g., "Odds Ratio", "Hazard Ratio", "Rate Ratio", "Coefficient"). digits Integer specifying number decimal places effect estimates confidence intervals. Default 2. p_digits Integer specifying number decimal places p-values. Values smaller 10^(-p_digits) displayed "< 0.001" (p_digits = 3), "< 0.0001" (p_digits = 4), etc. Default 3. conf_level Numeric confidence level confidence intervals. Must 0 1. Default 0.95 (95% confidence intervals). CI percentage automatically displayed column headers (e.g., "90% CI" conf_level = 0.90). Note: parameter affects display ; underlying CIs come uniscreen result. font_size Numeric multiplier controlling base font size text elements. Default 1.0. annot_size Numeric value controlling relative font size data annotations. Default 3.88. header_size Numeric value controlling relative font size column headers. Default 5.82. title_size Numeric value controlling relative font size main plot title. Default 23.28. plot_width Numeric value specifying intended output width specified units. Used optimizing layout. Default NULL (automatic). Recommended: 10-16 inches. plot_height Numeric value specifying intended output height specified units. Default NULL (automatic based number rows). table_width Numeric value 0 1 specifying proportion total plot width allocated data table. Default 0.6 (60% table, 40% forest plot). show_n Logical. TRUE, includes column showing sample sizes. Default TRUE. show_events Logical. TRUE, includes column showing number events row. Default NULL, auto-detects based model type (TRUE binomial/survival, FALSE linear). indent_groups Logical. TRUE, indents factor levels parent variable name, creating hierarchical display. FALSE (default), shows variable level separate columns. condense_table Logical. TRUE, condenses binary categorical variables single rows showing non-reference category. Automatically sets indent_groups = TRUE. Useful tables many binary variables. Default FALSE. bold_variables Logical. TRUE, variable names displayed bold. FALSE (default), variable names displayed plain text. center_padding Numeric value specifying horizontal spacing table forest plot. Default 4. zebra_stripes Logical. TRUE, applies alternating gray background shading different variables improved readability. Default TRUE. color Character string specifying color point estimates forest plot. Default NULL, auto-selects based effect type: purple ("#8A61D8") hazard ratios (Cox), teal ("#4BA6B6") odds ratios (logistic), blue ("#3F87EE") rate/risk ratios (Poisson, Gamma, etc.), green ("#5A8F5A") coefficients (linear models). Use hex codes R color names custom colors. null_line Numeric value reference line position. Default NULL, uses 1 ratio measures (, HR, RR) 0 coefficients. log_scale Logical. TRUE, uses log scale x-axis. Default NULL, auto-detects (TRUE /HR/RR, FALSE coefficients). labels Named character vector providing custom display labels variables. Applied predictor names plot. Default NULL (uses original variable names). show_footer Logical. TRUE, displays footer outcome variable name. Default TRUE. units Character string specifying units plot dimensions: "" (inches), "cm", "mm". Default "". number_format Character string two-element character vector   controlling thousand decimal separators formatted output. Named   presets: "us" - Comma thousands, period decimal: 1,234.56 [default] "eu" - Period thousands, comma decimal: 1.234,56 "space" - Thin-space thousands, period decimal: 1 234.56       (SI/ISO 31-0) "none" - thousands separator: 1234.56 provide custom two-element vector c(big.mark, decimal.mark),   e.g., c("'", ".") Swiss-style: 1'234.56. NULL (default), uses   getOption("summata.number_format", "us"). Set global option   per session avoid passing argument repeatedly:
code: options(summata.number_format = "eu")

title: Create Forest Plot for Univariable Screening — uniforest
text: ggplot object containing complete forest plot. plot   can : Displayed directly: print(plot) Saved file: ggsave("forest.pdf", plot, width = 12, height = 8) customized ggplot2 functions returned object includes attribute "rec_dims"   accessible via attr(plot, "rec_dims"), list   containing: width Numeric. Recommended plot width specified units height Numeric. Recommended plot height specified units recommendations automatically calculated based number   variables, text sizes, layout parameters, printed console   plot_width plot_height specified.

title: Create Forest Plot for Univariable Screening — uniforest
text: forest plot displays univariable (unadjusted) associations predictor outcome. useful : Visualizing results initial variable screening Identifying potential predictors multivariable modeling Presenting crude associations alongside adjusted results Quick visual assessment effect sizes significance plot automatically handles: Different effect types (, HR, RR, coefficients) appropriate     axis scaling (log vs linear) Factor variables multiple levels (grouped variable name) Continuous variables (single row per predictor) Reference categories categorical variables

title: Create Forest Plot for Univariable Screening — uniforest
code: if (FALSE) { # Load example data data(clintrial) data(clintrial_labels)  # Example 1: Basic logistic regression screening uni_results <- uniscreen(     data = clintrial,     outcome = "os_status",     predictors = c("age", "sex", "smoking", "treatment", "stage"),     labels = clintrial_labels,     parallel = FALSE )  uniforest(uni_results, title = "Univariable Associations with Mortality")    options(width = 180) # Example 2: Survival analysis library(survival) surv_results <- uniscreen(     data = clintrial,     outcome = "Surv(os_months, os_status)",     predictors = c("age", "sex", "treatment", "stage"),     model_type = "coxph",     labels = clintrial_labels,     parallel = FALSE )  uniforest(surv_results, title = "Univariable Survival Analysis")  # Example 3: Linear regression lm_results <- uniscreen(     data = clintrial,     outcome = "los_days",     predictors = c("age", "sex", "surgery", "diabetes"),     model_type = "lm",     labels = clintrial_labels,     parallel = FALSE )  uniforest(lm_results, title = "Predictors of Length of Stay")  # Example 4: Customize appearance uniforest(     uni_results,     title = "Crude Associations with Mortality",     color = "#E74C3C",     indent_groups = TRUE,     zebra_stripes = TRUE,     bold_variables = TRUE )  # Example 5: Hide footer uniforest(uni_results,            title = "Predictors of Overall Survival Status",           show_footer = FALSE)  # Example 6: Save with recommended dimensions p <- uniforest(uni_results) dims <- attr(p, "rec_dims") # ggsave("univariable_forest.pdf", p, width = dims$width, height = dims$height) }

what: Univariable Screening for Multiple Predictors — uniscreen
title: Univariable Screening for Multiple Predictors — uniscreen
text: Performs comprehensive univariable (unadjusted) regression analyses fitting separate models predictor single outcome. function designed initial variable screening, hypothesis generation, understanding crude associations multivariable modeling. Returns publication-ready formatted results optional p-value filtering.

title: Univariable Screening for Multiple Predictors — uniscreen
code: uniscreen(   data,   outcome,   predictors,   model_type = "glm",   family = "binomial",   random = NULL,   p_threshold = 0.05,   conf_level = 0.95,   reference_rows = TRUE,   show_n = TRUE,   show_events = TRUE,   digits = 2,   p_digits = 3,   labels = NULL,   keep_models = FALSE,   exponentiate = NULL,   parallel = TRUE,   n_cores = NULL,   number_format = NULL,   verbose = NULL,   ... )

title: Univariable Screening for Multiple Predictors — uniscreen
text: data Data frame data.table containing analysis dataset. function automatically converts data frames data.tables efficient processing. outcome Character string specifying outcome variable name. survival analysis, use Surv() syntax survival package (e.g., "Surv(time, status)" "Surv(os_months, os_status)"). predictors Character vector predictor variable names screen. predictor tested independently univariable model. Can include continuous, categorical (factor), binary variables. model_type Character string specifying type regression model fit. Options include: "glm" - Generalized linear model (default). Supports multiple     distributions via family parameter including logistic, Poisson,     Gamma, Gaussian, quasi-likelihood models. "lm" - Linear regression continuous outcomes normally     distributed errors. Equivalent glm family = "gaussian". "coxph" - Cox proportional hazards model time--event     survival analysis. Requires Surv() outcome syntax. "clogit" - Conditional logistic regression matched     case-control studies stratified analyses. "negbin" - Negative binomial regression overdispersed count     data (requires MASS package). Estimates additional dispersion parameter     compared Poisson regression. "glmer" - Generalized linear mixed-effects model hierarchical     clustered data non-normal outcomes (requires lme4 package     random parameter). "lmer" - Linear mixed-effects model hierarchical clustered     data continuous outcomes (requires lme4 package random     parameter). "coxme" - Cox mixed-effects model clustered survival data     (requires coxme package random parameter). family GLM GLMER models, specifies error distribution link function. Can character string, family function, family object. Ignored non-GLM/GLMER models. Binary/Binomial outcomes: "binomial" binomial() - Logistic regression binary     outcomes (0/1, TRUE/FALSE). Returns odds ratios (). Default. "quasibinomial" quasibinomial() - Logistic regression     overdispersion. Use residual deviance >> residual df. binomial(link = "probit") - Probit regression (normal CDF link). binomial(link = "cloglog") - Complementary log-log link     asymmetric binary outcomes. Count outcomes: "poisson" poisson() - Poisson regression count     data. Returns rate ratios (RR). Assumes mean = variance. "quasipoisson" quasipoisson() - Poisson regression     overdispersion. Use variance > mean. Continuous outcomes: "gaussian" gaussian() - Normal/Gaussian distribution     continuous outcomes. Equivalent linear regression. gaussian(link = "log") - Log-linear model positive continuous     outcomes. Returns multiplicative effects. gaussian(link = "inverse") - Inverse link specific applications. Positive continuous outcomes: "Gamma" Gamma() - Gamma distribution positive,     right-skewed continuous data (e.g., costs, lengths stay). Default log link. Gamma(link = "inverse") - Gamma inverse (canonical) link. Gamma(link = "identity") - Gamma identity link additive     effects positive outcomes. "inverse.gaussian" inverse.gaussian() - Inverse Gaussian     positive, highly right-skewed data. negative binomial regression (overdispersed counts), use model_type = "negbin" instead family parameter. See family additional details options. random Character string specifying random-effects formula mixed-effects models (glmer, lmer, coxme). Use standard lme4/coxme syntax, e.g., "(1|site)" random intercepts site, "(1|site/patient)" nested random effects. Required model_type mixed-effects model type unless random effects included predictors vector. Alternatively, random effects can included directly predictors vector using syntax (e.g., predictors = c("age", "sex", "(1|site)")), though iterated predictors. Default NULL. p_threshold Numeric value 0 1 specifying p-value threshold used count significant predictors printed summary. predictors always included output table. Default 0.05. conf_level Numeric confidence level confidence intervals. Must 0 1. Default 0.95 (95% confidence intervals). reference_rows Logical. TRUE, adds rows reference categories factor variables baseline values (/HR/RR = 1, coefficient = 0). Makes tables complete easier interpret. Default TRUE. show_n Logical. TRUE, includes sample size column output table. Default TRUE. show_events Logical. TRUE, includes events column output table (relevant survival logistic regression). Default TRUE. digits Integer specifying number decimal places effect estimates (, HR, RR, coefficients). Default 2. p_digits Integer specifying number decimal places p-values. Values smaller 10^(-p_digits) displayed "< 0.001" (p_digits = 3), "< 0.0001" (p_digits = 4), etc. Default 3. labels Named character vector list providing custom display labels variables. Names match predictor names, values display labels. Predictors labels use original names. Default NULL. keep_models Logical. TRUE, stores fitted model objects output attribute. allows access models diagnostics, predictions, analysis, can consume significant memory large datasets many predictors. Models accessible via attr(result, "models"). Default FALSE. exponentiate Logical. Whether exponentiate coefficients (display /HR/RR instead log odds/log hazards). Default NULL, automatically exponentiates logistic, Poisson, Cox models, displays raw coefficients linear models GLM families. Set TRUE force exponentiation FALSE force coefficients. parallel Logical. TRUE (default), fits models parallel using multiple CPU cores improved performance many predictors. Unix/Mac systems, uses fork-based parallelism via mclapply; Windows, uses socket clusters via parLapply. Set FALSE sequential processing. n_cores Integer specifying number CPU cores use parallel processing. Default NULL, automatically detects available cores uses detectCores() - 1. R CMD check, number cores automatically limited 2 per CRAN policy. Ignored parallel = FALSE. number_format Character string two-element character vector   controlling thousand decimal separators formatted output. Named   presets: "us" - Comma thousands, period decimal: 1,234.56 [default] "eu" - Period thousands, comma decimal: 1.234,56 "space" - Thin-space thousands, period decimal: 1 234.56       (SI/ISO 31-0) "none" - thousands separator: 1234.56 provide custom two-element vector c(big.mark, decimal.mark),   e.g., c("'", ".") Swiss-style: 1'234.56. NULL (default), uses   getOption("summata.number_format", "us"). Set global option   per session avoid passing argument repeatedly: verbose Logical. TRUE, displays model fitting warnings (e.g., singular fit, convergence issues). FALSE (default), routine fitting messages suppressed unexpected warnings preserved. NULL, uses getOption("summata.verbose", FALSE). ... Additional arguments passed underlying model fitting functions (glm, lm, coxph, etc.). Common options include weights, subset, na.action, model-specific control parameters.
code: options(summata.number_format = "eu")

title: Univariable Screening for Multiple Predictors — uniscreen
text: data.table S3 class "uniscreen_result" containing formatted   univariable screening results. table structure includes: Variable Character. Predictor name custom label (labels) Group Character. factor variables: category level. continuous       variables: typically empty descriptive statistic label n Integer. Sample size used model (show_n = TRUE) n_group Integer. Sample size specific factor level       (factor variables ) events Integer. Total number events model survival       logistic regression (show_events = TRUE) events_group Integer. Number events specific factor       level (factor variables ) /HR/RR/Coefficient (95% CI) Character. Formatted effect       estimate confidence interval.
